crates/forge_app/src/dto/openai.description: This subtree contains DTOs and a provider transformation pipeline that translate between the project's internal chat/tool/domain model and OpenAI-style provider payloads and responses. It centralizes request/response shapes, provider/model-specific compatibility shims, and a collection of small transformers that mutate Request DTOs for different backends; this layer is critical because it enforces interoperability with many heterogeneous LLM/proxy implementations and influences runtime behavior (tool invocation, streaming, caching, and billing extraction).
key_behaviors: Map internal domain Context/ContextMessage/ToolCallFull into provider-compatible Request payloads, including serializing tool-call arguments and annotating caching/streaming flags.

Apply a configurable sequence of Transformers (ProviderPipeline) to mutate Request DTOs per-provider and per-model to satisfy compatibility quirks (field renames, schema shapes, param formats, and removal of unsupported features).
Parse OpenAI-like provider responses (streamed or non-streamed) into internal ChatCompletionMessage/Usage and reconstruct tool-call results, including normalizing cost/usage metadata from proxies.
Strip or adapt tool invocation semantics when target providers do not support tools (DropToolCalls) and enforce schema normalization (NormalizeToolSchema/EnforceStrictToolSchema).
Provide small provider-specific transformations (Kimi K2, GitHub Copilot, Minimax) that change request fields or flatten reasoning details for backend compatibility.

insights.title: Double-encoded tool-call arguments
insights.problem: Tool call arguments may already be JSON-encoded strings; naively re-serializing them produces a JSON string-of-a-string that breaks provider parsing.
insights.root_cause: The system accepts both raw structures and pre-encoded JSON strings as tool arguments, and serialization code did not detect and avoid double-encoding.
insights.solution: Detect and preserve pre-encoded JSON strings when serializing tool arguments (or normalize to a single consistent representation) and ensure Request DTOs send correctly typed JSON values expected by providers.

insights.title: Provider-specific field expectations (flat vs structured reasoning)
insights.problem: Some backends expect reasoning content as a flat field while others expect structured reasoning_details arrays or other enums; mismatches cause runtime rejections.
insights.root_cause: Different vendors (e.g., Kimi K2, GitHub Copilot, OpenCode Zen) each evolve slightly different request/response schemas from the OpenAI baseline.
insights.solution: Keep explicit transformers that map internal reasoning structures to each provider’s expected shape (flat string alias, reasoning_effort string, or array), and ensure those transformers run when the ProviderPipeline detects the backend/model.

insights.title: Inconsistent cost/usage payloads from proxies
insights.problem: Some proxies emit cost as strings, as separate cost-only events, or in non-standard fields; code that expects numeric cost fields may fail or ignore billing info.
insights.root_cause: Upstream proxies adapt/compact telemetry and may not follow OpenAI's usage schema precisely.
insights.solution: Parse cost/usage flexibly (accept strings, cost-only events, vendor-specific fields), normalize into internal Usage/Cost types, and ensure streaming handlers tolerate events that carry only billing info.

insights.title: Tools and models that lack tool-invocation support
insights.problem: Sending tool metadata or tool_call objects to providers that do not support them can lead to errors or ignored semantics.
insights.root_cause: A single Request shape is used for many models/providers with different feature sets.
insights.solution: Use DropToolCalls or other transformers to convert tool messages into plain user messages and remove tool metadata when the pipeline determines provider/tooling is unsupported.

insights.title: Provider-specific transformers must run in correct sequence and be enabled per-backend
insights.problem: Missing or mis-ordered transformers (e.g., caching for minimax models proxied through OpenRouter) can result in inefficiencies, incorrect payloads, or broken compatibility.
insights.root_cause: Many small, conditional transformers are composed; omissions or ordering changes have cross-cutting runtime effects.
insights.solution: Treat ProviderPipeline composition as a behavioral contract: order matters (schema normalization, then field mapping, then final shims) and ensure provider detection predicates enable the intended set of transformers.

key_files.why: Primary mapping from internal Context/ToolCallFull into provider Request DTOs and contains critical serialization logic for tool arguments and caching flags.
Parses provider responses into internal ChatCompletionMessage and Usage types; central to reconstructing tool calls and extracting cost/usage across proxies and streaming events.
Composes and applies provider/model-specific transformers; the place where compatibility rules are enforced and where transformer ordering matters.
Implements the logic to remove tool semantics for providers without tool support — a behavior that changes request semantics significantly.
Normalizes tool schemas to OpenAI-compatible shapes and enforces strict response formats; affects how tools are advertised to providers and how responses are validated.

construct_relationships: Request::from(Context) composes Message and ContentPart DTOs and uses serialize_tool_call_arguments when converting ToolCallFull into ToolCall to create provider payloads.

ProviderPipeline applies a sequence of Transformer implementations (DropToolCalls, NormalizeToolSchema, SetCache, KimiK2Reasoning, SetReasoningEffort, TrimToolCallIds, SetToolChoice, etc.) to mutate Request DTOs based on provider/model predicates (is_zai_provider, is_gemini3_model, supports_open_router_params).

Response DTOs (Response, Choice, ToolCall) are TryFrom-converted into internal ChatCompletionMessage/Usage; response parsing must mirror request tool schema normalization and recognize proxy-specific fields (cost-as-string, GitHub Copilot fields).

Transformer modules both read and mutate fields added by request.rs (messages, tools, assistant.tool_calls) — e.g., DropToolCalls converts tool messages to user messages and clears the tools field; TrimToolCallIds shortens tool_call IDs created by request serialization.
notable_relationships: These DTOs are the contract between forge_app and external provider clients (crates/forge_services); any shape or semantic change impacts network calls and provider integrations.

Domain types (forge_domain::Context, ToolCallFull, Model) are converted here; changes to domain types or to these conversions require coordination with domain crate consumers (CLI, agents, persistence).

Tests in this crate (transformer unit tests like test_mistral_transformer_tools_not_supported and when_model tests) validate behavior; regression here will surface in workspace-level tests and CI which enforces -Dwarnings.
ProviderPipeline decisions depend on provider/model identification logic that must remain consistent with configuration and provider detection elsewhere (forge_config and service client selection).

Streaming semantics and cost extraction interact with higher-level consumers that render streams (forge_stream/forge_markdown_stream) and telemetry (forge_tracker) — response parsing changes can affect those systems.
recommendations: Preserve and explicitly handle double-encoded vs raw JSON tool argument representations when modifying serialization code; validate with unit tests covering both cases.
When altering Request/Response shapes or transformers, maintain or update provider-specific shims (Kimi K2, GitHub Copilot, OpenCode Zen, Minimax) and ensure ProviderPipeline ordering is preserved.

Run the crate/unit test suite and workspace tests (cargo test --workspace), include insta tests where present (cargo insta test --accept when updating snapshots), and ensure builds pass with CI-style RUSTFLAGS (treat warnings as errors).

Verify downstream consumers (forge_services provider clients, streaming renderers, telemetry) for compatibility when changing DTO fields or semantics; coordinate any public DTO changes across crates that consume these types.
Be careful with streaming vs non-streaming response handling and cost extraction: changes must tolerate proxies that emit cost-only events or use string-typed cost fields.

Avoid breaking changes to serialized shapes sent to external providers — any change to request serialization must be validated against the actual provider endpoints or via mock/proxy tests to prevent runtime failures.

crates/forge_app/src/dto/anthropic.description: This folder contains DTOs and transformation pipeline logic that adapt internal forge_domain conversation/context and tool-call representations to the JSON request/response shapes expected by Anthropic (including Vertex Anthropic variants), and vice versa for streaming responses. It centralizes serialization, deserialization, provider-specific quirks (IDs, caching markers, output_format), and a set of request transforms that must be run before sending payloads to Anthropic. These artifacts are critical because they enforce protocol compatibility and tolerate provider streaming idiosyncrasies while preserving domain semantics.
key_behaviors: Convert forge_domain::Context and domain message/tool-call structures into Anthropic request JSON via TryFrom<Request> implementations.

Apply a sequence of provider-specific transforms to Request objects (sanitize tool IDs, drop/normalize invalid tool uses, enforce strict object schemas, remove output_format for Vertex, prepend auth system message, set cache markers).
Serialize tool calls, images, and reasoning/output configuration into Anthropic-compatible content shapes while preserving cache-control markers and tool-use intent.

Parse Anthropic streaming events into forge_domain types (ChatCompletionMessage, Usage, Model), mapping usage, stop reasons, and embedded tool-reasoning information, while tolerating different event encodings.
Gracefully handle provider errors and unknown/variant stream payloads (e.g., Ping cost as string or number) and map them into domain Error/Usage structures.

insights.title: Tool/Call ID sanitization required
insights.problem: Tool call IDs containing characters outside ^[a-zA-Z0-9_-]+ caused 400 Bad Request errors from Anthropic/Vertex Anthropic.
insights.root_cause: Anthropic enforces a restricted token format for tool identifiers; domain IDs may include characters disallowed by the provider.
insights.solution: Sanitize tool/call IDs to the provider-allowed character set during request preparation (this folder contains a sanitizer transform used in the pipeline). Risk: renaming must remain deterministic and traceable between request and response mapping.

insights.title: Ping/cost field variability in streaming events
insights.problem: Anthropic streaming Ping events sometimes encode cost as a numeric value and sometimes as a string.
insights.root_cause: Inconsistent provider-side encoding in streamed events or differences between Anthropic flavors.
insights.solution: Parse ping/cost tolerant of numeric-or-string encodings and normalize into domain Usage/cost types. Maintain tests that validate both encodings to avoid regressions.

insights.title: Strict object schema needed for tool inputs
insights.problem: Loose or invalid object shapes in tool-use payloads can be rejected or misinterpreted by the provider.
insights.root_cause: Providers expect well-formed JSON object schemas for structured tool arguments; domain structures may be more permissive.
insights.solution: Run an enforce-strict-object-schema transform as part of the request pipeline to normalize and validate object shapes before serialization. Modifications must preserve mapping between domain semantics and the strict schema applied.

insights.title: Conditional auth system message injection
insights.problem: OAuth-enabled requests require an additional system message to be injected to satisfy provider expectations.
insights.root_cause: Provider/Vertex Anthropic variants (or configured flows) expect authorization context messages to be present in the request messages.
insights.solution: Prepend an AuthSystemMessage when enabled via the transform. Ensure this behavior is toggled deterministically and that other transforms later in the pipeline do not remove the message.

insights.title: SetCache transform manipulates message positions to mark cache boundaries
insights.problem: Message-order based cache markers (first/last/second-to-last) are used and the SetCache transform flips the second-to-last message in some cases.
insights.root_cause: Provider cache-control behavior is encoded via message-position markers; request construction must maintain the expected ordering to signal caching correctly.
insights.solution: Respect the SetCache transform semantics when altering message sequences; any change to message ordering or trimming must preserve or explicitly update cache markers so provider-side caching remains correct.

key_files.why: Primary adapter converting forge_domain::Context and tool calls into Anthropic request JSON; contains TryFrom implementations and cache-control encoding logic.
Parses Anthropic streaming events into domain messages, maps usage and stop reasons, and contains logic that must remain tolerant of provider event encoding variations.
Implements ID sanitization required to avoid provider 400s; changes here affect request validity and ID mapping fidelity.
Encapsulates message reordering and cache marker logic; important for provider-side caching semantics tied to message positions.
Exports the transform pipeline components and determines which transforms are available/applied; editing affects the transform ordering and pipeline composition.

construct_relationships: Request.TryFrom<forge_domain::Context> constructs Message and Content instances used to form the final Anthropic request body.

Transform pipeline (exports in transforms/mod.rs) operates on Request objects, sequentially applying SanitizeToolIds, DropInvalidToolUse, EnforceStrictObjectSchema, RemoveOutputFormat, AuthSystemMessage, SetCache and other transforms before serialization.
SanitizeToolIds transform alters tool/call identifiers produced by Request.TryFrom to meet provider regex constraints; those sanitized IDs must remain mappable to domain tool identities.

response::EventData TryFrom implementations map streamed ContentBlock data and usage fields back into forge_domain ChatCompletionMessage and Usage types, including extraction of reasoning and tool-use details embedded in streamed content.

SetCache transform reads/writes message positions and markers that Request serialization uses to signal provider caching; response parsing does not alter these markers but must not assume different ordering.
notable_relationships: Upstream dependency on forge_domain types (Context, ChatCompletionMessage, Model, Usage) — changes here must preserve shape expectations used across the application.

This DTO layer is the boundary between internal domain semantics and the external Anthropic API (including Vertex Anthropic variants); provider-specific quirks handled here affect network requests and responses globally.

Transform behaviors (sanitization, schema enforcement, auth message injection, output_format removal) reflect differences between Anthropic product variants and must remain coordinated with service/client code that dispatches requests.
Response parsing must remain tolerant to provider stream encoding differences (e.g., numeric vs string ping costs) because upstream consumers rely on normalized Usage and stop-reason mappings.
recommendations: When editing Request.TryFrom or any transform, preserve exact mapping semantics so domain-to-provider ID and tool-call correspondences remain deterministic and reversible where expected.

Maintain and run unit tests that cover: streaming event deserialization (including numeric-or-string ping cost), sanitized tool IDs, strict object schema enforcement, and SetCache message-order behavior to avoid regressions.

Do not change transform ordering or side-effects without verifying end-to-end request shapes against Anthropic/Vertex test endpoints or existing fixtures; ordering affects sanitization, schema enforcement, and cache markers.
When modifying response parsing, ensure backward-compatible handling of unknown or variant Event types and normalize usage/cost fields to the domain Usage type.
Respect cache-control markers and message-position semantics applied by SetCache; any changes to message sequence construction or trimming must update SetCache logic and corresponding tests.

crates/forge_repo/src/provider.description: This folder implements the provider integration layer used by the repo crate to communicate with external LLM endpoints (OpenAI-compatible, Anthropic, Google, Bedrock, OpenAI Responses/Codex, OpenCode Zen, etc.). It contains HTTP clients, repository adapters, request/response conversion helpers, SSE parsing/streaming plumbing, provider configuration/credential handling, provider-specific transformations (sanitization, cache insertion, codex quirks), and test utilities — all of which are critical because downstream chat routing, model discovery, streaming UX, retry semantics, and provider compatibility depend on it.
key_behaviors: Route chat and model-list calls to the appropriate provider backend (ProviderRouter / ForgeChatRepository) based on provider configuration and model IDs.
Build and sign provider-specific HTTP requests (headers, API key vs bearer, custom headers) and serialize domain ChatContext into provider request formats (OpenAI Responses, ConverseStream, etc.).
Stream SSE/event-source responses, parse provider events into domain ChatCompletionMessage items, and map SSE semantics (OPEN, DONE, deltas) into message phases and reasoning payloads.

Apply provider-specific transformations to outbound requests (tool ID sanitization for Bedrock/Anthropic, cache-point insertion for Bedrock, Codex parameter adjustments) and preserve metadata required for reasoning replay.
Classify transport and provider errors into retryable vs terminal errors and expose this to upstream retry logic.
Fetch provider model lists, cache them on disk with background refresh tasks, and cancel background refreshes when repository objects are dropped.

insights.title: Some backends require relaxed/raw SSE parsing
insights.problem: Strict content-type validation or strict SSE parsing rejected valid streams from proxies/backends that use non-standard headers or content types.
insights.root_cause: Standard eventsource handling enforced strict content-type checks and assumptions about SSE event formats; some providers/proxies deviate from those expectations.
insights.solution: Allow raw SSE parsing mode and tolerate non-standard content types for endpoints known to need it; ensure event parsing maps OPEN/DONE/delta semantics into domain events and surfaces parse errors with enriched context.

insights.title: Tool call/result IDs must be sanitized for certain backends
insights.problem: Tool IDs containing characters like '.' or ':' are rejected by Bedrock and some Anthropic integrations.
insights.root_cause: Backends enforce ID patterns (e.g., ^[a-zA-Z0-9_-]+) while domain/tool IDs may include richer characters (namespaced or dotted IDs).
insights.solution: Apply a deterministic SanitizeToolIds transformer on outbound content blocks and ensure the transformer is applied consistently in all pipelines that target Bedrock/Anthropic-compatible endpoints.

insights.title: Responses vs Chat API routing must match provider capability
insights.problem: OpenAI Responses-compatible providers and Codex-style providers were sometimes routed incorrectly, causing wrong endpoints or missing conversation headers.
insights.root_cause: Routing logic did not account for 'responses-compatible' variants and sometimes rewrote provider endpoints to base/v1/responses incorrectly; conversation continuity headers were omitted for Codex-style providers.
insights.solution: Route providers to the repository implementation that matches their declared compatibility; preserve full configured endpoint URLs and include conversation headers for providers that require conversation continuity.

insights.title: Transient SSE and transport errors must be classified as retryable
insights.problem: Some SSE payloads (e.g., Anthropic 'overloaded' events) and reqwest-level transport errors were not being treated as retryable, causing immediate failures instead of retry attempts.
insights.root_cause: into_retry heuristics focused on HTTP status codes and didn't inspect SSE payloads or some transport error kinds.
insights.solution: Enhance retry classification to treat known provider SSE overloaded signals and transport-level reqwest errors as retryable, while distinguishing non-retryable parse/UTF-8 errors.

insights.title: Provider metadata and URL paramization must be authoritative and consistent
insights.problem: Provider model IDs, URL parameter names, and provider-level options were sometimes missing, incorrect, or lacked enumerated options for UI dropdowns.
insights.root_cause: provider.json had stale/incorrect entries and ProviderConfig parsing only supported string url_param_vars, preventing preset option lists.
insights.solution: Provider loader must merge embedded and custom provider configs correctly, support preset URL param options, and ensure the provider catalog is the source-of-truth for model metadata used by routing and UI.

insights.title: Backend-specific transformers can overwrite user intent
insights.problem: Codex transformer forced a low text verbosity that overrode user-specified verbosity settings; some transformations also stripped reasoning or phase labels.
insights.root_cause: Legacy transformer implementations applied aggressive defaulting/overwrites rather than minimally adjusting backend-required fields.
insights.solution: Transformers should only apply backend-required changes and preserve explicit user-requested configuration (verbosity, message phases, encrypted reasoning flags) when possible.

insights.title: Some utilities duplicated across crates
insights.problem: sanitize_headers logic was duplicated in provider utils while a centralized implementation belonged in forge_infra.
insights.root_cause: Historical drift created parallel helper implementations rather than reusing a single infra-level utility.
insights.solution: Prefer the centralized sanitize_headers implementation in forge_infra and avoid divergent copies in provider utils to prevent inconsistent behavior.

key_files.why: Top-level chat repository and router: selects provider backend, manages model caching and background refresh lifecycle, and is the primary entrypoint used by higher layers to dispatch chat/model calls.
Provider registry, config merging, credential migration, and provider template creation: authoritative source for provider config and credentials used across provider clients.
Main OpenAI-compatible client: builds requests, composes headers (including custom/provider-level headers), streams SSE responses, and enriches errors — used for many OpenAI-compatible backends.
Anthropic-specific client and ChatRepository: handles Anthropic-style request signing, raw SSE parsing support, and integration points for tool ID sanitization and model discovery.
Centralizes error-inspection and retry heuristics that all provider repositories rely on to decide retryable vs terminal failures.

construct_relationships: ForgeChatRepository (chat.rs) uses ProviderRouter to dispatch chat() and models() calls to concrete provider repositories (OpenAIResponseRepository, AnthropicResponseRepository, GoogleResponseRepository, Bedrock, OpenAIResponses provider implementations).
ProviderRepo (provider_repo.rs) produces ProviderConfig and credential templates consumed by ProviderRouter and by individual provider clients when creating HTTP clients.

openai_responses/request.rs converts domain ChatContext into provider-specific CreateResponse payloads which are used by OpenAIResponsesResponseRepository (openai_responses/repository.rs) to send requests; codex_transformer can mutate these payloads before dispatch.

event.rs transforms a reqwest_eventsource EventSource into a stream of ChatCompletionMessage; provider clients (openai.rs, anthropic.rs, google.rs, openai_responses/*) feed SSE streams into this parser for domain conversion.

SanitizeToolIds transformer (bedrock_sanitize_ids.rs) and Bedrock cache-point inserter (bedrock_cache.rs) are applied in the request pipeline for Bedrock/Anthropic-bound requests to satisfy backend constraints.
retry::into_retry is invoked by provider repositories to map infra-level errors (reqwest, SSE parse errors, provider payloads) into domain retryable errors handled by higher-level retry logic.
notable_relationships: Provider clients perform network I/O and depend on external HTTP infra and credential material: changes here affect runtime behavior with real provider endpoints and must consider credential migration performed by provider_repo.

Model caching writes to disk and runs background refresh tasks; the lifecycle (spawn/cancel on drop) influences memory/time behavior in higher-level services that expect model lists to be available or refreshed asynchronously.
Test utilities (mock_server.rs) are relied on by provider unit tests and snapshot tests; normalize_ports is used to stabilize test output for CI snapshots.
Some utilities are shared across crates (e.g., sanitize_headers move to forge_infra): edits here can require cross-crate coordination to avoid duplicate implementations.

Provider metadata (provider.json) is authoritative for model lists and routing decisions; updates here can change routing (e.g., introducing openai_responses_compatible) and must align with repository routing logic.
recommendations: When changing request/response conversion or SSE parsing behavior, run provider unit tests and SSE snapshot tests (provider tests that use MockServer) to catch regressions and verify normalize_ports-based snapshots still match.

Respect existing sanitization and transformer invariants: tool ID sanitization (Bedrock/Anthropic), Bedrock cache-point insertion, and codex-specific transformations are part of the outbound pipeline; ensure changes maintain compatibility for backends with strict constraints.

Any edits to retry heuristics must be validated against real and mocked transport errors; ensure Anthropic SSE 'overloaded' cases and reqwest transport errors remain treated as retryable where appropriate.

If modifying provider configuration parsing/merging (provider_repo or provider.json), consider credential migration logic and that provider_url/url_param_vars behavior affects UI dropdowns and routing decisions; run provider-related integration tests.

Avoid duplicating infra helpers (e.g., header sanitization): prefer the centralized implementation in forge_infra and adjust imports/usages accordingly so behavior remains consistent across provider clients.
When changing background model caching or BgRefresh lifecycle, verify that cancellation on drop is preserved and that callers relying on cached models handle transient empty/refresh states.
Because CI treats warnings as errors, run cargo check/clippy/tests with workspace RUSTFLAGS to ensure no new warnings are introduced and provider tests pass under CI conditions.

crates/forge_services/src/tool_services.description: This folder implements the concrete tool services that operate on the workspace and environment (filesystem reads/writes/patches/search, shell execution, HTTP fetch, image reads, skill loading, plan creation, and snapshot/undo coordination). These services act as the bridge between higher-level agents/skills and lower-level infra (file/environment/network), enforcing safety limits, normalization rules, snapshots/undo semantics, and content validation that the rest of the system relies on.
key_behaviors: Controlled file I/O with snapshot coordination: all mutating operations create snapshots (undo points), validate content, and return content-hash metadata.
Normalization and validation pipeline: reads and writes normalize line endings, detect/handle visual or binary MIME types, enforce size/line limits, and run syntax validators before exposing results.
Search and discovery: repository-wide searching using grep libraries with filters, context capture, binary-skipping and multiple output modes (files, content, counts).
Command execution abstraction: shell command execution is routed through infra, validated, environment-aware, and optionally ANSI-stripped before being returned to callers.
Fallback/robust matching: text patch operations include exact-match attempts and fuzzy-search fallbacks to handle inexact ranges or platform-specific newline variations.

insights.title: Mutating tools must coordinate snapshots for undo
insights.problem: Multiple mutating services (fs_write, fs_patch, fs_remove) need consistent snapshot creation and undo behavior; lacking coordination can produce inconsistent undo states or missing before-content.
insights.root_cause: Each service independently creates snapshots and returns metadata without a uniform contract for atomic multi-edit operations.
insights.solution: Treat snapshot creation and before-content metadata as first-class, consistent responsibilities of mutating services and assume callers may rely on atomic multi-edit semantics (i.e., changes to one mutating service often require corresponding snapshot semantics in others).

insights.title: CRLF/line-ending differences affect range computation, writing, and hashing
insights.problem: Patch range computation and content-hash mismatches occurred when source files used CRLF; replacements miscomputed byte positions and write hashes diverged from actual on-disk bytes.
insights.root_cause: Range/search logic assumed normalized line endings while write logic preserved or transformed endings inconsistently; hashing was sometimes computed against input buffers rather than final written bytes.
insights.solution: Normalize line endings consistently at read/patch/write boundaries and compute hashes from the final bytes as written; ensure range computations and fuzzy matching operate on the same normalized representation used for write.

insights.title: Exact-match patches fail on minor differences; batch edits are common
insights.problem: Single replace patch operations break when exact text/positions differ (e.g., whitespace/newline differences), and callers often need multiple related edits applied atomically.
insights.root_cause: The API focused on single, exact-match patches and lacked batch/atomic multi-patch semantics and robust fuzzy matching behavior.
insights.solution: Implement atomic multi-patch semantics (batch application with rollback on validation failure) and include well-defined fuzzy fallback behavior for individual replacements, taking normalized content into account.

insights.title: Reads must enforce size/line limits and represent visual/binary files safely
insights.problem: Large or visual files can blow up consumers; previously reads returned raw binary content or unbounded lines that consumers can't handle.
insights.root_cause: A single read abstraction was overloaded to return both text and binary without strong size or representation constraints.
insights.solution: Enforce explicit limits (size, lines, characters), detect MIME type reliably (magic/extension), return base64-encoded payloads for visual/binary types and truncated lines for overly long text lines. Consumers must expect these representations.

insights.title: Repository search must skip binary files and expose multiple output modes
insights.problem: Search consumers expect contextual snippets, counts, or file-only hits while ignoring binary files, but complexity arises with multiline matches and various filters.
insights.root_cause: Search bridges walker and grep-like matchers; mismatches in binary detection or context capture can change results and test expectations.
insights.solution: Keep binary-skipping logic explicit, preserve context capture via a dedicated sink, and test combinations of regex/multiline/glob/file-type filters to maintain stable semantics.

insights.title: Shell execution must be environment-aware and produce sanitized output
insights.problem: Raw shell output can include ANSI escapes or leak environment information; commands need validation.
insights.root_cause: Shell wrapper must balance returning useful output and not leaking undesirable bytes or executing invalid strings.
insights.solution: Validate command strings, forward declared environment variables explicitly, and make ANSI stripping optional and deterministic for callers that require clean output.

key_files.why: Primary mutating text-patch service: applies replacements/append/prepend/swap, coordinates snapshots, performs fuzzy fallback, and must align with write/validation semantics.
Core file write implementation that preserves/normalizes line endings, computes final content hashes, creates snapshots and runs validation hooks used by other mutating services.
Authoritative file read service: enforces size/line limits, detects MIME and visual files, truncates lines and computes content hashes relied on by consumers.
Repository search implementation (grep backend) that yields matches, contextual snippets, and handles binary-skipping and filters — used for code discovery and patch match selection.
Shell execution wrapper that validates commands, captures environment, and optionally strips ANSI; many higher-level tools rely on consistent shell behavior.

construct_relationships: ForgeFsPatch -> infra write API: computes new content and delegates final write + content-hash calculation to infrastructure/write service.
ForgeFsPatch <-> ForgeFsWrite: both create snapshots and validate content; they must agree on line-ending normalization and hash computation to avoid divergence in before/after metadata.
ForgeFsWrite -> validation shim (syn::validate): writes invoke syntax/validation hooks after mutation to ensure content correctness before completing operations.
ForgeFsRead -> consumers (image_read, fetch, search): read enforces size/MIME semantics that downstream services expect (e.g., image_read expects binary-safe reads and size checks).

FsSearchService -> file walker + grep backends: search composes directory walking, file-type filtering, and the grep matcher/sink to produce Match/SearchResult structures consumed by tools that select patches or show context.
ForgeShell -> EnvironmentInfra / ExecutionInfra: shell execution depends on infra-supplied environment and returns sanitized outputs to callers (which may feed results into other services).
FsRemove / FsUndo / FsWrite / FsPatch: mutating services share snapshot/undo contracts — FsUndo reverses snapshots created by the other mutators.
notable_relationships: These services depend on crate-wide infra traits (file/environment/network infra) defined outside this module; changes must respect those trait contracts and the domain types (e.g., forge_app::domain::Image).
Content hashes produced by write/read/patch are used across the system (metadata, caching, snapshot identification); inconsistent hash computation will break consumers relying on stable identifiers.
Search and patch behavior affects user-facing tools and agent reasoning: fuzzy-match fallbacks and context snippets will change what patches get applied — tests in this folder assert stable behavior.
Image reading and fetch perform external I/O (disk and network) and enforce size limits; these behaviors interact with privacy/security expectations and any workspace-indexing/remote services.
The mod.rs re-exports form the public API surface consumed by other crates; renames or API shape changes will require coordinated updates in callers across the workspace.
recommendations: When editing mutating services (fs_write, fs_patch, fs_remove, fs_undo), preserve snapshot creation semantics and ensure atomic behavior for multi-edit scenarios; update all mutators together if snapshot contract changes.

Any change touching line-ending handling or hashing must be synchronized between read, write, and patch implementations; compute hashes from the final bytes as written and run existing tests for CRLF/normalization edge cases.
Maintain size/line limit enforcement and MIME detection behavior in fs_read and image_read; callers expect base64-encoded visual payloads and truncated-lines semantics.

If modifying search logic, verify binary-skipping, multiline matching, context lines, glob/file-type filters and the ContextSink behavior with existing tests — search semantics are relied upon by patch matching.
Changes to shell execution must keep command validation, explicit environment forwarding, and optional ANSI-stripping behavior intact; callers depend on sanitized outputs.
Do not change public re-exports in mod.rs without auditing downstream consumers across the workspace to avoid breaking imports.

Run the crate's unit/integration test suite (and workspace tests) after edits, paying special attention to tests around CRLF handling, fuzzy patch fallback, snapshot/undo, search filters, and mime/size limits.

crates/forge_app/src/orch_spec.description: This folder contains the orchestrator integration-style test harness and related spec tests for the forge_app crate. It centralizes reusable test fixtures, a runner that simulates agent runs and UI/service interactions, and snapshot/spec tests (history, system prompt, doom-loop detection) so orchestrator behavior can be validated in a controlled environment.
key_behaviors: Provisioning a reusable TestContext fixture that seeds an agent, templates, mock tool/shell responses, and ForgeConfig for orchestrator tests.
Running orchestrator scenarios via a Runner that drives events, injects mock services (TemplateService, AttachmentService, ShellService), and collects TestOutput for assertions.
Simulating UI notifier coordination (acknowledgement of ToolCallStart and similar events) to avoid orchestrator deadlocks during tests.
Exercising persistence and conversation history flows, including verifying doom-loop detection behavior after repeated tool calls and persistence of conversation state.
Snapshot testing of system prompt content and extensions to ensure deterministic prompt generation across changes.

insights.title: Default TestContext must include new config fields
insights.problem: Tests constructing TestContext directly failed because a recently added config field (parallel_file_reads) was not being set in fixtures.
insights.root_cause: Test fixtures (Default for TestContext) were not updated when configuration schema added new fields, causing tests to run with incomplete or invalid environment configuration.
insights.solution: Ensure TestContext default fixtures include all current config fields (e.g., set parallel_file_reads) or make tests explicitly set required fields so fixtures reflect runtime expectations.

insights.title: Tests must simulate UI acknowledgements to avoid deadlock
insights.problem: Orchestrator tests could deadlock because the orchestrator now waits for notifier-based acknowledgements (e.g., ToolCallStart) that tests did not simulate.
insights.root_cause: Introduction of notifier-based coordination between orchestrator and UI means the orchestrator blocks until an acknowledgement is observed; test runner did not previously model that handshake.
insights.solution: Test runner must simulate UI notifier acknowledgements (ack ToolCallStart and similar events) or expose APIs to the test to trigger acknowledgements so orchestration flows progress.

insights.title: ShellService mock outputs must be exposed by the runner
insights.problem: Orchestrator tests that need to run shell commands failed because the runner lacked ShellService mock plumbing.
insights.root_cause: Runner previously exposed some mock services but did not expose ShellService behavior, leaving tests unable to inject command results.
insights.solution: Runner should expose or accept ShellService mock outputs and plumbing so tests can control shell command results deterministically during orchestrator runs.

insights.title: Doom-loop detection requires persisted state and repeated interactions
insights.problem: Verifying the doom-loop detector's behavior (e.g., appending reminders after repeated tool calls) is fragile unless tests ensure conversation state is persisted and the repeated-call pattern is reproduced.
insights.root_cause: Doom-loop detection is stateful across tool calls and persistence boundaries; tests that do not persist conversation state or simulate the full sequence may miss or misassert the detector's effects.
insights.solution: Integration tests should exercise repeated tool-call patterns and ensure conversation state is persisted before assertions that depend on doom-loop detection outcomes.

insights.title: System prompt snapshots must be reproducible
insights.problem: Snapshot tests for system prompts and extensions can become flaky if the test environment or fixtures inject nondeterministic data.
insights.root_cause: System prompt generation depends on seeded context and extensions; variations in fixtures, timestamps, or environment cause snapshot mismatches.
insights.solution: Ensure fixtures normalize context (templates, extensions, timestamps, config) used by system prompt generation so snapshots are deterministic and reliably comparable.

key_files.why: Defines TestContext, Default fixtures, and convenient run helpers that seed the environment and collect TestOutput used by nearly all orchestrator tests.
Implements the Runner that executes orchestrator scenarios, wires up mock services (TemplateService, AttachmentService, ShellService) and simulates notifier behavior required by orchestrator flows.
Contains integration tests asserting history persistence, simple conversation flows, and doom-loop detection behavior—consumers of TestContext and Runner behavior.
Holds snapshot/spec tests for system prompt generation and extension support; relies on deterministic fixtures and system prompt content.
Module entry that exposes the orchestration spec components (runner, setup, specs) used by test suites and other test harness code.

construct_relationships: TestContext (orch_setup.rs) constructs the seeded environment and configuration that Runner (orch_runner.rs) consumes to drive orchestrator runs.
Runner exposes/injects mock services (TemplateService, AttachmentService, ShellService) which TestContext populates with deterministic outputs used by orch_spec.rs tests.
Tests in orch_spec.rs and orch_system_spec.rs call Runner.run/run_event helpers and collect TestOutput to assert history persistence, doom-loop detection, and snapshot contents.
Notifier acknowledgements simulated by Runner are part of the coordination path between orchestrator runtime and UI; missing acknowledgements cause orchestrator to block during Runner-driven runs.
notable_relationships: Changes to the orchestrator core (notifier semantics, doom-loop detector, tool-calling protocol) will require corresponding updates in this test harness so tests simulate new coordination and state transitions.

Persistence and conversation history assertions depend on the same storage/persistence mechanisms used by forge_repo or app-level persistence; schema or storage changes upstream will affect these tests.
System prompt snapshots are sensitive to configuration and template generation in forge_config and any extension-loading logic; those upstream changes impact snapshot expectations.
File-system related config (parallel_file_reads) ties into workspace/file helpers (forge_fs/forge_walker); test fixtures must mirror realistic runtime configuration to avoid mismatches.
recommendations: When editing fixtures or runner behavior, ensure TestContext.Default includes all current configuration fields (e.g., parallel_file_reads) so tests run with valid environment state.
Respect notifier-based coordination: tests must simulate UI acknowledgements (ToolCallStart and similar) or provide hooks in the Runner to trigger them to avoid deadlocks.
If modifying mock service interfaces (TemplateService, ShellService, AttachmentService) update Runner and TestContext plumbing so tests can inject deterministic outputs.
For doom-loop and history tests, ensure conversation state is persisted at the right points in the test flow before making assertions that depend on persistence or stateful detectors.
Keep snapshot tests deterministic: normalize any variable data in fixtures (timestamps, randomized IDs, environment-specific text) before taking snapshots.
Run the orchestrator integration tests and snapshot tests (cargo test for this crate, cargo insta for snapshot acceptance) after making changes; be mindful CI treats warnings as errors (-Dwarnings).

crates/forge_app/src/fmt.description: This folder implements presentation formatting for tool invocations and outputs used by the CLI/TUI and streaming layers. It maps domain tool inputs and outputs (ToolCatalog, ToolOperation, todos, file reads/writes) into ChatResponseContent and ANSI/text payloads that the UI renders, so changes here directly affect what users see in the terminal and snapshot tests.
key_behaviors: Translate domain-level tool invocations (ToolCatalog variants) into short titles/subtitles for CLI/TUI display.
Convert ToolOperation result objects into optional ChatResponseContent payloads (diffs, todo lists, tool output) deciding which outputs are surfaced or suppressed.
Render todo lists and diffs into deterministic, ANSI-stylized checklist lines preserving insertion order and semantic statuses.
Provide a common formatting trait (FormatContent) and content representation (ChatResponseContent) consumed by UI/streaming crates.

insights.title: Write title selection may not reflect actual file state
insights.problem: The chosen display title for write operations is derived solely from an input.overwrite flag and can incorrectly show 'Overwrite' when the target file did not exist or under error conditions.
insights.root_cause: Formatting logic inspects only the request-level overwrite flag instead of including information about the target file's existence or the eventual operation outcome.
insights.solution: Ensure formatted titles for write operations are determined from operation semantics/outcome (or include existence/context) rather than only input flags; maintain consistency between what is displayed and what actually happened.

insights.title: Formatter tests and fixtures depend on specific ReadOutput/FileInfo fields
insights.problem: Unit tests and fixtures relied on flattened fields (start/end/total/content_hash) but ReadOutput was migrated to a FileInfo struct, causing tests to break until updated.
insights.root_cause: Type migration in domain layer (ReadOutput → FileInfo) was not propagated to formatter tests and fixtures.
insights.solution: When domain types change, update formatter code and tests to consume new structs (FileInfo) and keep fixtures in sync to avoid test regressions.

insights.title: Todo diffs were unstable and mishandled cancelled/removed items
insights.problem: Diff output ordering was non-deterministic and removed items were rendered without preserving their prior status, hiding insertion order and status semantics.
insights.root_cause: Diff logic keyed on stable IDs and sorted by id, which reordered items and lost prior status context; cancelled status was not handled.
insights.solution: Render todo diffs using content-keyed incremental updates, preserve insertion order, handle Cancelled status explicitly, and ensure deterministic ordering for stable snapshots.

insights.title: ANSI colorization causes snapshot flakiness
insights.problem: ANSI-colored output in tests could vary across environments leading to unstable snapshots.
insights.root_cause: Tests relied on environment-controlled ANSI behavior; color state was not forced or normalized during testing.
insights.solution: Force ANSI color state (or provide deterministic plain/color modes) in tests so snapshots are stable across CI and developer machines.

insights.title: Todo tool outputs were not initially mapped to UI tool output
insights.problem: New todo_read/todo_write ToolOperation variants were not producing ChatResponseContent outputs until formatter mapping was added.
insights.root_cause: Formatter mappings were not updated to include newly added ToolOperation variants for todo behavior.
insights.solution: Keep formatter-to-domain mappings current when adding new ToolOperation variants so outputs are surfaced as intended; update tests accordingly.

key_files.why: Entry point that maps ToolCatalog (tool invocation intents) to short, user-facing ChatResponseContent titles/subtitles; affects how invocations are labeled in CLI/TUI.
Converts ToolOperation results into ChatResponseContent payloads (diffs, tool outputs, todo lists); central to deciding which tool outputs are surfaced to users and used by streaming/UI layers.
Produces ANSI-stylized todo lists and diffs with deterministic ordering and status semantics; used by fmt_output to render todo tool outputs.
Defines the FormatContent trait and ChatResponseContent type relied upon by other formatters and consumed by UI/streaming crates; core contract for formatting adapters.
Module entry that exposes formatting utilities; coordinates submodules and is the logical import point for the rest of the application.

construct_relationships: FormatContent (content.rs) is the trait implemented by both fmt_input.rs and fmt_output.rs to produce ChatResponseContent.
fmt_input.rs consumes ToolCatalog domain variants to create title/subtitle ChatResponseContent, using environment cwd information to compute relative paths for subtitles.
fmt_output.rs maps ToolOperation domain results (writes/patches/todos/plan_create/etc.) into ChatResponseContent::ToolOutput payloads and uses todo_fmt.rs to render todo lists/diffs.
todo_fmt.rs exposes format_todos and format_todos_diff which fmt_output.rs calls to produce ANSI or plain todo payload strings.
All formatters rely on domain types (ReadOutput/FileInfo, ToolOperation, ToolCatalog, Todo item structures) from forge_domain and on test fixtures (fixture_environment) for unit tests.
notable_relationships: This formatting layer is upstream of UI/streaming crates (forge_stream, forge_markdown_stream, forge_display); changes here change what those consumers receive and render to the user.

Formatters depend on domain types defined in other crates (e.g., forge_domain types like ToolCatalog, ToolOperation, FileInfo); type changes or migrations there require coordinated updates here and in tests.
Formatting decisions (which variants are suppressed versus surfaced) affect persistence/visibility semantics of tool outputs in conversations stored by forge_repo and what appears in TUI sessions.

Snapshot and colorized tests interact with the repository's test suite conventions (cargo insta) and CI environments; unstable ANSI behavior or non-deterministic ordering will cause failing snapshots in CI.
recommendations: When editing formatters, update and run the crate's unit tests and snapshot tests (cargo test and cargo insta test --accept as appropriate) to keep fixtures in sync with domain type changes.
If domain types (ToolCatalog, ToolOperation, ReadOutput/FileInfo, Todo item structures) are modified, propagate those changes into fmt_input.rs, fmt_output.rs, and fixture data used by tests.
Preserve deterministic ordering in formatted outputs (especially todos and diffs) to avoid snapshot flakiness; consider test harness invariants related to insertion order.
Ensure ANSI/color behavior is explicit in tests (force color/no-color) so snapshots are stable across CI and developer environments.

Be careful when changing which tool variants are suppressed vs. surfaced — this will change user-visible output in the CLI/TUI and may require updates in downstream UI/streaming consumers and conversation expectations.
Avoid introducing warnings in this crate since CI and project conventions commonly treat warnings as errors; run cargo check/clippy as part of validation.

crates/forge_ci/src/jobs.description: This folder programmatically constructs GitHub Actions job definitions used by the project's CI workflows. It centralizes builders for recurring CI patterns (matrix release builds, release drafting, label synchronization, linting, and language-specific release steps) so workflow YAML can be generated from strongly-typed Rust code.
key_behaviors: Generate gh_workflow::Job objects that represent GitHub Actions jobs (steps, permissions, environment, conditions).
Create matrixed build jobs that set up toolchains (protoc, cross, rust targets) and optionally upload artifacts to GitHub Releases.
Produce release-drafting and release-publishing jobs (draft creation, release-drafter, autolabeler, Homebrew and NPM publishing) and export outputs for downstream jobs.
Build language- and ecosystem-specific job steps (npm/tsx scripts for label/bounty sync, Homebrew formula update, npm release matrix creation).
Assemble lint and formatting commands (fmt, clippy) and provide reusable command builders for CI steps.

insights.title: Jobs set explicit GitHub Action permissions and rely on least privilege
insights.problem: Several jobs require specific permissions (e.g., workflows, issues, pull requests, releases) to perform operations; incorrect or missing permissions cause runtime failures or insufficient access.
insights.root_cause: GitHub Actions enforces scoped permissions per job; actions like release-drafter, uploads, and label sync need elevated permissions that are not default.
insights.solution: When editing or adding jobs, preserve or explicitly set the required permissions declarations; ensure any permission changes are evaluated against the actions used and downstream consumers of job outputs.

insights.title: Build jobs depend on external tooling (protoc, cross toolchain, rust targets, node/npm)
insights.problem: Missing or incompatible versions of protoc, cross, rust targets, or node tooling will break CI builds and artifact production.
insights.root_cause: Release build steps prepare platform-specific toolchains and use native compilation and code generation tools which must be installed/configured in job steps.
insights.solution: Job definitions must continue to install and configure these tools (including specific versions) in the setup steps; preserve the ordering of setup before build and validate that environment variables controlling tool behavior are passed through.

insights.title: Optional release upload is conditioned on presence of release_id and job outputs are consumed downstream
insights.problem: Uploading artifacts to a GitHub Release or feeding downstream jobs relies on job outputs (release id, tag name) and conditional logic; renaming outputs or changing conditions will break consumers.
insights.root_cause: Workflows are chained via explicit job outputs and conditional steps keyed on release presence or event types (push to main, pull_request_target).
insights.solution: Retain stable output names and conditional expressions; when modifying outputs or conditions, also update all downstream job consumers to match.

insights.title: Matrix generation for multi-target builds and npm repos is non-trivial
insights.problem: Matrix keys and permutations (platforms/targets/repos) drive parallel builds; accidental changes can produce mismatched environment expectations or explode job counts.
insights.root_cause: Matrix creation code serializes Value/serde_json structures and relies on specific matrix keys for per-target setup steps.
insights.solution: Preserve matrix key names/structure and ensure serialization steps produce the expected JSON shape; validate matrix expansions locally or in CI before changing keys.

insights.title: Jobs orchestrate third-party actions (release-drafter, autolabeler) with event-specific guards
insights.problem: These actions are intended to run under particular GitHub events (e.g., pull_request_target vs push) and require the correct event context; mis-scoped execution can produce incorrect drafts or label operations.
insights.root_cause: Actions depend on runner event context and access model; using wrong trigger or event context can limit available tokens or repository state.
insights.solution: Keep event expressions and job event guards intact; respect pull_request_target semantics where required and maintain environment variables and permissions expected by those actions.

key_files.why: Core builder for matrixed release build jobs including toolchain setup, protoc installation, rust targets, and optional artifact upload to GitHub Releases.
Creates the draft release job and exposes release id/tag outputs that downstream publishing jobs rely upon.
Defines job steps to run repository TypeScript/tsx scripts for bounty label synchronization; represents npm-based job pattern including checkout and install steps.
Encapsulates label synchronization step and permissions/expressions patterns used by label-related jobs (used in label/bounty flows).
Reusable builders for fmt and clippy command lines used across CI jobs to enforce formatting and linting requirements.

construct_relationships: release_build_job::ReleaseBuilderJob -> converts into a gh_workflow::Job that performs toolchain setup (protoc, cross, rust targets) and optionally uploads artifacts when release_id output is present.

release_draft::create_draft_release_job -> produces job outputs (release id, tag name) which downstream jobs (e.g., release_build_job upload step, release_npm, release_homebrew) consume to publish artifacts.
bounty_job and label_sync_job -> both construct npm/tsx execution steps and share checkout/install/permission step patterns for repository-level TypeScript scripts.
lint::cargo_cmd/fmt_cmd/clippy_cmd -> are reused as command generators inserted into job Step definitions for CI linting stages in multiple job builders.
release_npm::create_npm_matrix -> emits a JSON/Value matrix that release_npm_job consumes to launch per-repo NPM publish steps, coupling matrix shape to job step logic.
notable_relationships: Jobs rely on external GitHub Actions (release-drafter, autolabeler) and must align job triggers (push to main vs pull_request_target) with those actions' expected contexts.

Build jobs require installation of native tools (protoc) and cross-compilation toolchains; these steps must match the environment expectations elsewhere in the workspace (build.rs and crate build scripts).
Artifacts and release lifecycle are integrated with GitHub Releases; release_id and tag outputs are critical contract points between draft creation and publishing jobs.
NPM-based scripts (bounty sync, label sync) depend on repository TypeScript tooling, package.json scripts, and Node environment presence in the runner.
CI generation code depends on gh_workflow types and serde_json shapes—altering the shape or key names affects serialized workflow YAML used by GitHub Actions.
recommendations: Preserve explicit permissions blocks and event expressions when changing jobs; verify token scopes required by actions (issues, pull_requests, releases, workflows).
Do not rename or remove job outputs (release id, tag name) without updating all downstream consumers; check every workflow that consumes outputs or uses conditional expressions.
When editing matrix generation or matrix key names, validate the JSON/Value serialization shape and the resulting expanded matrix to avoid runaway job counts or mismatches.

Ensure setup steps for external tools (protoc, cross, rust targets, node/npm) remain ordered and versioned; test changes where tool installation is involved because builds can fail silently if tooling is missing.

Run the Rust workspace CI-related tests and workflow-generation checks after changes (cargo test --workspace, cargo check --workspace) and, if possible, a dry-run or lint of generated GitHub Actions YAML to catch schema/permission regressions.

crates/forge_domain/src/transformer.description: This folder implements a set of modular Context transformers that normalize, sanitize, and adapt conversation data for downstream consumers (providers, UI, persistence). It centralizes transformations for model assignment, tool-call flattening, reasoning handling, image extraction, argument parsing, and tool ordering so the rest of the system can rely on a predictable, provider-compatible Context shape.
key_behaviors: Composable transformation pipeline: small, test-backed transformers implement a Transformer trait and are composed (pipe/when) to produce final Contexts.
Reasoning hygiene: transformers either remove reasoning details entirely or selectively strip outdated assistant reasoning based on model identity while preserving configuration when appropriate.

Tool normalization: tool-calls and tool results are flattened into standard messages (user-text or image attachments) and tool metadata is removed so providers that do not support tools can consume the Context.
Data normalization for resumed conversations: stringified tool-call arguments and inconsistent model annotations are parsed and normalized to match runtime provider expectations.
Attachment extraction: inline image payloads embedded inside tool outputs are extracted into standalone image messages with placeholder text left in the original output.

insights.title: Reasoning must be dropped when model changes to avoid invalid signatures
insights.problem: Reasoning details (including model-specific signatures) persisted from a previous model were being reused when the model changed, leading to invalid or inconsistent reasoning artifacts.
insights.root_cause: Assistant messages retained full reasoning details across model switches; reasoning signatures are model-specific and become invalid when the generating model differs.
insights.solution: When a model mismatch is detected, strip assistant reasoning details up to the contiguous tail produced by the current model, but keep the reasoning configuration so the new model can still produce reasoning.

insights.title: Transform order affects correctness for provider DTOs and UI
insights.problem: Downstream consumers expect parsed tool arguments, flattened tool messages, consistent model fields, and extracted attachments. If transforms run in the wrong order, providers can receive stringified args, leftover tool metadata, or missing images.
insights.root_cause: Transformers are modular and independent; certain transforms depend on prior normalization (e.g., TransformToolCalls expects parsed ToolCallArguments and consistent model annotations).
insights.solution: Respect a deterministic transform pipeline where normalization (parse args, set model IDs) runs before flattening tool calls and extracting images; tests/snapshots assume specific ordering.

insights.title: Resumed/persisted conversational data can be in legacy/serialized shapes
insights.problem: Persisted conversations may contain stringified tool call args or missing model annotations which break provider DTO builders and signature checks at runtime.
insights.root_cause: Storage retained pre-normalized message shapes (e.g., Unparsed argument strings and absent model fields for non-user messages).
insights.solution: On resume, run NormalizeToolCallArguments and SetModel to convert stringified args to JSON objects and to assign default model IDs for any TextMessage lacking one so provider builders receive valid DTOs.

insights.title: Inline image payloads must be surfaced as attachment messages
insights.problem: Tool outputs that embed binary/image payloads inline can be ignored by UIs/providers that expect attachments or separate image messages.
insights.root_cause: Tool result values can contain image payloads in place, and providers/consumers expect explicit message attachments or image message kinds.
insights.solution: Extract images from tool result values into separate image messages appended to the Context, and replace the inline image data with a placeholder text to preserve human-readable flow.

insights.title: There are two semantics for removing reasoning: selective normalization vs full drop
insights.problem: Different flows require either preserving reasoning config while removing message-level reasoning, or fully stripping all reasoning data and config (e.g., privacy or debug modes).
insights.root_cause: Separate transformers exist: ReasoningNormalizer (selective) and DropReasoningDetails (complete removal).
insights.solution: Be explicit about which transformer is applied in a pipeline; applying DropReasoningDetails will remove reasoning config entirely and is not equivalent to selective normalization.

key_files.why: Defines the Transformer trait, combinators (pipe/when), and composes available transformer modules — the entry point for building transformation pipelines.
Implements the nuanced logic for stripping outdated assistant reasoning while preserving new-model reasoning configuration — central to model-switch correctness.
Flattens tool-call workflows into plain Context messages and clears tool metadata, which is required for providers that do not support tool semantics.
Parses stringified tool-call arguments into JSON objects for provider DTO builders — necessary for resuming persisted conversations without errors.
Extracts inline image payloads into image messages and replaces them with placeholders; important for attachment delivery and UI rendering.

construct_relationships: Transformer (mod.rs) provides pipe/when combinators used to build ordered pipelines of small transformers (SetModel, NormalizeToolCallArguments, TransformToolCalls, ImageHandling, ReasoningNormalizer, DropReasoningDetails).
NormalizeToolCallArguments transforms tool-call argument fields from unparsed strings into parsed objects that TransformToolCalls and provider DTO builders expect.
SetModel annotates TextMessages with a default ModelId so downstream transformers and provider callers see a consistent model on user/assistant/system messages.

TransformToolCalls depends on normalized tool arguments and may produce user messages or image payloads; ImageHandling extracts inline images into image messages — these two must coordinate to avoid duplicate or lost image data.

ReasoningNormalizer and DropReasoningDetails both mutate reasoning content but with different semantics: ReasoningNormalizer selectively strips assistant reasoning on model mismatch while preserving reasoning config; DropReasoningDetails removes reasoning_details from messages and clears reasoning config entirely.
notable_relationships: Downstream provider/client layers assume the Context has no tool metadata (or have separate tool-capable code paths). The flattened Context produced here must match provider expectations to avoid invalid requests.
Persistence/resume flows (forge_repo) produce legacy or stringified shapes that transformers in this folder must normalize before provider invocation.
UI and markdown renderers rely on images being surfaced as image messages rather than embedded binary blobs; image extraction therefore affects both provider requests and display subsystems.
Reasoning artifacts interact with model identity; any change in how reasoning is retained/removed will affect signature/validation logic in provider integration code elsewhere.
recommendations: Preserve the Transformer trait's public API and combinator semantics (pipe/when); transformations are composed in tests and callers expect deterministic ordering.

When changing or adding transformers, respect ordering dependencies: parse/normalize (NormalizeToolCallArguments, SetModel) before flattening or extraction (TransformToolCalls, ImageHandling), and apply reasoning transformers (ReasoningNormalizer or DropReasoningDetails) with awareness of their mutually exclusive semantics.
Run the crate's unit tests and snapshot tests (they capture expected Context shapes) after edits; ensure cargo test for the crate passes and snapshots remain valid or are intentionally updated.
Avoid altering reasoning config retention semantics without considering downstream consumers that rely on the presence/absence of reasoning configuration or signatures.
Be mindful of persisted conversation shapes — edits here must continue to handle legacy stringified args and missing model fields to avoid runtime provider errors when resuming conversations.

crates/forge_ci/src/workflows.description: This folder programmatically builds and emits the repository's GitHub Actions workflows (YAML) used for CI, autofixing, release publishing, stale-issue management, label syncing and bounty automation. It centralizes workflow definitions as Rust code so the repository's CI and release automation are generated deterministically and can embed shared job/step definitions and permissions policies.
key_behaviors: Construct declarative Workflow objects (events, schedules, jobs, permissions, concurrency) and serialize them to .github/workflows/*.yml files.
Compose and reuse job and step definitions across different workflows (build/coverage/performance, autofix, release publish, label sync, stale close, bounty sync).
Embed sensitive configuration into workflow jobs via permissions and secret references (publish jobs for npm/homebrew, release-drafter, sync actions).
Control workflow triggers (push, pull_request_target, schedule, repository_dispatch) and concurrency to coordinate automated runs.
Expose a single module boundary (mod.rs) that aggregates and re-exports individual workflow builders for higher-level assembly and emission.

insights.title: Generated YAML must be stable and reproducible
insights.problem: Workflow files produced by these generators may differ across runs if generation is non-deterministic (ordering, timestamps, platform-dependent formatting). This can cause spurious diffs or CI failures when generated files are committed or compared in tests.
insights.root_cause: Multiple generator code paths and serialization defaults can produce variations in key ordering, whitespace, or inclusion of non-deterministic metadata.
insights.solution: When modifying generators, ensure serialization yields deterministic output (stable ordering, fixed formatting) and validate generated YAML against repository expectations (compare to checked-in files or run the existing generation step used by CI).

insights.title: Publish and sync jobs require explicit permissions and secret usage
insights.problem: Jobs that publish releases or sync external services reference GitHub secrets and require repo permissions; incorrect permission scopes or changed secret names can break publishing and may widen attack surface.
insights.root_cause: Publish workflows embed permissions, secret names, and external-action expectations directly in job definitions.
insights.solution: Respect existing permissions and secret names when editing workflows; verify referenced GitHub secrets and permission scopes are unchanged and validate publish jobs in a safe environment (dry-run or non-production branch) before changing emitted workflows.

insights.title: Consumers and CI expect specific workflow filenames and semantics
insights.problem: Other tooling or CI jobs may depend on exact workflow filenames, job IDs, or event triggers; renaming or altering triggers can break downstream automation or scheduled tasks.
insights.root_cause: Workflows are emitted to .github/workflows with stable names and job IDs that external tooling references (release/release-drafter, scheduled bounty syncs).
insights.solution: Maintain existing file paths, workflow names, job IDs and event triggers unless you confirm all downstream consumers are adapted. After changes, run the repository's CI generation/validation steps and verify scheduled/triggered behaviors.

insights.title: Referenced GitHub Actions and schemas may change over time
insights.problem: Workflows reference external actions (autofix actions, release-drafter, homebrew/npm publishing actions); API changes or action deprecations can cause runs to fail after generator updates.
insights.root_cause: Hard-coded action versions or assumptions about action inputs are embedded in generated job steps.
insights.solution: When editing steps that reference external actions, check the actions' compatibility (versions and inputs) and run workflows in a controlled environment; ensure emitted YAML aligns with the action's required inputs and permissions.

insights.title: Central re-export (mod.rs) couples consumers to exposed workflow builders
insights.problem: mod.rs aggregates many generators; adding/removing exports or changing public signatures can break callers that assemble workflows elsewhere.
insights.root_cause: Higher-level code depends on stable function names and module exports to assemble the full set of CI workflows.
insights.solution: When changing function names, signatures or exports, update mod.rs accordingly and verify any code that imports these builders still compiles; run cargo build/test across the workspace to catch breakages.

key_files.why: Primary generator for the project's main CI workflow (build/coverage/performance/release jobs); changes here fundamentally alter core CI behavior.
Generates the autofix workflow that runs formatting and lint autofixes on PRs/pushes; impacts automatic code hygiene runs and potential automated commits.
Produces release publish jobs for npm/homebrew; directly affects publishing pipelines that use secrets and external actions.
Generates scheduled and event-driven workflows for bounty/issue reconciliation; affects issue/label automation and scheduled tasks.
Aggregation and re-export surface used by upstream code that assembles or emits all workflows; changes here affect exports and availability of builders.

construct_relationships: Each generate_* function (ci, autofix, release_publish, labels, stale, bounty, release_drafter) builds a Workflow object composed of Events, Jobs, Steps and Env constructs and then serializes/emits YAML.
Jobs and step definitions are reused across generators: job-building utilities referenced by bounty and ci attach pre-defined jobs (e.g., sync jobs, release builder jobs).
mod.rs re-exports the individual workflow generator functions so higher-level code can import and assemble a complete set of workflows programmatically.
Workflows define permissions and secret references that are consumed at run-time by GitHub Actions and by external actions invoked in Steps.
notable_relationships: Emitted YAML files are intended to be written into .github/workflows in the repository root and are consumed directly by GitHub Actions; changing emitted content affects production CI and release automation.
Publish workflows depend on external GitHub secrets and repository-level permissions (npm tokens, GitHub token, Homebrew/Formula configuration) that are managed outside this crate.
The crate depends on a workflow-generation/serialization library; updates to that library or differing serialization behavior can change generated output.
Repository-level CI (CI job matrix, cross-compilation release builds, RUSTFLAGS -Dwarnings) forms an environment constraint; generators must produce workflows compatible with those CI policies.
recommendations: Treat generated YAML as an output artifact: validate that edits produce deterministic YAML and compare against expected/check-in locations (.github/workflows).
When modifying job definitions that reference secrets or permissions, verify secret names and scopes with repository settings and test publish flows non-destructively.
Run cargo check/test for the workspace after edits to catch signature/export regressions (mod.rs exports and public generator functions).
Validate generated workflows with a YAML linter or GitHub Actions schema checker and, if possible, run them in a safe or staging environment to confirm external action compatibility.
Preserve existing workflow filenames, job IDs and major event triggers unless you confirm downstream consumers and scheduled tasks have been updated accordingly.

crates/forge_domain/src/auth.description: This folder defines the typed domain model for authentication flows used across the application: API keys, OAuth authorization flows (authorization code, device code, token exchange), provider credential containers, and small safety/newtype wrappers for secret strings and URL parameters. It centralizes the wire formats (serde), helpers for constructing auth requests/responses, and token/expiry logic that other crates (providers, services, persistence, and UI) depend on.
key_behaviors: Modeling and serializing/deserializing multiple auth flows (API key, OAuth authorization-code, device-code, Google ADC, Codex device) into strongly typed request/response structures.
Constructing and pairing typed AuthContext<Request, Response> instances to drive interactive provider login flows and provider-side handlers.
Normalizing OAuth token responses (OAuthTokenResponse) and exposing token containers (OAuthTokens / AuthDetails) with expiry/refresh inspection utilities.
Encapsulating secret strings and UI parameter specs as newtypes (ApiKey, token types, URLParamSpec) with safe Display/serialization behavior (truncation/display rules).
Exposing OAuth client/config types and accessors used by auth flow constructors and consumers.

insights.title: Types are serialized/deserialized for inter-process and on-disk usage
insights.problem: Changing field names, enums or serialization attributes may break persisted credential files, provider responses, tests, and downstream consumers.
insights.root_cause: Auth types are authoritative models for both provider JSON and local persistence; many consumers rely on stable serde shapes.
insights.solution: Treat serde shapes as part of the crate's public contract — maintain tags/aliases or provide migration paths when changing shapes; update dependent crates/tests and ensure round-trip unit tests are kept passing.

insights.title: Auth secrets must not be exposed in logs or debug output
insights.problem: Secret-bearing types (API keys, tokens) are printed/displayed in multiple layers (UI helpers, Display impls) and risk accidental leakage.
insights.root_cause: Newtypes and credential structs implement Display/serde; default Debug/serialization could reveal secrets if used naively elsewhere.
insights.solution: Respect existing truncation/safe-display behavior; when editing, do not revert or broaden visibility of secrets. Ensure dependent code does not rely on Debug/Display for auditing secrets.

insights.title: Expiry calculations and refresh decisions are centralized
insights.problem: Incorrect time handling or inconsistent expiry semantics can cause premature logouts or failed refresh attempts.
insights.root_cause: AuthDetails/OAuthTokens include expiry fields and helpers to determine refresh needs; consumers depend on their behavior to decide refresh and token use.
insights.solution: Maintain clear semantics for expires_in vs expires_at, use consistent time units/timezone assumptions, and ensure helpers behave deterministically under tests.

insights.title: Auth domain types are shared by providers, services, persistence, and UI
