insights.problem: Internal changes here require coordinated updates in multiple crates (provider clients, workspace persistence, UI flows).
insights.root_cause: This crate defines the canonical auth model; it is imported by other workspace crates that implement flows and storage.
insights.solution: When changing public types or APIs, search & update all consumers (providers, forge_services, forge_repo, CLI/TUI). Run unit and workspace tests to catch API mismatches.

insights.title: Multiple OAuth flows are supported and exposed via enums/constructors
insights.problem: Implicit assumptions about which OAuth flow is in use (e.g., fields present for device-code vs authorization-code) can lead to runtime errors if variants diverge.
insights.root_cause: AuthMethod and AuthContext model multiple variants with different required fields and helper constructors.
insights.solution: Preserve variant discriminants and provide constructors/accessors that clearly indicate required fields; update unit tests validating (de)serialization and variant construction when modifying flows.

key_files.why: Central container types pairing auth requests with responses for the interactive login flows — used by provider login logic and flow orchestration.
Canonical credential types and refresh/expiry helpers consumed by provider clients, persistence, and runtime authorization checks.
Defines supported authentication method enum and oauth_config accessor; changes affect flow selection and serialization across consumers.
Models OAuth token exchange responses (access/refresh/id tokens); this maps directly to provider JSON and normalization logic.
Newtype wrappers and safe-display/truncation logic for secrets and URL parameter specs used across UI and storage — important for secret-handling invariants.

construct_relationships: AuthContext<Request, Response> (auth_context.rs) pairs request variants (ApiKeyRequest, CodeRequest, DeviceCodeRequest) with their Response variants and is the orchestration payload for login flows.
AuthMethod (auth_method.rs) encodes which flow is used and exposes oauth_config; consumers use AuthMethod to choose which AuthContext and request/response types to construct.
OAuthTokenResponse (auth_token_response.rs) is normalized and converted into OAuthTokens/AuthDetails (credentials.rs) to drive expiry/refresh logic and persistable credential containers.
Newtypes (new_types.rs) such as ApiKey and token wrappers are used by request/response structs and credential containers to ensure typed handling and safe Display across the module.
OAuthConfig/ClientId (oauth_config.rs) is referenced via AuthMethod::oauth_config and used to build authorization request params (auth_params.rs) and AuthContext request objects.
notable_relationships: Downstream crates (forge_services provider clients, forge_repo persistence, forge_main CLI/TUI) depend on these domain types for interactive login flows, storing credentials, and invoking provider APIs — changes here can propagate wide.
Serialized shapes must align with external provider JSON contracts and any stored credential files consumed by persistence layers; providers expect specific field names and token semantics.
UI and display layers rely on URLParamSpec and newtype Display semantics to present auth prompts and to avoid leaking secrets, so editing these types affects user-facing output.

Token handling intersects with scheduling/refresh logic in service crates and possibly with telemetry/analytics in tracker crates; expiry behavior affects runtime authentication decisions across the app.
recommendations: Treat serde field names/enums as stable public contracts: when editing, search for all consumers in the workspace and update them; run crate and workspace tests.
Preserve safe Display/Debug behavior for secret newtypes; avoid exposing full secrets in Debug/Display or logs.
Maintain explicit and well-tested semantics for token expiry (expires_in vs expires_at); run unit tests that validate refresh decision helpers under edge timestamps.
Run cargo test for the crate (and cargo test --workspace when cross-crate changes occur) and ensure CI constraints (warnings as errors) are met.
When modifying public types or constructors, update and run any unit tests for (de)serialization round-trips and consumers in forge_services/forge_repo to prevent runtime breakage.

crates/forge_main/src/zsh.description: This folder contains zsh integration plumbing for the Forge CLI: generation and normalization of the embedded zsh plugin and theme, safe installation/updating of a managed block in a user's .zshrc, runtime helpers to execute embedded zsh helper scripts (with streaming), and rendering utilities for the ZSH right prompt. It centralizes cross-platform concerns (CRLF normalization, Windows CreateProcess/quoting, temp-script execution) and prompt styling so the installer, rprompt rendering, and diagnostics use a consistent, reversible integration approach.
key_behaviors: Produce a single consolidated zsh plugin and theme string from embedded shell files and Clap completions, suitable for insertion into a user's .zshrc or external plugin manager.

Normalize embedded shell script content for cross-platform use (strip CR characters, avoid -c quoting pitfalls on Windows) and choose execution strategy (inline vs. temp file) based on platform constraints.
Safely insert or update a managed block inside ~/.zshrc: detect existing marker state, backup prior .zshrc, and validate marker parsing to avoid corrupting user config.
Render a styled right prompt (ZshRPrompt) using shared styling helpers, including active/inactive visual states, optional nerd-font symbols, token/cost formatting and currency conversion.
Execute embedded helper scripts with streaming output for diagnostic flows (zsh doctor / keyboard helpers), handling platform-specific execution details and streaming semantics.

insights.title: CRLF in embedded scripts breaks zsh on Windows
insights.problem: include_str! or embedded files committed with CRLF (core.autocrlf) can produce zsh parsing failures on Windows and other platforms.
insights.root_cause: Embedded script text may contain CR characters which zsh cannot parse; passing long scripts via -c on Windows/CreateProcess also mangles quoting and can fail.
insights.solution: Normalize scripts by stripping CR characters and use a temp script file for execution on Windows rather than passing a long -c string; maintain a normalize_script utility that is applied before writing/executing plugin code.

insights.title: Managed .zshrc block insertion requires careful marker handling and backups
insights.problem: Modifying users' .zshrc can corrupt configurations if markers are mis-detected or partial writes occur.
insights.root_cause: Multiple insertion/update strategies and previous changes (e.g., typeset scope changes) introduced regressions; lack of robust marker state detection or missing backups increases risk.
insights.solution: Maintain explicit MarkerState detection, create backups before writing, validate marker parsing before committing changes, and ensure idempotent insertion/update logic so repeated runs do not produce duplicate or malformed blocks.

insights.title: typeset scoping changes affect plugin loader behavior
insights.problem: Altering typeset scope (e.g., switching to global -g for lazy-loading plugin managers) caused regressions in some shell-plugin consumers and was partially reverted.
insights.root_cause: Zsh scoping semantics interact with various plugin managers and lazy-loading behavior; a change that is compatible in one environment may break others.
insights.solution: Treat typeset/variable scoping changes as high-risk cross-environment changes. Any edits affecting scoping must preserve previous behavior or be guarded by environment detection to avoid breaking plugin manager consumers.

insights.title: ZshRPrompt rendering depends on consistent escape formatting
insights.problem: RPROMPT styling requires exact ZSH prompt escape sequences and deterministic output for tests (including nerd-font symbol toggles and currency formatting).
insights.root_cause: Zsh native escapes (%F{n}, %B/%b) and wrapper helpers must be applied consistently; small formatting changes break tests and the terminal display.
insights.solution: Centralize prompt escape creation in style.rs (ZshStyled/ZshStyle) and ensure ZshRPrompt uses these helpers so tests and runtime output remain consistent. Keep tests for expected string outputs up-to-date when formatting changes.

insights.title: Differences in embedding APIs change iteration and normalization behavior
insights.problem: Switching between include_dir/rust-embed or different embedding helpers changed how files are iterated and normalized, which impacted generated plugin content.
insights.root_cause: Embed APIs differ in available iteration APIs and on-disk metadata; normalization and comment stripping must be applied uniformly across all embedded file iteration paths.
insights.solution: Apply the same normalization and comment/whitespace-stripping logic regardless of embedding mechanism; keep embedding-specific handling isolated to the plugin generation path to avoid divergent behaviors.

key_files.why: Entry module that re-exports zsh helpers and provides normalize_script used throughout plugin generation, installation, and runtime helpers.
Contains core plugin/theme generation, execution of embedded helper scripts (streaming), and the .zshrc managed-block insertion/update logic with backups and marker parsing.
Implements ZshRPrompt rendering and Display impl used by the theme and runtime prompt output; depends on style helpers for escape sequences and formatting.
Provides ZshColor and ZshStyled wrappers that produce ZSH-native escape sequences; foundational for consistent prompt styling and testable output.

construct_relationships: normalize_script (mod.rs) is applied to all embedded zsh file content before generation or execution in plugin.rs to ensure cross-platform correctness.
generate_zsh_plugin/generate_zsh_theme (plugin.rs) read embedded files, apply normalization, and produce the consolidated plugin/theme string consumed by setup_zsh_integration.
execute_zsh_script_with_streaming (plugin.rs) runs normalized scripts and streams output to the CLI; on Windows it may write a temp script file rather than invoking -c.
ZshRPrompt (rprompt.rs) uses formatting helpers from style.rs (ZshStyled/ZshStyle) to produce the final RPROMPT string; tests assert exact string output.
setup_zsh_integration (plugin.rs) uses MarkerState and ZshSetupResult to detect/backup/update ~/.zshrc; mod.rs re-exports this for upstream installer usage.
notable_relationships: Upstream: forge_main CLI installer and setup command call setup_zsh_integration to modify user shell config; changes here directly affect the installer UX.
Downstream: theme and rprompt strings generated here are consumed by the interactive TUI and by the zsh prompt at shell startup — formatting or escaping regressions affect runtime prompt rendering.

Cross-crate: plugin generation references Clap completions created by the CLI (integration with argument parsing code in other parts of forge_main), and may use forge_embed helpers for reading embedded assets.
Environment: relies on the presence/behavior of zsh and on platform-specific process execution semantics (Windows CreateProcess quoting), so local environment differences influence behavior.
Runtime safety: modifications to .zshrc and use of temp scripts introduce filesystem and permission concerns; backups and marker parsing are part of risk mitigation.
recommendations: Preserve normalize_script behavior: ensure CR characters are stripped from embedded scripts and that normalization is applied before writing or executing any plugin/theme content.

When modifying plugin installation, keep MarkerState detection, backup creation, and idempotent insertion/update semantics intact; test with realistic .zshrc files (existing managed block, absent block, malformed block).
Validate changes on all supported platforms: run unit tests and exercise Windows-specific paths (temp-script execution and quoting) as well as Unix zsh flows to catch quoting and parsing regressions.
Run the crate's unit tests that assert exact RPROMPT/escape outputs after any change to style.rs or rprompt.rs; ensure tests for formatting and symbol toggles continue to pass.
Confirm streaming execution behavior remains correct: exercise execute_zsh_script_with_streaming in diagnostic flows (zsh doctor/keyboard) and verify output ordering and partial flush semantics.

Be mindful of external dependencies and consumers: changes affect the CLI installer and runtime prompt. Run integration scenarios (setup flow, shell startup with generated plugin) and CI test suite (cargo test --workspace) to detect regressions.

Avoid altering zsh scoping semantics (typeset/variable scope) without explicitly validating compatibility with common plugin managers and lazy-loading setups; such changes have caused regressions historically.

crates/forge_domain/src/compact.description: This directory implements context compaction for conversations: configuration and trigger logic, strategies to select which messages to evict, compact representations of messages/tool-calls used as summaries, and result metrics produced by compaction. It matters because compaction controls how much conversation history is retained vs. summarized/evicted, preserves tool-call and assistant-message atomicity, and produces metrics that downstream systems (persistence, telemetry, model prompts) rely on.
key_behaviors: Decide when compaction should run based on configured thresholds (tokens, user turns, message counts, last-message policies, and configured eviction percentages).
Compute an eviction span over a conversation while preserving tool-call atomicity and assistant boundaries so that tool calls/results are not split or left inconsistent.
Produce compact summary blocks (messages, tool calls, todos) that replace evicted messages and apply content-keyed incremental updates when summarizing tool interactions.
Emit compaction metrics describing token and message reductions for consumption by persistence/telemetry layers.

insights.title: Threshold-driven compaction triggers
insights.problem: Compaction is conditional on multiple thresholds (tokens, turns, messages, last-message policies) and invalid percentage inputs could disable or misconfigure eviction windows.
insights.root_cause: Configuration values are deserialized and used directly to decide compaction timing; percentage fields require validation during deserialization.
insights.solution: Respect Compact config validation (deserialize_percentage) and the various threshold checks in Compact::should_compact; any edits must preserve deserialization validation and the multi-threshold decision logic to avoid accidental change of compaction behavior.

insights.title: Preserve atomicity when selecting eviction ranges
insights.problem: Evicting an arbitrary slice of messages can split tool-call/result pairs or assistant message boundaries, leaving conversation state inconsistent.
insights.root_cause: Messages include interdependent sequences (tool-call -> tool-result, assistant turn boundaries) that are meaningful only when kept intact.
insights.solution: Eviction_range logic and helpers (find_sequence_preserving_last_n) explicitly expand/adjust ranges to keep these atomic units intact; any changes must retain these invariants so tool calls and assistant boundaries are never split.

insights.title: Content-keyed incremental todo updates
insights.problem: Replacing entire todo lists when the model sent incremental updates by content-key breaks continuity and can lose or duplicate changes.
insights.root_cause: Previous summarizers matched todo updates by ID and replaced whole lists rather than applying content-keyed merges.
insights.solution: When creating or merging SummaryToolCall/TodoChange entries, apply content-keyed updates (merge/incrementally apply) rather than wholesale replacement to preserve semantics of model-provided deltas.

insights.title: Compaction metrics are used downstream
insights.problem: Compaction affects token counts and retained message counts; downstream systems (persistence, telemetry, prompting) rely on accurate metrics and semantics.
insights.root_cause: CompactionResult exposes token/message reduction percentages and totals which are likely consumed by persistence/telemetry and used in decisions.
insights.solution: Maintain correctness and stability of CompactionResult calculations; any changes to how tokens/messages are counted or reported must consider consumers that interpret these metrics.

key_files.why: Defines the Compact config and the should_compact decision logic — changing behavior here alters when compaction runs and must preserve validation semantics.
Contains the eviction algorithms and the logic to compute eviction ranges while preserving tool-call atomicity — core to which messages are evicted.
Holds compact summary data structures and todo-update semantics (content-keyed updates) used to replace evicted context; changes affect summary correctness.
Represents compaction metrics (token/message reductions) consumed by downstream systems; keep calculations stable and compatible.
Public re-exports for the compaction module — edits here affect module API visibility across the workspace.

construct_relationships: Compact (compact_config.rs)::should_compact reads configured thresholds (tokens, turns, messages, eviction percentage) and determines whether to invoke strategy eviction logic.

CompactionStrategy (strategy.rs)::eviction_range analyzes a Context sequence and returns a span; it relies on helpers that detect tool-call / tool-result pairs and assistant-turn boundaries to expand ranges.
Eviction logic produces or hands selected spans to the summarizer structures (summary.rs) which create SummaryBlock / SummaryMessage / SummaryToolCall to represent replaced content.
CompactionResult (result.rs) is constructed from pre- and post-compaction state (token & message counts) and exposes reduction percentages used by persistence/telemetry consumers.
notable_relationships: Conversation persistence in crates/forge_repo likely calls into this compaction module when storing or trimming conversation histories; changes here can affect disk representations and persisted conversation sizes.
Configuration overlay in crates/forge_config or top-level app config may supply compaction settings that are deserialized into Compact — respect shared config schemas/serde behavior.

Token counting is performed elsewhere (tokenizer/embedding modules like forge_embed or provider clients); thresholds in Compact assume compatible token accounting — any change to token semantics must be reconciled across crates.
Telemetry/analytics (crates/forge_tracker) and UI/display layers may consume CompactionResult metrics — altering metrics semantics affects downstream reporting and UX.

Summaries created here may be sent to external services (workspace server / indexing in forge_embed) or used in prompts — summary shape and content-keyed todo handling affect model inputs and indexing privacy.
recommendations: When editing, preserve invariants: tool-call/result atomicity and assistant-turn boundaries must not be broken by eviction logic.
Retain serde validation behavior for percentage and numeric fields (deserialize_percentage) to avoid accidentally accepting out-of-range inputs.
Keep CompactionResult calculation semantics stable; if token counting logic is touched, verify integration with the project's tokenizer/token-counting implementation.

Run unit and integration tests touching compaction, summaries, and conversation persistence; run workspace tests (cargo test --workspace) and project linters (cargo check / cargo clippy) — CI enforces warnings-as-errors.
If changing public APIs or summary formats, update any consumers in other crates (persistence, telemetry, embedding/indexing) to avoid inconsistent serialization or runtime errors.
When modifying summary merge/apply logic, ensure content-keyed todo updates are applied incrementally (merge semantics) rather than replacing whole lists to avoid data loss or duplication.

crates/forge_domain/src/tools.description: This subtree defines the domain schema, parsing, and runtime constructs for agent-invoked tools. It is the contract layer between LLM-produced tool calls and the executor/host environment: tool wire formats, name sanitization, argument normalization, call assembly, execution context (streaming + metrics), and human-readable tool descriptions live here. Changes here directly affect tool invocation, serialization, persistence, and runtime behavior across agents and executors.
key_behaviors: Define and serialize the canonical set of callable tools and their input/output schemas (ToolCatalog and per-tool structs).
Sanitize and normalize tool names for robust lookup against LLM outputs (ToolName + lookup map).
Parse and assemble tool calls from streaming parts or XML-like encodings into full ToolCall objects, including argument normalization.
Provide execution-time context for tool calls that streams events, tracks metrics, and manages todo items in a thread-safe manner (ToolCallContext).
Track tool failures and calculate temporary disabling/retry behavior (ToolErrorTracker) to protect agents from repeating failing tool invocations.

insights.title: Separate content-keyed todo items from server-side records
insights.problem: Tool schemas previously exposed full Todo records (including server IDs) to models, causing confusion and mismatches with incremental update semantics.
insights.root_cause: Presenting server-backed identifiers and full records to LLMs conflated identity vs content; downstream incremental updates expect content-keyed items rather than full authoritative records.
insights.solution: Introduce a TodoItem type focused on content-keying and document the content-keyed behavior so callers and persistent stores do not assume server IDs are authoritative during incremental updates.

insights.title: LLM-produced tool names vary in whitespace/case
insights.problem: Tool lookups failed when LLMs returned names with differing casing or surrounding whitespace; earlier code handled only a few capitalization aliases.
insights.root_cause: Tool names are modeled as strings in LLM outputs and are subject to case/whitespace variance and aliasing.
insights.solution: Normalize tool names (trim + case-insensitive) and maintain a fast lookup map of aliases to canonical ToolCatalog entries; ensure ToolName sanitization is the single source of truth.

insights.title: Providers persist double-encoded or malformed JSON tool arguments
insights.problem: Tool call arguments arriving double-encoded or as malformed JSON were previously stored as raw unparsed strings and echoed back, causing API rejections (400 errors) or information loss.
insights.root_cause: Heterogeneous providers (and model outputs) serialize/escape JSON differently; the pipeline assumed well-formed JSON payloads.
insights.solution: Implement robust parsing that attempts to repair/double-decode when appropriate, preserves raw content as a fallback when repair fails, and normalizes arguments into the expected shape before downstream usage.

insights.title: Ambiguous todo access and metrics mutation semantics
insights.problem: ToolCallContext methods and docs were ambiguous about whether todo lists are returned, clobbered, or mutated in place; changes to the Metrics type altered caller expectations.
insights.root_cause: Concurrent mutation of shared metrics and todos plus evolving APIs without synchronized doc updates led to confusion about ownership and thread-safety guarantees.
insights.solution: Document precise thread-safety, ownership and return semantics of ToolCallContext methods; ensure callers know whether they receive copies or references and how/when to lock the shared metrics/todo state.

key_files.why: Defines the canonical ToolCatalog, per-tool input/output structs, Todo types and JSON Schema/serde attributes — the primary wire-format contract for tool calls.
Provides the runtime execution context used during tool invocation: streaming of events, shared metrics, and todo mutation semantics that executor code relies on.
Assembles streaming/XML-embedded tool calls into full ToolCall objects and houses the ToolErrorTracker that governs failure counting and temporary disabling of tools.
Normalizes and reparses tool call arguments (handles double-encoded/malformed JSON) — a critical component to avoid malformed payloads being sent to APIs.
Centralizes ToolName sanitization and helpers used by the lookup and normalization logic across the catalog and parser layers.

construct_relationships: ToolCatalog (catalog.rs) defines the enums and per-tool structs that ToolCallFull (call/tool_call.rs) uses as the payload for assembled calls.
ToolName (definition/name.rs) is the canonical sanitizer used by the catalog's fast lookup map and by parser/argument normalization logic to match LLM-provided names to ToolCatalog variants.

ToolCallArguments (call/args.rs) is produced/normalized during parsing and then embedded into ToolCallFull; malformed argument repairs must be preserved for consumers expecting either raw or structured payloads.

ToolCallContext (call/context.rs) is the execution-time holder that streams ChatResponse events for a ToolCallFull and mutates shared Metrics and todo lists; callers rely on its thread-safety guarantees.
ToolErrorTracker (call/tool_call.rs) is consulted by executor logic (via ToolCallFull lifecycle) to increment failure counts and compute temporary disable durations for problematic tools.
notable_relationships: Consumers in other crates (executors, services, TUI, persistence) depend on stable serde/JSON Schema for tool structs; changing serde tags or field names will affect networked APIs and persisted conversations (crates/forge_repo).

Provider integrations and external services sometimes return double-encoded or malformed arguments — args.rs behavior ties directly to provider client robustness in forge_services and any persisted tool call traces.
Tool name normalization impacts LLM-driven flows across the system (agents in forge_main, pickers in forge_select); mismatches here manifest as 'unknown tool' errors at runtime.
Tool call metrics/todo mutation semantics intersect with telemetry (forge_tracker) and conversation persistence; concurrent behavior must align with those downstream consumers.

Human-readable tool descriptions in descriptions/*.md are consumer-facing documentation used by prompts and UI; schema changes that alter parameter names or shapes must be consistent with these descriptions.
recommendations: MUST preserve backward-compatible serde field names and JSON Schema for tool structs unless all consumers (including persisted data and external services) are coordinated to accept breaking changes.
MUST run the workspace test suite and provider-specific tests that include double-encoded/malformed-argument cases after edits (cargo test --workspace; include insta tests where present).
MUST validate normalization logic against realistic LLM outputs (varying case/whitespace/aliases) to ensure lookup maps and ToolName sanitization remain consistent.
MUST maintain clear thread-safety and ownership semantics for ToolCallContext methods; document and unit-test concurrent todo/metrics access paths.

MUST update any dependent serialization consumers (persistence, RPCs, provider clients, UI) in lockstep when changing argument shapes, tool names, or todo representation to avoid runtime incompatibilities.

crates/forge_app/src/transformers.description: This folder contains a small suite of ContextSummary transformers used to compact and normalize assistant-produced tool-call history before persistence or embedding. The transformers implement focused, composable operations (path prefix stripping, redundant-operation trimming, role-level dedup/drop, and a composer) that together reduce context size and make summaries portable across environments. Platform-specific path behavior and the correct ordering of transforms are important because these changes affect persisted conversation semantics and downstream indexing/search.
key_behaviors: Composeable pipeline: individual transformers are composed by the compaction pipeline to produce a compacted ContextSummary.
Path normalization: StripWorkingDir removes an environment-specific working-dir prefix from file paths embedded in file-related tool calls.
Operation compaction: TrimContextSummary deduplicates consecutive assistant tool operations, preserving the most recent operation per resource.
Role-based filtering: DedupeRole collapses consecutive messages of the same role and DropRole removes all messages of a given role.
Non-file safety: Transformers generally leave non-file tool calls and content blocks untouched to avoid altering semantic content.

insights.title: Path stripping relies on std::path::Path::strip_prefix and is platform-dependent
insights.problem: StripWorkingDir behavior differs between POSIX and Windows due to std::path semantics, which can change which prefixes are stripped and how separators are interpreted.
insights.root_cause: Use of std::path::Path::strip_prefix and OS-native path handling without normalization to a canonical form.
insights.solution: Any changes must preserve existing platform-specific semantics and tests; exercise both POSIX and Windows path cases in unit tests and avoid implicit path normalization that would change behavior across platforms.

insights.title: Compaction must preserve final resource state and non-file semantics
insights.problem: TrimContextSummary prunes redundant assistant operations but mistakes in resource identification or ordering can drop required operations or reorder effects.
insights.root_cause: Deduplication operates by mapping tool calls to a resource key (Operation via to_op) and keeping the most recent entry; incorrect keying or ordering assumptions break correctness.
insights.solution: Maintain stable, well-defined resource keys and preserve the last-seen semantics; any change to operation mapping or ordering must be validated with unit tests that assert final states remain identical after compaction.

insights.title: Transform ordering affects outcome and portability
insights.problem: Applying transforms in a different order (e.g., stripping paths before/after deduplication) can produce different compacted summaries or change deduplication results.
insights.root_cause: Transforms mutate overlapping aspects of tool-call messages (paths, operations, message sequences), so pipelines are not always commutative.
insights.solution: Respect the existing composition order defined by compaction.rs; when editing or adding transformers, ensure their intended placement in the pipeline is explicit and covered by tests comparing pipeline outputs.

insights.title: Module re-exports and crate boundaries matter
insights.problem: New or modified transformers must be exposed consistently via mod.rs and maintain the expected Transformer trait/signature used by the rest of the crate and other crates.
insights.root_cause: Consumers import transformer types from this module and rely on stable names and signatures.
insights.solution: Keep mod.rs exports in sync, do not change public type names/signatures without coordinating consumers, and run workspace-level builds/tests to detect breaking API changes.

key_files.why: Implements path-prefix stripping for file-related tool calls; platform-dependent behavior and extensive unit tests make it high-risk for changes.
Performs deduplication of assistant operations and preserves final resource state; central to compaction correctness.
Composes the transformer pipeline used by the application; changes here alter transform ordering and overall behavior.
Handles role-level message deduplication that impacts message stream shape and interactions with other transformers.
Module re-exports control public visibility of transformers and must remain consistent with other crates that import these types.

construct_relationships: compaction.rs composes SummaryTransformer pipeline by instantiating and sequencing StripWorkingDir, TrimContextSummary, DedupeRole, DropRole, and possibly other transformers.
TrimContextSummary maps assistant tool-call messages into Operation keys (via to_op) to decide deduplication; it relies on the structure of tool-call messages (file, command, search identifiers).

StripWorkingDir specifically inspects and mutates path fields on FileRead/FileUpdate/FileRemove/Undo tool calls; it must not alter non-file tool-call fields that TrimContextSummary may later deduplicate.

DedupeRole and DropRole operate at the message stream level and can change the set/order of messages that TrimContextSummary and StripWorkingDir later see, so the order in the pipeline determines final output.
mod.rs re-exports the concrete transformer types so consumers use these names and construction patterns when building or invoking pipelines.
notable_relationships: Transformers operate on ContextSummary/tool-call structures persisted by conversation storage (crates/forge_repo); changes affect what gets stored and later indexed or surfaced to users.

StripWorkingDir impacts portability when workspace indexing or embedding (crates/forge_embed) consumes summary content; path normalization or stripping can change semantic content sent to external workspace servers.
Unit tests include POSIX and Windows path cases; CI runs on specific platforms and treats warnings as errors, so cross-platform behavior and compilation cleanliness are enforced upstream.
Public transformer types and signatures are consumed elsewhere in the forge_app crate and possibly other crates; changing exports may cause compile-time breakage across the workspace.
recommendations: When editing any transformer, run the local unit test suite for this crate and workspace-level tests (cargo test --workspace) and ensure POSIX and Windows path cases are covered; CI enforces cross-platform correctness and -Dwarnings.
Preserve existing public type names and Transformer trait signatures; update crates that import these types if you change public APIs and run a full workspace build to catch breakage.
Do not alter StripWorkingDir's use of std::path::Path::strip_prefix semantics without validating behavior on both Windows and POSIX paths; maintain tests that assert current behavior.

Respect transform ordering in compaction.rs: validate the pipeline output when changing composition, and include regression tests that compare pre- and post-change compacted summaries for representative scenarios.
Ensure non-file tool calls and content blocks remain unchanged by path-oriented transforms; add explicit tests if changing message traversal or mutation logic.

crates/forge_app/src/hooks.description: This folder implements lifecycle and background event hooks that run during conversation processing in the application: detection and mitigation of repeating/tool-call doom loops, background conversation title generation, post-response context compaction, and structured tracing/logging. These hooks are small, concurrency-sensitive components wired into the conversation/request/response event flow and are important because they keep conversation state bounded, prevent pathological agent behavior, and surface telemetry for debugging and cost accounting.
key_behaviors: Per-conversation lifecycle handling: handlers receive typed event payloads (Start, Request, Response, End, Toolcall events) and run corresponding logic at specific phases of processing.

Background task coordination: Title generation spawns and coordinates a single background generator per conversation using concurrency-safe sentries and oneshot channels, ensuring deduplication and safe cancellation across Start/End events.

Reactive context maintenance: After agent responses, compaction logic examines conversation context size and triggers a Compactor to replace verbose context with a summarized compact form when configured thresholds are exceeded.

Safety/defensive protections: Doom-loop detection scans conversation history at request time to detect repeated or consecutive identical tool call patterns and inserts system reminders to break loops before further tool execution.

Observability plumbing: Tracing handler logs lifecycle events, token usage, tool calls and failures without panicking, enabling telemetry and debugging while keeping handlers lightweight and non-intrusive.

insights.title: Background title generation must be single-task, cancellable, and race-safe
insights.problem: Spawning per-conversation tasks with JoinHandle required aborting tasks and could panic or create duplicated work when Start/End events raced.
insights.root_cause: Earlier designs used JoinHandle-based tasks per conversation and relied on abort semantics; concurrent Start/End events could race and lead to panics or leaking tasks.
insights.solution: Use a concurrency-safe sentry registry (DashMap keyed by conversation) and oneshot channels for task result delivery; deduplicate spawn on Start, await oneshot on End, and treat cancellation/absent results gracefully.

insights.title: Context compaction must run post-response and respect configured thresholds
insights.problem: Conversation contexts can grow unbounded and degrade performance or increase costs if not compacted.
insights.root_cause: No automatic pruning/summary is applied during the response lifecycle to bound context size.
insights.solution: Expose an event hook that inspects conversation context after responses; when configured thresholds are exceeded, call the Compactor to replace verbose context with a compact summary and update the conversation state atomically.

insights.title: Detect and remediate repeating tool-call doom loops
insights.problem: Agents can get stuck repeatedly calling the same tool without making progress (doom loops).
insights.root_cause: Absent or delayed detection of repeating/consecutive identical tool call patterns in conversation history.
insights.solution: Run a request-phase detector that scans recent history for repeating patterns and inserts a system reminder message into the conversation to break the loop before further tool execution.

insights.title: Tracing must be resilient and side-effect free
insights.problem: Tracing code that panics or throws in lifecycle handlers can destabilize request processing or mask failures.
insights.root_cause: Tracing touches diverse event payloads (start/request/response/toolcall/end) and may interact with optional telemetry inputs (token/cost data).
insights.solution: Implement TracingHandler with EventHandle for each payload type, ensure logging paths are non-panicking and lightweight, and include tests asserting no panics on typical events.

insights.title: Hook re-exports must reflect refactors
insights.problem: Handlers split into separate files can be omitted from the public hooks module, causing import failures elsewhere.
insights.root_cause: Title generation was moved to its own file but initially not exported from the hooks mod.
insights.solution: Centralize and re-export the hook types (CompactionHandler, DoomLoopDetector, TitleGenerationHandler, TracingHandler) from mod.rs so other crates import a single stable surface.

key_files.why: Manages per-conversation background title generation with concurrency-safe sentries and oneshot coordination (Start/End event handlers) — central for background task lifecycle semantics.
Implements post-response compaction logic that triggers the Compactor to summarize conversation context when thresholds are exceeded — critical for bounding state and cost.
Detects and inserts mitigations for repeating tool-call patterns at request time to prevent agents from getting stuck in loops — affects request-phase behavior and tool execution flow.
Provides structured, non-panicking logging for lifecycle events (including token usage and tool call outcomes) used by debugging and telemetry.
Public export surface for hook handlers — other crates import from here, so changes affect module visibility and cross-crate compilation.

construct_relationships: TitleGenerationHandler registers per-conversation TitleTask sentries in a DashMap and implements EventHandle for Start and End payloads; Start enqueues a background generator, End awaits the oneshot result and applies the title.

CompactionHandler implements an EventHandle for response-phase EventData<ResponsePayload> and invokes a Compactor component to replace full conversation context with its compacted summary when configured thresholds are exceeded.

DoomLoopDetector implements an EventHandle for request-phase EventData<RequestPayload>, scans a Conversation's history for repeating tool-call patterns, and mutates the conversation by inserting a system reminder message to break loops.

TracingHandler implements EventHandle for multiple payload types (start, request, response, toolcall start/end, end) and consumes lifecycle event data (including token usage/cost metadata) to emit logs without affecting normal handler control flow.
mod.rs re-exports the handler types so orchestrator components subscribe to EventHandle implementations via the hooks module rather than file-specific imports.
notable_relationships: These hooks operate on Conversation and EventData types defined elsewhere in the application/forge_repo; changes must preserve the shape and semantics of those event payloads.

CompactionHandler relies on a Compactor implementation (outside this folder) that will transform conversation context; compaction changes affect downstream consumers that read conversation context (e.g., semantic search, tool selection, persistence).
Title generation tasks produce metadata that is applied when conversations end; Orchestrator or persistence layers expect titles to be set at End-time and may race with other End-time actions.
DoomLoopDetector's insertion of system reminder messages alters conversation history seen by subsequent handlers (compaction, tracing, providers) and therefore changes effective agent behavior.
Tracing records token usage and costs which may be produced by provider integrations; modifications to tracing or event payloads can affect telemetry aggregation and billing-related logic elsewhere.
recommendations: Preserve EventHandle<EventData<...>> signatures and the intended event-phase (Start, Request, Response, End, Toolcall) when editing handlers so orchestration wiring and subscription mechanisms remain valid.

When modifying title_generation logic, maintain the DashMap sentry pattern and oneshot-based result delivery to avoid task duplication, leaking JoinHandles, or panics on cancellation; unit tests around concurrency and lifecycle should be run.

Any edits to compaction must honor configured thresholds and atomic replacement semantics — ensure tests expect summarization to occur post-response and update persisted conversation state consistently.

Changes to doom_loop detection must keep detection timing at request phase and the behavior of inserting system reminder messages; ensure downstream handlers (compaction, tracing, tool execution) see the updated history as expected.

Keep TracingHandler non-panicking and low-overhead; avoid causing side effects that would alter event processing order. Run the crate's tests and workspace tests (cargo test --workspace) to validate no regressions.

Because these hooks interact with cross-crate types (Conversation, payload structs, Compactor, telemetry inputs), run at least the local crate tests and dependent crate tests after edits and ensure the public re-exports in mod.rs remain consistent.

Be mindful that compaction and title generation change conversation content that may be sent to external services (indexers, providers); validate privacy/telemetry expectations when altering what is inserted or removed from conversations.

crates/forge_domain/src/policies.description: This folder implements a small, focused policy subsystem for permission checks used across the codebase. It defines policy data types, pattern-matching rules, a configuration container, and an evaluation engine that resolves Allow/Deny/Confirm decisions for PermissionOperation instances. These components are the authoritative source of access-control semantics and thus influence any feature that performs filesystem access, tool execution, or guarded operations.
key_behaviors: Policy definition and composition: Policy and Rule types express permission intent (Read/Write/Execute/Fetch) with glob and directory-matching semantics.
Policy configuration: PolicyConfig collects ordered policy entries, supports lookup of matching rules, and exposes evaluation helper APIs.

Policy evaluation engine: PolicyEngine consumes a PolicyConfig and a PermissionOperation and returns Allow/Deny/Confirm by evaluating policies in order, short-circuiting on Deny or Confirm and otherwise returning the last Allow.
Operation-level checks: PermissionOperation values (Read/Write/Execute/Fetch) are the inputs to evaluation; rules and policies map to these operations and determine the final Permission.

insights.title: Ordered, short-circuiting resolution
insights.problem: Evaluation depends on policy order and stops early for Deny or Confirm, but otherwise returns the last observed Allow. Mistaking this semantics can change access decisions.
insights.root_cause: The engine intentionally treats Deny/Confirm with higher immediate precedence and Allow as fallthrough; the ordering of policies therefore affects outcome.
insights.solution: Respect and preserve the engine's order-sensitive, short-circuiting semantics when editing logic or adding new APIs. Any modifications must keep or explicitly re-document precedence rules and ensure callers expect the same behavior.

insights.title: Glob and directory-aware rule matching
insights.problem: Rules match operations using glob patterns and optional directory constraints; inconsistent path normalization or pattern handling will produce incorrect matches.
insights.root_cause: Rule types encapsulate pattern matching logic; differences in path normalization or escaping between components will change which rules match a PermissionOperation.
insights.solution: Maintain consistent path normalization and pattern evaluation across the module and callers. When changing rule logic, consider impacts on existing policies and ensure matching remains stable for typical filesystem/path inputs.

insights.title: Tight coupling between PermissionOperation, Policy, Rule, and Engine
insights.problem: The engine and PolicyConfig rely on specific variants and Display/enum shapes; adding variants or changing the Permission/Operation shapes can break exhaustive matches and evaluation paths.
insights.root_cause: Multiple modules (operation, policy, rule, engine, config, types) are interdependent and use enum matching and Display impls as part of evaluation and logging.
insights.solution: When modifying enums or public signatures, update all dependent pattern matches and Display implementations. Keep API stability in mind because callers across the workspace may construct PermissionOperation values or read policy outputs.

insights.title: Display implementations used for diagnostics
insights.problem: Display impls exist for Policy, rules, and Permission and are likely used in logs, config serialization, or user diagnostics; changing formats may affect tooling or stored text representations.
insights.root_cause: Display impls are implemented in multiple types and can be used by external callers for logging, config dumps, or CLI output.
insights.solution: Preserve or version any changes to human-facing textual representations. Validate downstream consumers (logs, saved policy files, CLI output) still parse or present the expected format after changes.

key_files.why: Implements the PolicyEngine which performs ordered evaluation and is the runtime entrypoint used to decide Allow/Deny/Confirm for operations.
Holds PolicyConfig which stores policy collections, supports lookup and evaluation helpers relied upon by the engine and configuration loading.
Defines Policy enum and evaluation helpers that map policy records to rule sets and permissions; core to interpreting policy entries.
Encapsulates rule types and pattern matching logic (glob/dir), the basis for matching PermissionOperation inputs.
Defines PermissionOperation variants (Read/Write/Execute/Fetch) used as inputs to the evaluation pipeline.

construct_relationships: PolicyEngine (engine.rs) drives evaluation by querying PolicyConfig::eval and Policy::eval to compute a Permission for a given PermissionOperation.
PolicyConfig (config.rs) contains ordered Policy entries and exposes find_rules which delegates to Policy::find_rules to obtain Rule sets for a given operation/context.
Policy (policy.rs) maps to specific Rule types (rule.rs) and exposes permission() and eval() helpers that the engine uses to interpret policy entries.
Rule implementations (rule.rs) perform glob/directory matching against PermissionOperation (operation.rs) inputs to determine applicability.
Permission enum (types.rs) is the normalized result type returned by PolicyEngine and used by callers to decide runtime behavior (permit, deny, require confirmation).
notable_relationships: Filesystem and tool access callers (e.g., crates/forge_fs, crates/forge_services) will depend on this policy subsystem to gate operations; ensure behavioral compatibility when changing evaluation or matching.
Configuration loading code (crates/forge_config) and any code that serializes or presents policies (CLI diagnostics, logs) may rely on the textual forms provided by Display impls in this module.
Stored project or user policy files that are parsed into PolicyConfig must remain compatible with the rule and policy shapes; changes here have downstream effects on persisted policy behavior.
Because policy evaluation influences potentially privacy-sensitive actions (file reads/uploads, executions), changes can alter security posture across workspace indexing and provider integrations.
recommendations: Preserve the engine's order-sensitive, short-circuiting semantics or explicitly document/review any change to precedence; callers expect Deny/Confirm to take immediate effect.
When modifying pattern-matching logic, ensure consistent path normalization and test against representative file/path inputs to avoid silent mismatches.
If adding or changing enum variants (PermissionOperation or Permission), update all matches across the folder and any consumers to avoid compilation/runtime mismatches.
Keep Display/textual representations stable for user-facing output and persisted forms; validate CLI output and any serialization consumers after edits.

Run the workspace test suite, clippy, and the crates that call into this module (notably filesystem and service integration crates) to catch regressions; consider tests that cover order and edge-case rule matches.

crates/forge_repo/src/conversation.description: This folder implements the conversation persistence layer: typed storage records and a Diesel-backed repository that maps in-memory Conversation domain objects to database rows and back. It isolates storage schema and migration logic from the domain model so persisted conversations remain readable across domain changes, and provides workspace-scoped CRUD operations used by higher-level services.
key_behaviors: Bi-directional conversion between domain Conversation types and storage Record types (serialization/deserialization) with TryFrom/From implementations.
Diesel-backed repository operations: upsert, fetch by id, fetch all, fetch last active, and delete, all scoped to a workspace and filtering out empty contexts.
Legacy variant detection and migration during deserialization to preserve compatibility with older persisted conversations.
Unit tests that exercise persistence behaviors including deserialization of older conversation forms (though gaps exist in coverage for some legacy migrations).

insights.title: Legacy ToolValue variants must be migrated during deserialization
insights.problem: Older persisted conversations used ToolValueRecord variants (Markdown, FileDiff, Pair) that no longer exist in the current domain model, causing deserialization failures when reading legacy data.
insights.root_cause: Domain model changes removed or renamed tool value variants while persisted data remained in older formats; the repository layer must reconcile the two.
insights.solution: Keep explicit legacy variants and migration code in the record->domain conversions, fail gracefully on unknown legacy shapes, and ensure conversion logic is exercised by tests. Treat the repository as the authoritative place to handle format evolution.

insights.title: Storage record types encapsulate a schema independent of domain model
insights.problem: Direct changes to domain types can silently break persisted data compatibility if conversions in this module are not updated accordingly.
insights.root_cause: Conversion logic (TryFrom/From) centralizes mapping; when domain shapes change, the repository will be out of sync unless conversions and migrations are adapted.
insights.solution: Any domain-type change that affects persisted fields requires coordinated updates to record types and conversion code here, and verification via persistence tests and DB migrations.

insights.title: Persistence tests exist but do not fully verify legacy migrations
insights.problem: Tests exercise many persistence behaviors and legacy deserialization paths, but the migration of specific legacy tool value formats is not fully validated.
insights.root_cause: Some legacy formats were added historically and conversions were implemented without comprehensive regression tests for each variant.
insights.solution: When modifying conversion or persistence code, run and extend unit tests that deserialize real legacy fixtures and assert mapping outcomes; treat these tests as contract checks for backward compatibility.

key_files.why: Defines the storage-focused Record types, conversion implementations, and legacy migration logic that determine how persisted conversation data is serialized/deserialized.
Implements the Diesel-backed ConversationRepository with workspace-scoped CRUD operations and is the entry point used by other crates to persist and load conversations.
Module surface that exposes the conversation record and repository implementations to the rest of the crate and downstream consumers.

construct_relationships: ConversationRecord types in conversation_record.rs implement TryFrom/From to convert to/from the domain Conversation types used elsewhere in the codebase.

ConversationRepositoryImpl in conversation_repo.rs depends on ConversationRecord conversions to map domain objects to DB representations when upserting and to reconstruct domain objects when fetching.
Migration logic lives in the record->domain conversion paths: legacy variants (old ToolValueRecord shapes) are detected and transformed into current domain equivalents during deserialization.

Repository methods (upsert_conversation, get_conversation, get_all_conversations, get_last_conversation, delete_conversation) interact with Diesel DatabasePool and rely on the record schemas defined in conversation_record.rs.
notable_relationships: Downstream callers (other crates/services) rely on ConversationRepositoryImpl for persistence; changes here affect any feature that loads or stores conversations (agents, CLI session persistence, TUI state).

Storage compatibility affects long-lived persisted data on disk or in workspace databases — migrations here interact implicitly with DB schema/migrations and any external tools that read those DB rows.
Conversion code must remain compatible with domain types defined elsewhere (forge_domain or equivalent); editing domain types without updating these conversions will break persistence.
CI constraints (treat warnings as errors) and existing unit tests in this crate mean edits must compile cleanly and preserve test expectations for legacy data handling.
recommendations: Preserve or intentionally update the TryFrom/From conversion invariants: any domain shape changes must be mirrored in record conversion logic and tested.
When touching serialization or record schemas, run the repository unit tests and add/maintain fixtures for legacy conversation variants to avoid regressions in migration paths.
Respect workspace scoping and the existing filtering behavior (e.g., filtering out empty contexts when listing or retrieving last conversation) to avoid changing semantics observed by callers.
Be mindful of Diesel/DB schema compatibility: coordinate code changes with any required DB migration steps and validate read/write behavior against existing persisted rows.
Ensure edits compile without warnings and run cargo test --workspace (CI enforces -Dwarnings); confirm that persistence-related tests (including legacy deserialization) pass before committing.

crates/forge_infra/src/auth.description: This folder implements the authentication layer used by Forge: HTTP OAuth provider integrations, shared OAuth/HTTP utilities, strategy-level decisions for provider-specific flows, and a central Error enum for auth flows. It is the source of truth for building auth URLs, exchanging/refreshing tokens, constructing HTTP clients with provider-specific quirks, and mapping runtime failures into well-defined errors consumed by higher-level services.
key_behaviors: Constructing provider-specific authorization URLs and PKCE/state handling for sign-in flows (build_auth_url).
Exchanging authorization codes / device/poll flows for tokens and creating normalized token responses (exchange_code, build_token_response, extract_oauth_tokens).
Refreshing access tokens and computing/annotating token expiry (refresh_access_token, calculate_token_expiry).

Building and configuring HTTP clients for provider calls, including injecting custom headers and provider-specific request shapes (build_http_client, inject_custom_headers, github_compliant_http_request).
Centralized error modeling and timeout/polling failure handling, enabling callers to react to initiation, polling, expiry, denial, and refresh errors.

insights.title: Anthropic uses non-standard PKCE/state handling
insights.problem: Anthropic deviates from standard OAuth PKCE/state expectations which requires special casing.
insights.root_cause: Provider-specific OAuth implementation differences that don't conform to RFC PKCE/state behavior.
insights.solution: Keep AnthropicHttpProvider as a separate implementation that overrides URL construction and token exchange behavior; callers must use the Anthropic provider type rather than assuming RFC semantics.

insights.title: Token expiry and refresh must be consistent across providers
insights.problem: Inconsistent expiry calculation or refresh behavior can lead to unexpected expired tokens or failed refreshes in clients.
insights.root_cause: Multiple provider implementations and utility helpers calculating expiry or refresh semantics independently.
insights.solution: Use shared utilities (calculate_token_expiry, build_token_response, refresh_access_token, extract_oauth_tokens) to normalize expiry timestamps and refresh flows; ensure providers feed token data into these helpers.

insights.title: HTTP client construction and header injection is central
insights.problem: Provider calls depend on a correctly-configured HTTP client and on the injection of provider-specific headers; mistakes cause API call failures or non-compliance with provider expectations.
insights.root_cause: Different providers require different headers or request formatting (e.g., GitHub compliance), and client configuration (timeouts, TLS, proxies) must be uniform.
insights.solution: Route provider HTTP setup through build_http_client and inject_custom_headers/github_compliant_http_request so that all providers share consistent client configuration while allowing per-provider customizations.

insights.title: Errors must map to actionable user guidance
insights.problem: Terse error messages or opaque failures (e.g., from ADC/Vertex flows) make re-authentication unclear to users.
insights.root_cause: Strategy-level flows (like ADC token acquisition) may omit necessary scopes or produce low-context errors.
insights.solution: Map internal errors (Error variants) to clear guidance and ensure strategy logic includes required scopes (e.g., cloud-platform for Vertex AI ADC) and augments error messages to instruct retry/reauthentication.

key_files.why: Defines the canonical Error enum used by all auth flows (initiation, poll, completion, refresh, timeout) — changes here affect callers and error handling semantics.
Houses shared helpers for token parsing/expiry, HTTP client setup, header injection, and error translation — core normalization point for provider implementations.
Contains strategy-level decisions (e.g., Google ADC/Vertex AI scope handling and error messaging) that alter auth behavior beyond per-provider mechanics.
Provider-specific implementation for Anthropic that intentionally deviates from standard PKCE/state handling and must be treated as special-case by callers.
Implements RFC-compliant OAuth flows used as the baseline for other providers; many providers will rely on this standard behavior.

construct_relationships: All Http provider types (StandardHttpProvider, GithubHttpProvider, AnthropicHttpProvider) expose build_auth_url, exchange_code, and build_http_client and therefore must feed token results into util::build_token_response / extract_oauth_tokens.
Provider implementations call util::build_http_client and util::inject_custom_headers to obtain correctly-configured clients and request shapes prior to network calls.

util::refresh_access_token and calculate_token_expiry are relied upon by strategy logic and higher-level callers to determine token freshness and trigger refresh paths; Error variants produced by refresh paths map back to auth::error::Error::RefreshFailed or Error::Expired.

auth::strategy augments provider behavior (e.g., by adding scopes for Google ADC) before invoking provider utilities and maps low-level errors into more actionable messages using util::handle_oauth_error.
error.rs’ Error variants are returned or converted by provider modules and util functions to provide a unified error surface to downstream callers.
notable_relationships: Higher-level crates (e.g., forge_services, forge_main) depend on this auth layer for provider login flows, token acquisition, and constructing authenticated HTTP clients; API stability here is important for those callers.

Token persistence and consumption (conversations, service clients) in other crates (e.g., forge_repo, forge_services) expect normalized token responses (expiry timestamps, refresh tokens) produced by util helpers.

Provider-specific quirks (Anthropic PKCE/state, GitHub request compliance) imply that consumer code must not assume uniform OAuth semantics; provider selection logic lives outside this folder but must align with these implementations.

Strategy-level choices (Google ADC scope injection for Vertex AI) interact with cloud provider SDKs and platform-specific authentication mechanisms outside this crate; changing scopes or messages can affect downstream integrations and user workflows.
recommendations: Do not change the Error enum shapes or meanings without updating all callers and error mappings across the workspace; any rename/add/remove will be widely breaking.
Keep token normalization (expiry timestamp, refresh token presence) stable — callers in other crates rely on util-built token responses. Verify token expiry calculations when modifying related code.
Preserve provider-specific behavior: Anthropic's non-standard PKCE/state and GitHub-compliant request shapes are intentional; edits must maintain those special cases or ensure consumers are updated.
When modifying HTTP client construction or header injection, ensure the changes maintain per-provider request expectations and consider timeouts/retry behavior; test live provider flows.

Run integration tests and live provider login flows (interactive) after changes, and execute workspace-wide test suites that exercise provider login, token refresh, and HTTP requests from downstream crates.

crates/forge_main/src/completer.description: This folder implements the interactive input completion layer used by the forge CLI/TUI. It provides reedline Completer implementations that offer file-path fuzzy completions, slash/command completions, special-character escaping and parsing of inline search markers (waterfall '@'), and formats suggestions for the interactive prompt. These components are important because they shape user experience for interactive workflows and must be consistent with command registration, workspace walking, and fuzzy-matching semantics elsewhere in the codebase.
key_behaviors: Provide context-aware completions for interactive input: file paths, shell-prefixed invocations, and slash-commands.
Fuzzy-match file system paths using a Walker and scoring (nucleo) and return sorted suggestions by computed score.
Delegate slash-prefixed inputs to a command completer that queries a central ForgeCommandManager and preserves special prefixes (slash, !).
Parse and handle inline search/term markers (waterfall '@') to influence completion parsing and match behavior.
Escape and sanitize user query strings before parsing into matching patterns to avoid malformed matches.

insights.title: Query string escaping is centralized and sensitive
insights.problem: User input may contain special characters that break pattern parsing or fuzzy matching if not escaped properly.
insights.root_cause: The completion code performs its own escaping for pattern parsing and relies on that escaping to feed the fuzzy matcher and any pattern parsing logic.
insights.solution: Maintain a single, well-tested escaping function and ensure callers use it consistently; unit tests should cover edge cases with shell metacharacters and mixed prefixes.

insights.title: Command vs file completion must be consistently routed
insights.problem: Ambiguity in whether an input should be treated as a slash-command or a file path could lead to incorrect suggestions or missing command completions.
insights.root_cause: InputCompleter decides when to delegate to CommandCompleter based on input prefixes; different prefix-handling rules are applied (slash vs shell !).
insights.solution: Preserve and respect the exact prefix-detection rules across completers and ensure CommandCompleter receives the raw and prefixed context it expects; tests should assert correct prefix retention in suggestion values.

insights.title: Fuzzy scoring determines suggestion ordering and UX
insights.problem: Inconsistent scoring or unstable sort orders can surface poor suggestions or confusing ranking for similar matches.
insights.root_cause: Matches are scored (via nucleo) and then sorted; differences in scoring inputs or tie-breaking rules can change the ordering.
insights.solution: Treat scoring as canonical for ordering, document tie-break rules (stable sort, secondary keys), and include tests that verify ranking behavior for representative path sets.

insights.title: Waterfall '@' marker parsing affects tokenization
insights.problem: Improper handling of inline search markers can change how the rest of the input is parsed and thus what completions are produced.
insights.root_cause: SearchTerm parsing logic interprets '@' markers and produces TermResult objects that feed completion parsing; mismatches between parser expectations and completer usage cause subtle bugs.
insights.solution: Keep SearchTerm behavior and its consumers aligned; include unit tests for various '@' placements and ensure completers use the TermResult structure consistently.

key_files.why: Primary user-facing completer: orchestrates file fuzzy-completion, escaping, scoring, and delegates slash commands to the CommandCompleter.
Handles slash- and shell-prefixed command suggestions by querying ForgeCommandManager and formats suggestions with the correct prefixes.
Parses inline '@' waterfall markers used in input tokenization and influences completion behavior across the completers.
Module surface used by callers; re-exports completer types and must remain in sync with public APIs of the completer implementations.

construct_relationships: InputCompleter implements reedline::Completer and uses a Walker to enumerate filesystem paths and nucleo for fuzzy scoring.
InputCompleter detects slash-prefixed inputs and delegates completion to CommandCompleter instead of performing file matching.
CommandCompleter queries a ForgeCommandManager (external registry) to obtain available commands and formats results with appropriate prefixes (slash or '!' shell prefix).
SearchTerm provides parsing/tokenization (TermResult) consumed by the completers to interpret '@' waterfall markers and adjust matching behavior.
mod.rs re-exports InputCompleter, CommandCompleter and ties their public surface into the rest of the crate.
notable_relationships: Depends on an external reedline completion contract — breaking changes to the reedline types or expected suggestion shape will require synchronized updates here.
Relies on a Walker implementation from the workspace/fs layer (another crate) for path enumeration; changes to Walker API or behavior affect path suggestions and performance.
Relies on a ForgeCommandManager (command registry) elsewhere in the codebase for available command metadata; changes to command registration will change suggestion content.
Uses a fuzzy-matching library (nucleo) for scoring; changing the matcher or its configuration could alter suggestion ordering across the UI.
Completion suggestions are surfaced in the interactive UI and therefore impact downstream UX components (TUI rendering, zsh plugin expectations about prefixed values).
recommendations: Preserve prefix semantics: ensure slash '/' and shell '!' prefixes remain intact in suggestion values and in the decision logic that delegates to CommandCompleter.
When editing escaping or parsing logic, run unit tests covering inputs with special characters, spaces, and mixed prefixes to avoid regressions in pattern parsing.
If touching scoring or sorting, include deterministic tie-breakers and tests that assert expected ordering for representative path sets to prevent UX regressions.
Consider thread-safety and performance: accesses to Walker and ForgeCommandManager may be IO-bound or shared; ensure edits do not introduce blocking or race conditions.
Keep mod.rs re-exports in sync with any public API changes so callers in other crates can find the completer types; update dependent call sites if signatures change.
Run cargo test and any completer-specific unit/integration tests; ensure CI constraints (warnings-as-errors) are satisfied when compiling changes that touch these files.

.github/scripts/bounty/src.description: This folder implements a self-contained TypeScript utility that synchronizes bounty-related labels and comments on GitHub issues and pull requests. It encapsulates a small domain model for GitHub objects, a rules engine that computes label/patch decisions, and CLI entrypoints that plan and apply those patches through a lightweight GitHub REST API wrapper. These scripts are intended to run in CI or maintenance contexts (under .github) so correctness, idempotency, and safe API usage matter.
key_behaviors: Model GitHub entities (issues, PRs, labels, users) and represent label operations as patches.
Evaluate repository-specific bounty rules (value labels, claimed/rewarded states, linked issue detection) to produce a minimal set of label add/remove operations for each issue or PR.
Interact with GitHub via a small REST wrapper: fetch issues/PRs, list issues by label prefix, and mutate state with add/remove labels and comments.
Provide CLI flows for planning-only and applying patches for a single issue or all issues, including token resolution and dry-run vs apply semantics.
Resolve and surface state transitions in a deterministic way so the patch application is idempotent and auditable (plan -> apply).

insights.title: GitHub REST interactions are central and failure-prone
insights.problem: Label and comment mutations depend on networked API calls which can fail, rate-limit, or return transient errors.
insights.root_cause: The folder implements direct REST calls without internal governance split across files; callers (sync scripts) assume successful eventual application.
insights.solution: Ensure callers and the API wrapper preserve error propagation and retry/backoff semantics where necessary; maintain distinction between planning and applying to avoid irreversible operations in case of partial failures.

insights.title: Rules engine encodes label semantics and parsing
insights.problem: Label classification (value labels, claimed, rewarded) and linked-issue detection rely on specific label name formats and regex patterns.
insights.root_cause: Rules (VALUE_LABEL_RE, prefixes like BOUNTY_*) are encoded in rules.ts and are assumed by plan/apply flows and by tests/automation.
insights.solution: Any edits must preserve regex semantics and label naming conventions, and update all affected consumers (plan, apply, tests, CI callers) when changing formats.

insights.title: Token resolution and runtime environment vary between CI and local runs
insights.problem: resolveToken and CLI scripts read tokens/environment differently; changes may break CI workflows or local maintenance runs.
insights.root_cause: Scripts live under .github and are expected to be invoked by workflows that set env vars or by maintainers with different env setups.
insights.solution: Maintain existing token resolution behavior or ensure any change preserves compatibility with GitHub Actions environment variables and locally-provided tokens; clearly surface missing-token errors.

insights.title: Patch computation returns minimal ops but application can be non-atomic
insights.problem: computeIssuePatch/computePrPatch produce label ops which are applied via multiple API calls; partial application can leave state inconsistent.
insights.root_cause: GitHub REST API exposes label adds/removes as separate endpoints; no transactional endpoint exists.
insights.solution: Respect the plan/apply separation: callers must consider ordering, handle partial failures, and preserve the ability to re-compute and reconcile patches safely.

insights.title: Local TypeScript types mirror GitHub payloads
insights.problem: Types (Issue, PullRequest, Label, Patch) are authoritative for logic but can drift from actual GitHub API shapes.
insights.root_cause: Types are defined locally in types.ts rather than generated from an API schema.
insights.solution: When modifying request/response handling, keep types.ts in sync with API wrapper changes and any consumers of those types (rules, planners, CLI).

key_files.why: Encapsulates all GitHub REST interactions used by the sync tools (fetching and mutating issues/PRs); edits here affect network behavior and error handling.
Contains the rules engine that computes label patches and the label parsing/regex logic that drives all state decisions; core business logic lives here.
CLI entrypoint for planning and applying patches for a single issue; wires token resolution, planning, and application flows.
CLI entrypoint to enumerate issues (by label prefix) and run plan/apply across many items; coordinates bulk operations and interacts with rate/throughput concerns.
Defines the domain model and patch types consumed by rules and API wrapper; changes here cascade to logic and API call signatures.

construct_relationships: sync-issue.ts and sync-all-issues.ts call into GitHubRestApi (api.ts) to fetch issues/PRs and to apply label/comment mutations.
planIssue and planAllIssues use rules.computeIssuePatch / computePrPatch to derive LabelOp patches from Issue/PR instances defined in types.ts.
api.ts returns typed objects that conform to types.ts; rules.ts consumes those types to apply regex-based classification (VALUE_LABEL_RE, BOUNTY_* constants).
applyPatch in sync-issue.ts translates Patch.LabelOp entries into sequences of GitHubRestApi.addLabels or removeLabel calls, so ordering and idempotency depend on both modules.
notable_relationships: These scripts are housed under .github and are likely invoked by GitHub Actions workflows; changes to token usage, invocation args, or output behavior must remain compatible with workflow consumers.
Network-dependent behavior ties the folder to GitHub API semantics and rate limits; callers in CI may rely on predictable timing and failure modes.
Label naming conventions and bounty-related labels are repository-level conventions (BOUNTY_*); other tooling or documentation in the repo may depend on these exact labels and regexes.
Runtime assumptions: this is TypeScript code expected to run in a Node environment available in CI or local maintenance scripts—build/TS runtime compatibility matters for changes.
recommendations: Preserve the plan vs apply separation: keep compute* functions pure and ensure CLI flows continue to support dry-run planning before mutating GitHub state.
Do not change label name formats, regexes, or the semantics of BOUNTY_* labels without updating all consumers and CI workflows that depend on them.
When editing api.ts, maintain or explicitly handle HTTP error propagation and consider where retries/backoff are required; ensure callers receive meaningful errors for partial failures.
Any changes to token resolution (resolveToken) must remain compatible with GitHub Actions environment variables and local developer usage; surface clear errors when token is missing.
Keep types.ts in sync with request/response shapes used by api.ts and rules.ts; update all references when type shapes change.
Because patch application is multi-step, ensure edits preserve idempotency and make it possible to re-run planners to reconcile state after partial failures.
Run integration-style validation (or a CI workflow that exercises the scripts against a test repo) after changes that affect API calls, label ops, or token handling.

crates/forge_repo/src/database.description: This folder contains the SQLite database plumbing for the forge_repo crate: a connection-pool builder with PRAGMA tuning and retry semantics, the Diesel schema mapping used by repository code, and the embedded, versioned SQL migrations that evolve the on-disk schema (conversations, workspace, indexing_auth, indexes, and metrics). It matters because other crates and higher-level repository logic depend on pool creation, applied migrations, and the exact table/column/index layout defined here; changes here affect persistence, upgrades, tests, and runtime concurrency behavior.
key_behaviors: Create and configure r2d2/Diesel SQLite connection pools with PRAGMA tuning for concurrency and configurable PoolConfig.
Apply embedded Diesel migrations (MIGRATIONS) at pool creation time and provide up/down SQL scripts for versioned schema changes.
Retry and backoff on transient connection/migration failures to increase robustness at startup.
Provide an in-memory pool option tailored for tests to avoid filesystem DB side-effects.
Expose a Diesel-generated schema module that must match the current migrations for compile-time ORM bindings.

insights.title: Migrations must be kept consistent with Diesel schema.rs
