insights.problem: SQL migrations add/remove columns and tables (e.g., metrics, workspace, indexing_auth) while schema.rs provides compile-time mappings; divergence causes compile/runtime errors or incorrect queries.
insights.root_cause: Schema changes are expressed in SQL migration files but require a matching update to the Diesel-generated schema module and any consumer code that references columns/tables.
insights.solution: When modifying migrations or SQL schema, update/re-generate schema.rs and audit all consumers of the affected tables/columns; ensure embedded MIGRATIONS are updated and the migration up/down scripts are consistent.

insights.title: Transient SQLite errors require retry/backoff
insights.problem: Pool creation and applying migrations can fail transiently (e.g., database locks, busy errors) causing startup/migration to abort.
insights.root_cause: SQLite contention and platform-specific file-lock semantics combined with concurrent processes or early database access.
insights.solution: Respect and retain the existing retry/backoff logic and PRAGMA tuning when changing pool creation behavior; any change to timeouts or concurrency PRAGMAs must be validated under contention.

insights.title: Table lifecycle changes introduce consumer risk
insights.problem: Migrations show tables created and later dropped (workspace, indexing_auth). Dropping or altering tables risks breaking other crates or losing persisted data.
insights.root_cause: Evolving feature requirements leading to removal/renaming of persistence artifacts without guaranteed consumer migration paths.
insights.solution: Before editing or removing migrations/tables, identify all cross-crate consumers of the affected tables/columns and ensure migration paths or compatibility layers are considered; keep up/down scripts present and consistent for rollbacks.

insights.title: Index migrations affect performance and implicit query expectations
insights.problem: Index creation/removal changes query performance characteristics and may be relied upon by repository query patterns (e.g., ordering/filtering by workspace/updated).
insights.root_cause: Indexes are managed via migrations and not enforced in application code; removing/renaming indexes can degrade performance or alter query plans.
insights.solution: When editing migrations or altering indexed columns, check repository queries that depend on those indexes and benchmark/validate query performance; keep index up/down SQL in sync.

insights.title: In-memory pool differences versus on-disk DB
insights.problem: An in-memory pool option is provided for tests; behavior (locking, persistence) differs from on-disk DB and can hide integration issues.
insights.root_cause: SQLite semantics differ between shared on-disk DBs and ephemeral in-memory databases used in tests.
insights.solution: When changing pool configuration or migrations, run both in-memory tests and integration tests against on-disk SQLite to validate behavior under real-world conditions; be cautious that in-memory tests may not exercise file-lock contention or migration persistence.

key_files.why: Central place that constructs r2d2/Diesel SQLite pools, applies PRAGMA tuning, runs embedded migrations (MIGRATIONS), and implements retry/backoff and test in-memory pool creation.
Diesel-generated schema module providing compile-time table/column mappings used by repository code; must match migrations.
Initial create of the core conversations table (conversation_id, title, workspace_id) — foundational persisted domain model used across the repo.
Adds critical indexes used by repository queries for workspace/updated/active filtering; impacts performance and query expectations.
Module entrypoint that wires pool and schema modules together and is the import surface for other crates in the workspace.

construct_relationships: PoolConfig -> DatabasePool: PoolConfig parameters (timeouts, sizes) are consumed when building DatabasePool which configures r2d2 and PRAGMAs.
DatabasePool -> MIGRATIONS: DatabasePool runs the embedded MIGRATIONS during pool initialization to ensure schema is at expected version.
Sqlite PRAGMA tuning in pool.rs -> runtime concurrency/locking: PRAGMA settings set at connection creation affect how SQLite behaves under concurrent access.
schema.rs (Diesel) <-> migrations/*.sql: schema.rs must reflect the current state of the SQL migrations; migrations mutate the structures schema.rs references.
In-memory pool (tests) -> pool.rs: test code paths create an in-memory DatabasePool variant that bypasses filesystem persistence but still expects migrations to be applied.
notable_relationships: Other crates (repository layer, features referencing conversations/workspace) depend on table/column names defined here (conversation_id, workspace_id, metrics) and will break if migrations or schema.rs change without coordinated updates.

Workspace/indexing features and external indexing services rely on indexing_auth and workspace table structures — creating/dropping these tables affects integrations with the embedding/indexing subsystem.
Diesel and r2d2 are upstream dependencies; changes here must respect Diesel's compile-time schema expectations and r2d2 pooling semantics used across the workspace.
Tests across the workspace may rely on the in-memory pool variant supplied by this module; CI and local test runs may exercise different code paths (in-memory vs on-disk).
recommendations: If you change any SQL migration, update embedded MIGRATIONS and the Diesel schema.rs to remain consistent, and include matching down.sql for rollbacks.
Preserve or carefully adjust retry/backoff and PRAGMA tuning in pool.rs; validate changes under contention to avoid startup/migration failures.

Before removing or altering tables/indexes, scan other crates for usages of those tables/columns (conversation_id, workspace_id, workspace, indexing_auth, metrics) to avoid runtime breakage or data loss.
Run both unit tests that use the in-memory pool and integration tests using an on-disk SQLite instance after edits; ensure CI (cargo test --workspace) and migrations succeed locally.
Keep index creation/removal changes accompanied by query performance validation; ensure index names and semantics used in migrations match repository query assumptions.
Remember CI enforces strict warnings-as-errors; run cargo check/clippy in workspace to avoid CI failures after edits impacting database code.

.github/scripts/bounty/tests.description: This folder contains TypeScript unit tests that validate the bounty-labeling and reward-patch logic used by the .github/scripts/bounty workflow. The tests exercise per-issue and per-PR sync flows, the orchestration that plans and syncs all issues, and the core patch-computation helpers; they encode expectations about GitHub API interactions and label naming conventions that other bounty scripts rely on.
key_behaviors: Computation of textual/patch updates for issues and PRs (computeIssuePatch / computePrPatch) used to apply bounty/reward information.
Per-issue synchronization flow that uses a mocked GitHub API to add/remove labels and apply computed patches.
Per-PR synchronization flow that propagates reward/label state between PRs and their linked issues (linkedIssueNumbers).
Bulk planning and syncing that enumerates issues, plans required updates, and invokes per-issue sync logic (planAllIssues -> syncAllIssues -> syncIssue).
Test harness construction of GitHub-like objects (makeIssue/makePr) and a controllable mock API (makeMockApi) to assert side effects and label names.

insights.title: Tests tightly couple to a mock GitHub API shape
insights.problem: The tests depend on a specific mocked API surface (makeMockApi) and expect particular method calls and return shapes.
insights.root_cause: Production scripts interact with GitHub REST objects and the tests assert side effects via a handcrafted mock that mirrors that shape.
insights.solution: When changing GitHub interaction code ensure the mock API and tests are updated in lockstep; maintain the mock's method signatures and expected object shapes to avoid test breaks.

insights.title: Label name constants and semantics are central to assertions
insights.problem: Multiple tests assert exact labelNames and label-manipulation behavior; changes to label naming or label-driven logic will break many tests.
insights.root_cause: Business logic for reward levels and bounty status is expressed via label strings and label operations asserted in tests.
insights.solution: Treat label naming and label application/removal semantics as an important contract: any edits to label logic require corresponding updates to all tests that reference labelNames and label operations.

insights.title: Patch computation tests encode exact formatting and linked-issue handling
insights.problem: computeIssuePatch/computePrPatch tests validate exact patch contents and behavior for edge cases (e.g., linked issues, missing fields).
insights.root_cause: The user-visible content inserted into issues/PRs must adhere to a stable text/patch format, so tests check for exact outputs.
insights.solution: If the textual layout, delimiters, or handling of linked issues changes, update the tests that assert computeIssuePatch/computePrPatch outputs; treat these tests as guards for user-visible output.

insights.title: Bulk sync tests enforce orchestration order and behavior
insights.problem: planAllIssues and syncAllIssues tests assert planning and per-issue sync invocation sequences; modifying orchestration can invalidate test expectations.
insights.root_cause: The multi-step flow (plan -> per-item sync -> label/patch application) has interdependent steps whose order and conditions are asserted in tests.
insights.solution: When changing orchestration, align tests to reflect new sequences and ensure mocks capture the same call ordering and side effects.

key_files.why: Validates core patch-generation logic and linked-issue computations that determine the exact content applied to issues/PRs.
Exercises the per-issue sync flow including label changes and patch application against a mock API; critical for verifying single-item behavior.
Tests PR-specific propagation and how PRs influence linked issue labels/rewards, ensuring cross-object interactions are correct.
Asserts bulk planning and syncing behavior, ensuring the end-to-end orchestration invokes per-issue logic correctly.

construct_relationships: computeIssuePatch and computePrPatch produce textual patches that syncIssue and syncPr apply via mock API calls in tests.
linkedIssueNumbers is used by PR sync tests to determine which issues a PR should influence and to validate propagation of labels/rewards.
planAllIssues produces a plan consumed by syncAllIssues; syncAllIssues iterates and delegates to syncIssue for each planned item in tests.
makeMockApi is shared across tests to assert expected GitHub interactions (label additions/removals, patch application) and to simulate API responses.
makeIssue / makePr create the test fixtures (issue/PR shapes) that drive compute* and sync* test scenarios; labelNames are referenced by these fixtures.
notable_relationships: These tests mirror and assert behavior of scripts in .github/scripts/bounty (production code) and therefore are coupled to that implementation's public contracts.

The test suite encodes expectations about GitHub API object shapes and side effects (labels, comments/patches), implying downstream workflows (CI, release automation) rely on stable label/payload formats.
Any changes to label naming, patch format, or orchestration in the bounty scripts will require coordinated updates to these tests to keep CI green.
recommendations: Run the test suite after any edits: update or re-run the TypeScript tests that use the mock API to ensure call shapes and assertions remain valid.
When editing code that interacts with GitHub objects, update makeMockApi/makeIssue/makePr fixtures and ensure labelNames referenced in tests match the implementation's constants.
Preserve or intentionally update computeIssuePatch/computePrPatch outputs together with their tests—these tests check exact user-visible text and linked-issue handling.
If changing orchestration (planAllIssues/syncAllIssues/syncIssue/syncPr), adjust test expectations about call ordering and side effects in sync-all-issues and per-item sync tests.
Keep mock API method signatures and return shapes synchronized with production code interactions; failing to do so will cause widespread test failures.

crates/forge_app/src/truncation.description: This folder provides focused utilities for truncating different kinds of textual content produced or consumed by the application: network fetch responses, search results, and shell command outputs. The truncation primitives produce small, metadata-bearing output types so callers can safely display, store, or transmit large payloads while preserving information needed to reason about what was removed. These utilities matter because they centralize size/format decisions and invariants that other subsystems (UI, storage, network, indexing) rely on for correctness and privacy.
key_behaviors: Accept large text-like inputs (HTTP fetch bodies, search hits, stdout/stderr) and return a bounded representation plus metadata indicating truncation.
Support multiple truncation strategies/modes (e.g., character-count, byte-count, line-based) and encode which mode was used in the output.
Preserve contextual metadata (original length, truncated flag, which portion was kept) so downstream code can render placeholders or request full content when necessary.
Operate deterministically with respect to boundaries and encoding (e.g., not splitting multi-byte UTF-8 sequences when truncating).
Expose small, typed DTOs (TruncatedFetchOutput, TruncatedSearchOutput, TruncatedShellOutput) that other crates consume for display, storage, or telemetry.

insights.title: Truncation must respect encoding and line boundaries
insights.problem: Truncating by raw byte counts or naive character counts can split multi-byte UTF-8 sequences or break expected line boundaries, corrupting displayed text or producing invalid UTF-8.
insights.root_cause: Different truncation modes (bytes vs characters vs lines) are used across files and callers may pass inputs with non-ASCII content or varying newline conventions.
insights.solution: Ensure truncation implementations consistently handle UTF-8 (avoid splitting code points), define clear semantics per mode (what 'line' means), and include metadata that records the mode and exact counts used so callers can interpret results reliably.

insights.title: Outputs must remain stable and self-describing
insights.problem: Callers across the app expect specific fields and guarantees from Truncated*Output types; changing fields or semantics silently can break UI rendering, storage schemas, or network payloads.
insights.root_cause: The truncation functions return small DTOs consumed by multiple subsystems; there is no code here that indicates schema evolution handling.
insights.solution: Maintain field names and semantics (truncated flag, original length, mode, snippet content). When evolving fields, keep backward-compatible serialization/field defaults and update all consumers and tests together.

insights.title: Different truncation utilities must present consistent semantics
insights.problem: Search truncation (lines/bytes), fetch truncation (character limit), and shell truncation (stdout/stderr clipping) use different criteria and may surface different metadata, leading to inconsistent behavior in UIs that display heterogeneous content.
insights.root_cause: Separate implementations evolved for distinct inputs without an enforced cross-file contract.
insights.solution: Treat the outputs as part of a conceptual contract: each truncated result should expose whether truncation occurred, what mode and limit were applied, and the original size metric. Callers should not rely on implicit behaviors that differ between utilities.

key_files.why: Implements HTTP/fetch body truncation by character limit and defines TruncatedFetchOutput used by network/display code.
Implements line/byte-based truncation for search results, defines TruncationMode and TruncatedSearchOutput which are likely consumed by search presentation and indexing flows.
Handles clipping of command stdout/stderr and produces TruncatedShellOutput with per-stream metadata; relevant to running tools and displaying their output safely.
Acts as the submodule aggregator and public surface for the truncation utilities; changes here affect how callers import and expose these helpers.

construct_relationships: truncate_fetch::truncate_fetch_content returns TruncatedFetchOutput containing the snippet, original size, and a truncation flag; callers rely on this DTO to decide display vs fetch-full behavior.
truncate_search::truncate_search_output uses TruncationMode (e.g., Lines, Bytes) to decide how to slice search hits and returns TruncatedSearchOutput with detailed metadata about what was kept.

truncate_shell::truncate_shell_output truncates Stdout and Stderr independently and packages them into TruncatedShellOutput; consumers expect per-stream metadata (truncated, length) to render or log appropriately.
All Truncated*Output types play the same role: carry both the truncated content and descriptive metadata so downstream code can make informed decisions without re-inspecting the original payload.
notable_relationships: These truncation utilities are upstream of UI/display, storage/persistence, and network/indexing code — their outputs may be serialized, persisted, or sent externally, so format and metadata choices have cross-crate impact.
Search truncation decisions affect workspace indexing/semantic search consumers; inconsistent truncation semantics could change ranking or snippet presentation across clients.

Shell truncation interacts with tool-running code and may be used to decide what to log or include in telemetry — truncation semantics therefore have privacy/security implications for what is sent/stored.
recommendations: Preserve DTO field names, meanings, and serialization behavior; if changing, update all callers and tests in same commit to avoid breakage.
Respect UTF-8 and multi-byte character boundaries; verify truncation does not produce invalid encoding or split grapheme clusters if callers expect intact characters.
Ensure truncation metadata includes the mode used, original size metric, and a boolean truncated flag so callers can react without re-processing the original input.
Run the crate's unit tests and the workspace test suite after edits; CI enforces -Dwarnings, so keep builds warning-free.

When editing public signatures in mod.rs or renaming exports, update imports across the repository (UI, storage, search, and tool-running code) and check serialization compatibility where these outputs are persisted or transmitted.

crates/forge_ci/src/steps.description: This folder provides small, reusable CI step implementations used when generating GitHub Actions workflows for the workspace. Its primary role is to centralize the logic for installing the Protobuf compiler (protoc) so workflow generators across the repository can include a consistent, versioned step for proto compilation during CI builds.
key_behaviors: Produce a standardized GitHub Actions step that installs protoc via a marketplace action.
Expose the step(s) through a module re-export so workflow generation code can import them.
Serve as the single source of truth for protoc installation details (action name, inputs, versioning) used across generated workflows.

insights.title: Single reusable step centralizes protoc install details
insights.problem: If the step hardcodes an action/version or input shape that changes upstream, all generated workflows will break simultaneously.
insights.root_cause: Centralization of protoc installation configuration without parameterization or compatibility checks.
insights.solution: Treat the step as the authoritative place for protoc action/version; when updating, coordinate with workflow consumers and pin versions to avoid unexpected upstream changes.

insights.title: Protoc version compatibility with generated code and plugins
insights.problem: Installed protoc version may be incompatible with proto-generated code, codegen plugins, or language-specific protoc-gen binaries used elsewhere in CI.
insights.root_cause: The install step determines which protoc binary is available in CI but does not control plugin versions or downstream build assumptions.
insights.solution: Ensure the chosen protoc version aligns with consumers (proto crates, codegen tools) and document required versions; consider exposing protoc version as an input consumed by workflow generators.

insights.title: Runner/platform differences affect installer action
insights.problem: Marketplace actions or their inputs may behave differently on Linux, macOS, or Windows runners, causing CI divergence per platform.
insights.root_cause: The step delegates installation to an external action that may have platform-specific behavior or requirements.
insights.solution: Be aware of target run environments in consumers and validate the step across all CI runner OSes used by the repository.

insights.title: External action availability and network dependency
insights.problem: The step relies on an external GitHub Action and network access; outages or changes in the action can break CI.
insights.root_cause: Dependency on a third-party action hosted on GitHub Marketplace rather than a bundled binary.
insights.solution: Recognize that CI runs depend on network and upstream action availability; consider pinning action versions and coordinating updates with CI owners.

insights.title: Module re-export expectation
insights.problem: Consumers expect a stable function signature or exported name; changing the export or function signature will break callers.
insights.root_cause: mod.rs simply re-exports setup_protoc and other code imports it directly.
insights.solution: Maintain the public API (exported names and signatures) or prepare to update all consumers when making breaking changes.

key_files.why: Defines the reusable setup_protoc() function that constructs the GitHub Actions step to install protoc; the functional core of this folder.
Re-exports setup_protoc, forming the public module surface that workflow generators import.

construct_relationships: mod.rs re-exports setup_protoc from setup_protoc.rs so callers import steps::setup_protoc directly.
setup_protoc produces a GitHub Actions step object consumed by workflow-generation code elsewhere in the forge_ci crate or by other crates that generate CI workflows.
notable_relationships: Downstream CI workflows (generated .github/workflows) depend on the exact behavior and inputs emitted by setup_protoc; changes here affect CI jobs that compile protobufs across the workspace.

Protoc installation must be compatible with proto compilation steps in other crates (e.g., crates that include .proto files or proto-generated code); version mismatches can cause build failures in those crates.
Relies on GitHub Marketplace action availability and network access during CI; any breaking changes or outages upstream will impact workspace CI runs.
recommendations: Preserve the public API: do not change the exported name or signature of setup_protoc without updating all callers.
When editing, validate generated workflow output (the produced step YAML/object) to ensure action names, inputs, and versions remain correct and compatible with CI runners.
Consider cross-platform behavior: test the resulting workflow step on the CI runner OSes used by the project (Linux, macOS, Windows) or at least validate installer inputs for those platforms.
Run the crate's unit tests and any workflow-generation integration tests; ensure workspace CI builds that invoke protoc still pass after changes.
When updating pinned action versions or default protoc versions, coordinate with consumers that perform proto compilation to avoid version incompatibilities.

crates/forge_app/src/dto.description: This dto subtree translates between the project's internal chat/tool/domain model and external provider JSON shapes (OpenAI-style, Anthropic, Google/Gemini). It centralizes request serialization, response parsing, and a provider-specific transformation pipeline that applies compatibility shims, schema enforcement, caching markers, and tool-call adaptations so the rest of the system can remain provider-agnostic. This layer is critical for correct tool invocation, streaming behavior, billing/usage extraction, and interoperability with heterogeneous proxies and model backends.
key_behaviors: Map internal forge_domain Context, ContextMessage, and ToolCallFull into provider-specific Request DTOs (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google) including tool-call argument serialization, images, and cache-control annotations.

Apply a conditional, ordered transformation pipeline (ProviderPipeline) that mutates Request DTOs per provider/model by composing many small Transformers (e.g., DropToolCalls, SetCache, SanitizeToolIds, EnforceStrictObjectSchema, compatibility shims).
Serialize/normalize tool call arguments and tool metadata (including converting double-encoded JSON and enforcing tool input schema) to match provider expectations.

Parse streamed and non-streamed provider responses (events, content blocks, choices) into internal ChatCompletionMessage and Usage objects, normalizing provider quirks (cost-as-string, proxy-only cost events, stop reasons, tool-result assembly).
Gracefully tolerate provider/proxy idiosyncrasies (unknown events, ping/cost events, different reasoning output formats) and surface domain-equivalent semantics downstream.

insights.title: Tool call argument double-encoding
insights.problem: Tool call arguments sometimes arrive already serialized as JSON strings or in forms that naive re-serialization produces incorrect double-encoded payloads.
insights.root_cause: Tool arguments are heterogeneous (strings that may be JSON or structured values) and the serializer indiscriminately applies serde_json::to_string without normalizing the existing content shape.
insights.solution: When converting domain tool calls to provider payloads, detect and preserve already-serialized JSON vs structured values; normalize to the provider-expected JSON schema to avoid double-encoding and ensure tool parsers receive correct types.

insights.title: Cost/usage fields vary across providers and proxies
insights.problem: Some providers or proxies emit cost/usage as strings, numbers, or as separate ping/cost events rather than within standard usage objects, causing deserialization or semantic loss.
insights.root_cause: Heterogeneous proxy implementations and provider event variants (e.g., ping events carrying cost) lead to inconsistent shapes.
insights.solution: Parsers must accept numeric-or-string cost encodings and recognize cost-only ping events, normalizing them into the internal Usage/cost model so billing/telemetry consumers receive consistent data.

insights.title: Model-specific field expectations (reasoning_content, Kimi K2)
insights.problem: Some backends expect reasoning output encoded as a flat field (reasoning_content) rather than an array of reasoning_details, producing invalid requests if not adapted.
insights.root_cause: Different model/proxy implementations have divergent schemas for reasoning/output metadata.
insights.solution: Apply provider/model-aware transformers in the pipeline (e.g., add flat alias fields for Kimi K2) so requests conform to backend expectations while preserving domain semantics.

insights.title: Tool/Call ID characters rejected by Anthropic/Vertex
insights.problem: Tool call IDs containing characters outside the allowed set cause 400 Bad Request rejections from Anthropic/Vertex endpoints.
insights.root_cause: Anthropic requires tool IDs to match a stricter regex (^[a-zA-Z0-9_-]+$) while domain IDs may be arbitrary.
insights.solution: Sanitize or map tool IDs to an allowed character set in a transformer before sending to Anthropic variants and ensure mappings are reversible/traceable so tool results correlate back to domain tool IDs.

insights.title: Pipeline ordering affects semantics (caching, tool removal, schema enforcement)
insights.problem: Missing or mis-ordered transformers (for example, SetCache not applied for particular proxy paths like open_router/minimax) can change runtime semantics (duplicate calls, missing cache markers) or produce invalid payloads.
insights.root_cause: Provider and model differences require conditional application and ordering of transformers; tests and defaults do not cover all proxy/model combinations.
insights.solution: Maintain explicit, well-documented ordering invariants in the ProviderPipeline and ensure provider detection logic enables the correct transformer set per model/proxy to preserve caching, tool semantics, and schema validity.

insights.title: Streaming events and unknown variants
insights.problem: Provider streaming can include unexpected event types or embed tool/reasoning details in unusual places; failing to tolerate unknown events can drop useful information or crash parsers.
insights.root_cause: Proxies and upstream services evolve and emit heartbeat/ping/cost events or alternative payload shapes.
insights.solution: Response parsing should be defensive: accept unknown events gracefully, extract cost/ping when present, and assemble content blocks into ChatCompletionMessage while preserving tool-use and reasoning fragments.

key_files.why: Primary conversion logic mapping internal Context and tool calls into OpenAI-style Request payloads; contains tool argument serialization and caching annotations that affect outbound behavior.
Composes provider-specific transformers and encodes ordering/conditional logic for request adaptations per provider/model; changes here alter how all OpenAI-like requests are mutated.
Converts domain Context into Anthropic request shapes and encodes Anthropic-specific fields (reasoning/output_config, tool-call encoding) and cache markers used by Anthropic pipeline.
Parses Anthropic streaming events into domain ChatCompletionMessage and Usage types, handling provider idiosyncrasies like ping/cost encodings and tool-use extraction.
Normalizes OpenAI-like responses (streamed and non-streamed) into internal messages and usage, including handling proxy-specific cost formats and reconstructing tool-call results.

construct_relationships: forge_domain::Context -> dto.openai::Request and dto.anthropic::Request via From/TryFrom implementations: domain context and messages are transformed into provider request DTOs.

ProviderPipeline (openai/transformers/pipeline.rs) composes Transformer implementations (DropToolCalls, SetCache, SanitizeToolIds, EnforceStrictObjectSchema, etc.) to mutate Request DTOs conditionally based on provider/model detection functions.

Transformer modules (openai and anthropic transforms) operate on the same Request DTO shape and must preserve mapping metadata (tool IDs, cache annotations) so response parsers can re-associate provider results with domain tool calls.

Provider response DTOs (openai/response.rs, anthropic/response.rs, google/response.rs) parse events/choices into forge_domain::ChatCompletionMessage and Usage; these conversions rely on the outbound request’s tool-call encoding and id sanitization to reconstruct tool results correctly.
dto::mod.rs re-exports provider DTO modules so upstream network/service clients consume the correct Request/Response types for each provider integration (forge_services and provider clients).
notable_relationships: dto layer is upstream of network/service clients in crates/forge_services which send serialized request JSON to providers and receive streamed responses that feed into dto parsers; changes to DTO shapes or transformers directly affect network payloads and runtime provider compatibility.

Conversation persistence and downstream UI/streaming (forge_repo, forge_stream, forge_markdown_stream, forge_display) expect normalized ChatCompletionMessage and Usage shapes from dto response converters; mismatches can break storage, UI streaming, or telemetry.

Caching markers set by SetCache and related transformers influence whether upstream provider clients (and proxies like open_router/minimax) will cache responses; missing or misapplied cache flags can cause redundant provider calls and affect performance/cost.

Provider/proxy heterogeneity (OpenCode Zen, OpenRouter, Kimi K2, Vertex Anthropic, GitHub Copilot proxies) requires dto to normalize many non-standard encodings (cost-as-string, ping events, reasoning_content aliases, nullable enum shapes).
recommendations: When editing conversions (From/TryFrom) preserve existing mapping semantics for tool-call argument encoding and cache-control annotations; any change affects outbound provider payloads and response re-association.
Respect transformer ordering and conditional provider/model detection in ProviderPipeline; verify that any added/modified transformer is composed in the correct place for all supported backends.

Update and run unit tests covering serialization/roundtrip (including double-encoded tool args), provider-specific transformers, and streaming event parsing; ensure tests exercise proxy oddities (string-or-number cost, ping events).
Maintain defensive parsing of provider streams: accept numeric-or-string cost fields and unknown event variants, and ensure response-to-domain mappings remain tolerant to proxy variations.
If altering tool ID handling or sanitization, ensure mappings are reversible or adequately logged so tool results can be correlated back to original domain IDs.

Run the crate and workspace test suite, clippy, and the project's CI linting constraints (CI treats warnings as errors via RUSTFLAGS -Dwarnings) to avoid regressions that fail CI; consider integration tests that exercise multiple provider pipelines where available.

crates/forge_app/src.description: The crates/forge_app/src subtree is the application core that orchestrates agent chat flows, tool execution, provider/model selection, compaction, and the translation between domain operations and external provider DTOs. It centralizes high-level behaviors (request lifecycle, tool dispatch, formatting of tool results, and persistence/compaction) and defines the interfaces the runtime implements (infra/services), so changes here affect end-to-end agent behavior, UI output, and integrations with provider/back-end implementations.
key_behaviors: Drive end-to-end chat turns: ForgeApp loads conversations/config, resolves agent/provider/model, builds context, invokes orchestrator, and streams ChatResponse events.

Orchestration and tool lifecycle: Orchestrator runs the request/response loop, applies transformers, executes tool calls (parallel tasks vs sequential system tools), fires lifecycle events, handles retries, and streams lifecycle notifications.

Tool dispatch and execution: ToolRegistry validates and resolves tool targets (built-in, agent-delegated, MCP), enforces policies/timeouts, and delegates execution to ToolExecutor which normalizes paths, enforces read-before-edit, calls services, and produces ToolOperation/ToolOutput.

Compaction and truncation: Compactor and associated transformers compress long context sequences into summaries while preserving usage metrics and last reasoning, and truncation utilities produce bounded outputs with metadata and temp-file references.

Provider DTO mapping and model calls: dto modules (e.g., Anthropic request DTO) translate internal Context/messages/tool calls into provider request shapes, including schema enforcement and tool-call serialization.
Formatting and UI mapping: fmt modules convert tool inputs/outputs into UI-facing ChatResponseContent and ANSI/text payloads used by CLI/TUI and snapshot tests.
Git and auxiliary flows: GitApp wraps git diff/context capture, LLM-based commit message generation with retry semantics, and commit execution with proper attribution.

insights.title: Per-agent provider/model selection and config access
insights.problem: Flows assumed a single global active provider/model or used cached configs passed through many constructors, causing incorrect provider/model selection and brittle threading of config.
insights.root_cause: Global active-agent/model assumptions and inconsistent propagation of ForgeConfig through constructors instead of reading resolved config from infra/services at runtime.
insights.solution: Thread AgentId and per-agent preference through resolution paths and expose get_config() on EnvironmentInfra so code can re-read config where needed; ensure provider/model resolver is used by chat flows and Git/agent-executor paths.

insights.title: Masking vs returning conversation-not-found errors
insights.problem: Earlier code masked missing conversations via unwrap_or_default() and panicked later with expect(), preventing graceful error returns.
insights.root_cause: Use of defaults and panicking helpers instead of propagating Option/Result semantics at boundaries.
insights.solution: Return explicit errors when a conversation is not found and propagate Result-based error handling from ForgeApp.chat and callers.

insights.title: Losing or misreporting usage metrics during context compaction
insights.problem: Compaction replaced message sequences with summaries but discarded accumulated usage metrics or created reasoning duplication across successive compactions.
insights.root_cause: Compactor pipeline did not aggregate and transfer per-message usage/metrics into the summary message, and reasoning_details handling appended/duplicated state across compactions.
insights.solution: Accumulate usage metrics into the summary entry and inject the last non-empty reasoning_details into the preserved assistant message; ensure transformers do not cause exponential reasoning accumulation.

insights.title: Parallel file-read concurrency and detector configuration
insights.problem: File-change detection and other file-read paths used hard-coded or outdated concurrency defaults leading to inconsistent behavior when config changed.
insights.root_cause: Test fixtures and services did not expose or pass through a parallel_file_reads parameter; FileChangeDetector constructed without dynamic config.
insights.solution: Pass parallel_file_reads from dynamic config into detectors and test fixtures; ensure read_batch_utf8 caller semantics match the new streaming/individual-result behavior.

insights.title: AgentId and enum/variant signature changes
insights.problem: Callsites passed old String-based agent identifiers or matched deprecated tuple-style enum variants, causing compile mismatches after refactors.
insights.root_cause: API refactors that changed types (String -> AgentId) and enum shapes to struct-like variants were not propagated to all callers.
insights.solution: Update callsites to use AgentId types and structural pattern matches; ensure AgentExecutor, ToolRegistry, and Orchestrator invocations match the new signatures.

insights.title: Relative path ambiguity and read-before-edit policy
insights.problem: Tools accepted relative paths which could be interpreted inconsistently by services, and edits sometimes proceeded without enforcing a prior read.
insights.root_cause: Lack of consistent absolute path normalization and enforcing prior-read checks in tool execution pipeline.
insights.solution: Normalize relative paths against EnvironmentInfra.cwd for all tools and enforce read-before-edit/read-before-overwrite policies in ToolExecutor before delegating to services.

insights.title: Tool stdout interleaving and UI header lag
insights.problem: Tool executions wrote stdout/stderr while orchestrator UI header rendering lagged, producing interleaved output and confusing displays.
insights.root_cause: Orchestrator dispatched tool execution immediately after sending ToolCallStart without a notifier/hands-off acknowledgement to the UI layer.
insights.solution: Include a ToolCallStart notifier mechanism so the UI can render headers/acknowledge before tool stdout is emitted to the terminal.

insights.title: Internal IDs and file-range metadata leaking into serialized outputs
insights.problem: Operation serialization exported internal todo/file IDs and split range metadata across fields, leading to incorrect diffs/snapshots and exposing internal identifiers.
insights.root_cause: ToolOperation->Element/XML serialization included internal-only fields and legacy attributes rather than consolidated FileInfo metadata.
insights.solution: Stop exporting internal IDs; consolidate file-range and metadata into FileInfo structures used by outputs and snapshots.

insights.title: Use heavier provider models for simple command suggestions
insights.problem: Command suggestion flow used the active provider/model (potentially expensive) instead of a suggest-specific, cheaper model.
insights.root_cause: No dedicated suggest-config branch; command generator defaulted to global provider/model selection.
insights.solution: Use a dedicated suggest provider/model when configured to limit cost for simple shell command generation flows.

insights.title: Empty or transient failures for generated content (commit messages)
insights.problem: LLM-generated commit messages could be empty or transiently fail without retries, causing downstream errors or poor UX.
insights.root_cause: No retry/empty-result detection logic around model calls used to generate structured outputs like commit messages.
insights.solution: Treat empty model results as retryable and implement retry semantics with sensible limits for critical generation flows (e.g., commit_message generation).

key_files.why: High-level application orchestrator and entrypoint for chat flows; resolves per-agent provider/model, compaction and streams ChatResponse – central glue consumers will call or modify.
Core orchestrator control loop implementing lifecycle events, tool execution sequencing, transformers and streaming; changes here affect request execution semantics and concurrency.
Bridges high-level ToolCatalog calls to service implementations; normalizes paths, enforces read-before-edit, manages truncation/temp files and produces ToolOperation values.
Resolves and dispatches tools (built-in, agents, MCP), enforces restricted-mode policies and timeouts; acts as gatekeeper for permitted tool calls.
Defines the trait surface for filesystem, env, HTTP, MCP, command execution and user I/O used by the application layer; contract changes here ripple to runtime implementations and tests.

construct_relationships: ForgeApp orchestrates chat flows by composing AgentProviderResolver, Orchestrator, ToolRegistry, Compactor, and ApplyTunableParameters to build context, run turns, and persist/compact results.

Orchestrator invokes ToolRegistry to validate/route tool calls and then uses ToolExecutor (via Services + EnvironmentInfra) to actually execute tools; it fires lifecycle events (Start, ToolCallStart, ToolCallEnd, End) that hooks consume.

ToolExecutor depends on services.rs traits and EnvironmentInfra (infra.rs) for file reads/writes, shell, network, MCP operations; it creates ToolOperation values that operation.rs serializes into ToolOutput and updates conversation Metrics.
Compactor and transformers in the transform pipeline consume conversation Context/messages to produce ContextSummary and are invoked by hooks and ForgeApp when threshold/conditions are met.

AgentExecutor executes agents-as-tools by creating or reusing conversations and calling ForgeApp.chat; results are converted into ToolOutput for the caller and agent definitions are exposed to ToolRegistry.

dto/anthropic maps internal Context and tool calls into provider Request DTOs used by AgentService/Providers when Orchestrator calls the chat API; transformations here affect model behavior and tool-call encoding.
notable_relationships: This crate depends on domain types (forge_domain) for Conversation, ToolCatalog, FileInfo, Metrics; many downstream serialization/formatting and persistence behaviors rely on stable domain shapes.

Infra trait changes (get_config, read_batch_utf8 streaming semantics) require corresponding updates in runtime infra implementations and test doubles across the workspace (tests in orch_spec and other crates).
Formatting logic here (fmt/*) feeds CLI/TUI and snapshot tests; changes will affect user-facing output and existing insta snapshots in the test suite.
Provider DTO changes (dto/*) directly affect provider clients (forge_services and external proxies) and billing/usage extraction, so modifications can impact network integration and cost accounting.

Tool execution and truncation behavior create temp files and metrics that other subsystems (persistence, telemetry, workspace indexer) expect; altering truncation or temp-file paths affects downstream consumers and tests.
recommendations: When editing APIs or signatures (AgentId, DTO shapes, infra traits), update all callsites (AgentExecutor, ToolRegistry, Orchestrator, tests) and the runtime infra/service implementations that implement the traits.
Preserve and correctly transfer usage metrics and reasoning_details across compaction; run compaction-related unit tests and transformers pipeline tests after changes.
Respect path normalization and read-before-edit enforcement in ToolExecutor; ensure tests and services expect absolute normalized paths.

Be mindful of concurrency and notification ordering: maintain ToolCallStart notifier semantics to prevent stdout interleaving and handle parallel file-read configuration (parallel_file_reads) consistently in detectors and tests.

Update test fixtures when changing DTOs/ReadOutput shapes (FileInfo migration) and ensure orch_spec fixtures expose new fields (parallel_file_reads); run orchestration integration tests and snapshot tests (cargo test --workspace, cargo insta test --accept where appropriate).
Do not reintroduce exposure of internal IDs in serialized outputs; use FileInfo and consolidated metadata for tool outputs and snapshots.

When changing provider/request serialization or retry semantics, run end-to-end provider integration tests and verify commit-generation and other structured-output flows to avoid empty or non-deterministic results.
Keep CI constraints in mind (warnings-as-errors): ensure code is warning-free and run cargo clippy and cargo check across the workspace before submitting changes.

crates/forge_main/src.description: This crate implements the forge CLI/TUI binary: it defines the command surface, bootstraps runtime and platform quirks, and hosts the interactive UI loop, prompt/editor/integration plumbing, streaming output, and a number of user-facing utilities (info/porcelain display, sandbox worktrees, OAuth loopback). It is the entrypoint and orchestration layer that wires configuration, provider/model resolution, conversation selection, completions, and display together — making it critical when modifying behavior visible to end users or integrating with other crates (config, repo, services, fs).
key_behaviors: Startup and initialization: parse CLI, detect piped input, enable platform terminal features (Windows VT), load ForgeConfig, thread CLI overrides into API/ForgeAPI construction, and choose sandbox/worktree.

Interactive loop orchestration: initialize UI state, present a stylized prompt, read user input via reedline, complete and parse slash commands, run subcommands or agent conversation flows, and persist active agent/conversation state.

Editor/completion integration: provide fuzzy file/path completions, slash-command completions, and map reedline signals into application-level events (ReadResult) while allowing prefilled buffers and history.
Streaming output coordination: render markdown/porcelain Info to the terminal while managing a shared spinner so streaming output and spinner text do not interleave or corrupt terminal output.

Provider/model and OAuth flows: orchestrate interactive provider activation and optional model selection, and handle browser-based OAuth flows via a localhost callback listener with timeout/shutdown behavior.

insights.title: CLI flags are authoritative runtime overrides and must be threaded into API initialization
insights.problem: Provider/model overrides provided on the CLI could be applied inconsistently, leading to mismatched in-memory vs persisted state or prompting during scripted runs.
insights.root_cause: Multiple override entrypoints existed (CLI flags and deprecated env vars) and API/UI initialization paths were not uniformly receiving the runtime override values.
insights.solution: Treat clap-defined top-level flags as the single source of runtime overrides and ensure they are propagated into ForgeAPI/UI construction paths so interactive and one-shot flows behave identically; also be aware of deprecation of env-var overrides when modifying flag handling.

insights.title: Provider activation must be atomic with optional model selection
insights.problem: Completing provider credential flows without performing model selection atomically produced transient state mismatches where the UI showed one model/provider but persisted config differed.
insights.root_cause: Provider activation flow and model write were separate operations with potential interruption between them.
insights.solution: Preserve the invariant that provider activation and model selection/persistence occur together (or that the system records an explicit pending state) — code touching activation/persistence must respect this atomicity to avoid inconsistent persisted configuration.

insights.title: Background cache hydration can race with short-lived commands and process exit
insights.problem: Spawning background hydration tasks (e.g., hydrate_caches on UI on_new) caused panics or 'polled after completion' join-handle issues when the process exited quickly (info or one-shot commands).
insights.root_cause: Background tasks were started during short-lived command handlers without lifecycle tying or selective initialization, allowing them to outlive the synchronous flow and race with shutdown.
insights.solution: When modifying code that spawns background tasks from UI initialization or command handlers, ensure tasks are started conditionally for interactive/long-running sessions or have proper cancellation/await/shutdown handling so short-lived flows don't race on exit.

insights.title: Info/Porcelain formatting must handle multibyte characters and maintain alignment
insights.problem: Truncation used byte lengths leading to broken multibyte output and Info sections previously had inconsistent key alignment across sections.
insights.root_cause: String operations used byte-based slicing and prior display logic was ad-hoc across multiple UI locations.
insights.solution: Use character-aware (grapheme/char count) truncation when preparing porcelaine output and maintain fixed-width key columns per section; code altering Info-to-Porcelain or display formatting must preserve these behaviors to avoid breaking CLI/porcelain output consumers.

insights.title: Windows VT/ANSI handling and panic reporting are platform-sensitive
insights.problem: ANSI escape sequences appeared raw on Windows consoles when VT processing wasn't enabled on STDOUT, and some panic reporting required special setup.
insights.root_cause: Platform APIs differ and enabling VT on one handle (e.g., CONOUT$) does not necessarily apply to STD_OUTPUT_HANDLE; runtime initialization must explicitly enable VT on the correct handle and install panic hooks.
insights.solution: Retain explicit platform initialization steps (enable VT processing on STD_OUTPUT_HANDLE and set panic hooks) when editing startup logic; changes to terminal behavior must be tested on Windows configurations to avoid degraded output.

key_files.why: Entrypoint: parses CLI, detects piped input, performs platform setup (VT and panic hooks), loads config, and constructs the UI/ForgeAPI — changes here alter global startup behavior and flag propagation.

Central interactive UI orchestration: manages interactive loop, provider/model resolution delegation, background cache hydration, spinner and streaming coordination — modifications impact session lifecycle and asynchronous background work.

Clap-based authoritative definition of the CLI surface: declared flags, subcommands, and runtime override semantics used by the binary and zsh plugin — keep this the single source of truth for command shapes.
Slash/command registration and parser: ForgeCommandManager and SlashCommand drive completions, parsing, and command resolution used by editor/completer and the UI.
Public crate exports and global tracker initialization: changes here affect what other workspace crates consume from forge_main (e.g., exported types) and global side effects.

construct_relationships: main.rs reads Cli (cli.rs) and ForgeConfig, then initializes ForgeAPI and constructs UI (ui.rs) with CLI-provided runtime overrides
UI (ui.rs) drives the interactive loop using Console/Input (input.rs) which uses ForgeEditor (editor.rs) and ForgePrompt (prompt.rs) to render the prompt and read user input
ForgeCommandManager (model.rs) is the authoritative source for slash commands and is queried by completions (completer/*) and by the Console/parser to convert input into SlashCommand values

StreamingWriter/StreamDirectWriter (stream_renderer.rs) coordinate with SharedSpinner to pause/resume spinner output while rendering markdown from the UI, ensuring streams and spinner do not interleave

Info (info.rs) converts domain objects into structured sections consumed by Porcelain (porcelain.rs) for tabular/machine-friendly output; Porcelain enforces character-aware truncation used by CLI output
notable_relationships: This crate is the CLI/TUI entrypoint that depends on and invokes other workspace crates: forge_config (config loading), forge_repo (conversations), forge_services/providers (provider activation & APIs), forge_fs/forge_walker (path completions and sandboxing), and forge_embed/remote workspace servers (indexing) — changes here can affect cross-crate initialization and API expectations.
The zsh submodule (src/zsh) consumes the clap completions and CLI surface defined in cli.rs; altering command names or flags impacts shell-plugin generation and users' installed zsh integrations.
OAuth callback (oauth_callback.rs) is used by provider login flows: network, timeouts, and loopback port handling here affect browser-based auth flows across providers.
Porcelain/Info output is consumed by scripts and tooling: modifications to display format or truncation behavior can break machine-readable consumers and the zsh prompt's parsing of status strings.
recommendations: When changing CLI shapes, treat cli.rs as the authoritative source-of-truth and verify zsh plugin generation and shell completions remain consistent with any rename/flag changes.
Thread CLI runtime overrides (provider, model, agent flags) through main.rs into ForgeAPI and UI construction paths to maintain consistent in-memory and persisted state.

Avoid spawning unguarded background tasks from short-lived command handlers; if background tasks are required, ensure they are conditionally started for interactive sessions or have proper cancellation/await semantics to prevent shutdown races.

Run crate tests and workspace CI checks after edits: cargo test (crate), cargo test --workspace where relevant, and ensure code compiles with warnings-as-errors (CI uses RUSTFLAGS -Dwarnings). Also run platform-specific checks for Windows ANSI VT behavior if startup changes are made.

Preserve character-aware truncation/alignment behavior in Porcelain/Info conversions (support multibyte/Unicode) because CLI output is consumed by scripts and users; validate with examples containing multibyte characters.

When modifying OAuth, provider activation, or persistence logic, ensure atomicity between credential activation and model selection is preserved and verify the OAuth localhost callback timeouts/shutdown behaviors remain correct.

crates/forge_services/src.description: This crate implements the application-level service layer that sits between the Forge application logic (forge_app) and lower-level infrastructure. It composes concrete services (provider, auth, workspace/context, conversation, policy, discovery, attachments, command loading, agent registry, etc.), adapts infra traits to domain APIs, and enforces cross-cutting invariants (hashing, discovery/filtering, persistence, credential refresh) that the rest of the system relies on.
key_behaviors: Compose a concrete Services container (ForgeServices) that implements forge_app::Services and forwards environment/config calls to an underlying infra implementation.
Adapt infra traits (file I/O, command runner, walker, provider repository, credential store) into domain services used by agents and application code.
Discover and filter workspace files (git ls-files with walker fallback), stream and coordinate workspace sync work to a remote indexer via a sync engine, and report progress events.
Prepare attachments and file payloads for agents by resolving paths against environment cwd, reading ranges or raw bytes, and producing stable content-hashes used by external detectors.
Manage provider authentication flows and credential lifecycle (init, complete, refresh) while persisting credentials via repository/infra.
Load and cache configuration-like artifacts (commands, AGENTS.md instructions, policies, agents) with precedence rules and session vs persistent scope distinctions.

insights.title: App-level commit/suggest configs must be persisted through infra
insights.problem: AppConfig previously lacked persistent storage for commit/suggest model configs, leading to ephemeral or inconsistent defaults.
insights.root_cause: App-level getters/putters were not mapped to on-disk environment config and lacked a service adapter that writes via infra.update_environment.
insights.solution: AppConfig service implementation funnels reads from the infra's on-disk config and applies writes by forwarding ConfigOperation to infra.update_environment so commit/suggest configs are durable and consistent.

insights.title: Attachment content hash must match raw file hash
insights.problem: Attachment service produced hashes derived from range/line-numbered content leading to false external-change warnings when compared to raw-disk hashes.
insights.root_cause: Line-range extraction/transformation changed the bytes used for hashing, while external-change detector hashed raw file contents on disk.
insights.solution: Attachment preparation uses the FileInfo returned from infra.range_read_utf8 (which contains the full-file hash) or otherwise computes and stores the raw full-file content hash to avoid mismatch with external-change detectors.

insights.title: Separate discovery from reading and propagate errors from git
insights.problem: Earlier sync combined discovery, reading batches, and swallowed git errors, making failures hard to diagnose and recovery fragile.
insights.root_cause: Monolithic sync function duplicated logic and suppressed git ls-files errors (returned None) and mixed discovery with reading/upload responsibilities.
insights.solution: Context engine delegates heavy lifting to a WorkspaceSyncEngine, separates discovery and reading pipelines, returns real git ls-files errors, and provides a walker fallback when git fails or returns empty results.

insights.title: Active agent selection should be session-scoped
insights.problem: Setting the active agent previously updated persistent app config, causing global/surprising disk writes for what is a session preference.
insights.root_cause: Active-agent state was stored in persistent AppConfig rather than an in-memory session registry.
insights.solution: Agent registry switched to in-memory session state (cached in DashMap with RwLocks) and loads from AgentRepository for defaults without persisting session-level selections to disk.

insights.title: Symlinks and non-allowed extensions must be excluded from indexing
insights.problem: Symlinked/dangling links and disallowed file extensions were previously included in discovery results leading to indexing undesirable targets.
insights.root_cause: Discovery/filtering pipeline initially resolved relative paths and followed symlinks without explicit exclusion rules.
insights.solution: filter_and_resolve excludes symlink paths and filters by ALLOWED_EXTENSIONS; file discovery tries git ls-files then falls back to walker and ensures symlinks/dangling links are removed.

insights.title: Special-case markers (e.g., Google ADC) must be recognized across provider variants
insights.problem: Google ADC marker handling only recognized a single provider enum (VERTEX_AI), causing misclassification for other Vertex-like providers and incorrect auth flow choices.
insights.root_cause: Auth logic had a narrow match for the provider variant and did not generalize detection to other Vertex-like provider enums.
insights.solution: Provider auth service treats Google ADC markers consistently across Vertex-like providers (recognize marker across relevant provider variants) so correct auth flows (ADC vs OAuth/device-code) are chosen and refreshed.

insights.title: Built-in and custom commands must have deterministic precedence
insights.problem: Built-in commands were not always loaded from embedded markdown and precedence between built-in and custom command definitions was ambiguous.
insights.root_cause: Init path for built-in commands and the order of discovery for global/local custom commands lacked a clear, enforced ordering.
insights.solution: Command loader initializes built-in commands via init_default and resolves name conflicts by keeping the last occurrence with CWD having highest precedence; built-in commands are explicitly loaded from embedded assets.

key_files.why: Service container/entry point that composes concrete service implementations and implements forge_app::Services and EnvironmentInfra — changes here affect all service wiring and runtime composition.
Workspace/context service that orchestrates discovery, sync, and search and delegates to WorkspaceSyncEngine — central to indexing and remote sync flows.
Attachment preparation and hashing logic used by chat/agent code — must preserve hash invariants and I/O semantics relied on by external detectors.
Directory-level file discovery/filtering, symlink and extension rules, and the git-fallback pipeline — core to which files are indexed and synced.
Provider authentication and credential lifecycle (init/complete/refresh) — changes affect provider credential persistence and authentication flows across providers.

construct_relationships: ForgeServices composes ForgeProviderService, ForgeAuthService, ForgeWorkspaceService (context engine), ForgeConversationService, ForgeAgentRegistryService, ForgePolicyService and exposes them via the forge_app::Services trait.

ForgeWorkspaceService (context_engine) delegates discovery to FileDiscovery implementations (FdDefault) which in turn route to FsGit (fd_git) or FdWalker (fd_walker); discovery results feed WorkspaceSyncEngine for sync/upload.

AttachmentService (ForgeChatRequest) uses EnvironmentInfra/infra.range_read_utf8 and FileReaderInfra to read files and relies on returned FileInfo.full_file_hash to populate Attachment.content_hash used by external-change detectors.

ProviderService renders provider URL/model templates, then delegates chat/model listing calls to ProviderRepository; ProviderAuthService uses StrategyFactory + ProviderRepository to initialize/complete auth and persist credentials via repository/infra.

CommandLoaderService reads embedded built-ins and discovers custom commands from global/local directories (via CommandInfra/File IO), resolving conflicts with defined precedence used by runtime command lookup.
notable_relationships: This crate depends on forge_config/EnvironmentInfra and ProviderRepository abstractions; changes here assume stability of infra traits defined elsewhere (e.g., range_read_utf8, get_environment, update_environment, CommandInfra, WalkerInfra).

Workspace sync/search flows interface with external workspace indexing server (remote indexer) and therefore changes can affect network uploads and remote state; FORGE_WORKSPACE_SERVER_URL and credentials are exercised here.

Attachment hashing semantics must remain consistent with external-change detection implemented in other crates (e.g., forge_fs or workspace snapshotting); mismatches create false external-modified warnings and destabilize undo/snapshot flows in tool_services.
PolicyService persistence interacts with user file system (policy YAML); changes may affect interactive prompts that other services expect to receive before performing operations.

AgentRegistry and ConversationService interact with repository persistence (AgentRepository, ConversationRepository) in forge_repo; altering caching or persistence behavior impacts user-visible state (active agent selection, saved conversations).
recommendations: Preserve public trait implementations and method signatures for forge_app::Services and EnvironmentInfra; altering them requires coordinated changes across callers in other crates.

When modifying attachment/file-read code, keep content-hash computation aligned with infra-provided full-file hashes (use FileInfo.full_file_hash) to avoid breaking external-change detection and snapshot/undo semantics in tool_services.

If changing discovery or filtering logic, ensure git ls-files errors are surfaced (not swallowed) and walker fallback behavior is preserved; re-run tests that cover discover_sync_file_paths, symlink exclusions, and git fallback paths.

Any changes to provider auth or credential storage must maintain credential refresh semantics and marker handling (Google ADC/Vertex-like providers); updating recognition or persistence requires exercising auth flows and refresh tests.

Respect session vs persistent boundaries: do not move session-scoped state (active agent selection) back into persistent app config without updating consumers; verify concurrency correctness around the agent cache (DashMap + RwLocks).

Run the crate's unit tests (cargo test -p forge_services) and relevant workspace tests (workspace sync, provider auth) after edits; ensure CI constraints (warnings as errors) are satisfied and update or add tests for behavior changes in discovery, attachment hashing, command loading, and policy prompts.

crates/forge_domain/src.description: This crate is the canonical domain model for Forge: it defines typed, serializable representations of agents, conversations, messages, tools, providers, auth flows, workspace/indexing nodes, compaction policies/algorithms, and repository interfaces. It centralizes contracts and wire formats that the rest of the workspace (CLI, services, persistence, indexer, UI) depend on, so backwards- and cross-crate compatibility here is critical to runtime behavior and on-disk/network interchange.
key_behaviors: Provide stable, strongly-typed domain types and serde wire formats consumed across the workspace (conversations, messages, providers, credentials, tools, index nodes).
Expose environment- and session-level helpers that map runtime state to canonical paths for logs, snapshots, credentials, agents and skills.
Model authentication flows and credential containers for interactive provider login and token lifecycle management.
Define the canonical tool contract (ToolCatalog and per-tool input/output schemas) plus name normalization used to resolve LLM-produced tool calls.
Decide and execute context compaction: trigger logic, eviction-range selection that preserves tool-call atomicity, and generation of compact summary blocks used by persistence and prompting.

insights.title: Centralize credentials / runtime path helpers
insights.problem: Credentials file paths and other runtime artifact locations were assembled at multiple call sites, causing duplication and fragile tests.
insights.root_cause: Lack of a single, testable Environment helper that returns canonical paths relative to base_path / cwd / home.
insights.solution: Provide Environment.credentials_path and other path helpers in env.rs and ensure call sites consume these helpers instead of computing paths inline.

insights.title: Template provider URL semantics
insights.problem: Template providers with a fixed url but no url_params returned None for url(), hiding a usable endpoint.
insights.root_cause: url() logic assumed templated providers always required parameters; fixed template strings with empty params were treated as absent.
insights.solution: Return the hardcoded URL for template providers when present even if url_params is empty, so callers can detect usable endpoints.

insights.title: Tool name normalization
insights.problem: LLM-produced tool names varied in casing/whitespace and caused catalog lookups to fail.
insights.root_cause: ToolCatalog lookup compared names without robust normalization and lacked a fast alias/lookup map.
insights.solution: Normalize tool names (trim, case-insensitive) and maintain a lookup map to resolve LLM outputs to canonical ToolCatalog variants.

insights.title: File content identity in attachments
insights.problem: Attachment payloads lacked content hashes and repeated fields for line ranges, causing duplication and difficulty validating content identity.
insights.root_cause: AttachmentContent::FileContent carried separate metadata fields dispersed across code paths; raw content hash was not captured.
insights.solution: Add content_hash to FileContent and consolidate file-range metadata into FileInfo to provide a single canonical representation for attachments.

insights.title: Duplicate reasoning blocks and model-specific reasoning reuse
insights.problem: Raw reasoning text and structured reasoning_details were both appended, producing duplicate thinking blocks and null signatures that some providers reject; reasoning was reused across model changes incorrectly.
insights.root_cause: append_message and transformers lacked checks to avoid converting raw reasoning into structured reasoning when reasoning_details already existed; reasoning signatures were treated as model-agnostic.
insights.solution: Avoid duplicating reasoning when structured reasoning_details exist, and associate reasoning signatures with model identity so reasoning isn't inappropriately reused after model changes.

insights.title: Tool call lifecycle needs notifier coordination
insights.problem: ToolCall lifecycle events did not carry a notifier necessary for UI/executor coordination, limiting the ability to coordinate streaming execution.
insights.root_cause: ToolCallStart previously embedded full ToolCall but lacked a lightweight notifier reference used by orchestrator/UI.
insights.solution: Ensure tool-call start events include a notifier reference and normalize enum shapes so downstream orchestrators can subscribe/coordinate on tool execution lifecycle events.

insights.title: Compaction trigger and eviction semantics
insights.problem: Compaction logic must avoid splitting tool-call atomicity and must respect configured thresholds; mis-selection can corrupt conversation state or produce invalid summaries.
insights.root_cause: EvictionRange computation without preserving assistant boundaries or tool-call/result atomic sequences led to partial tool runs being evicted.
insights.solution: Use compaction strategies that compute eviction spans while preserving tool-call atomicity and assistant boundaries and validate eviction percentage inputs during config deserialization.

key_files.why: Crate entry point and public re-exports; changes here affect all consumers of the domain API and module visibility.
Centralized Environment and session config helpers that produce canonical runtime paths (credentials, logs, snapshots) relied upon by other crates.
Defines ToolCatalog, per-tool schemas, and normalization logic; this is the contract between LLM tool calls and executors.
Core message/context types, token estimation, and reasoning-preservation behavior used by compaction, transformers, provider request construction and storage.
Provider identity, model/source abstraction, and URL/auth helpers consumed by services and provider login flows; changes impact provider configuration and endpoint resolution.

construct_relationships: Agent (agent.rs) maps to ToolDefinition and references ToolCatalog entries (tools/catalog.rs) to expose configured tools for runtime agents.

Context and ContextMessage (context.rs) are inputs to compaction logic (compact/*) which computes eviction ranges via CompactionStrategy while preserving tool-call atomicity defined by ToolCatalog semantics.
ChatResponse and tool-call lifecycle events (chat_response.rs) coordinate with ToolCatalog/tool execution via notifier references so UI and executors can subscribe to tool start/end events.

Provider types (provider.rs) and credential containers (auth/credentials.rs) are consumed by repository traits (repo.rs) for credential management and by services that stream chat completions (ChatRepository).

Attachment parsing (attachment.rs) produces Attachment/AttachmentContent consumed by transformers (transformer/*) and by message serialization logic in context.rs to normalize content and include content_hashes.
notable_relationships: forge_domain is the single authoritative API surface exported to other crates (CLI, services, repo, UI); any serde, type or behavior changes here can require coordinated downstream updates and migration.

Repository traits (repo.rs) define persistence contracts; altering domain wire formats (serde) affects on-disk conversation snapshots, provider credential migrations, and any external API exchanges (workspace/indexer).

Compaction and reasoning changes alter prompts sent to external model providers (services/providers); reasoning signature or message shape changes can cause provider rejections or semantic drift in model behavior.
Tool schema/name normalization affects executors and LLM-to-tool resolution across runtime components; mismatches can break tool invocation from agent outputs.

Authentication modeling changes impact interactive login flows and stored credential formats used by provider login commands and provider clients; backwards compatibility and migration are necessary for existing stored credentials.
recommendations: Preserve serde stability and compatibility: any renames or structural changes to serializable domain types (conversations, messages, credentials, tools, nodes) require migration strategies and tests that validate round-trip persistence.
When editing path or environment helpers (env.rs), ensure all callers use the centralized helpers rather than reconstructing paths; update unit tests referencing expected paths.
Validate provider.url semantics carefully: callers expect a usable URL for template providers with fixed strings even when url_params is empty.
Respect tool name normalization expectations: maintain trimming/case-insensitive lookup and update any alias maps used in catalog resolution if schema changes.
Avoid reasoning duplication and ensure reasoning signatures remain model-aware: changing context/message shaping must account for downstream transformers and provider acceptance rules.

Run the crate's unit tests and workspace integration tests after changes (cargo test for the crate and relevant workspace tests); ensure RUSTFLAGS -D warnings compatibility (CI treats warnings as errors).

Coordinate changes that affect repository traits (repo.rs) with persistence providers and ensure conversation snapshots, provider credential storage, and indexer payloads remain consistent or are migrated.

If modifying tool or attachment schemas, verify downstream executors, transformers, and UI consumers (streaming, tool-call events) still interoperate; update notifier/event shapes used by ChatResponse/tool lifecycle.

crates/forge_infra/src.description: This crate provides concrete infrastructure implementations used across the workspace: filesystem access, HTTP/gRPC clients, command execution, environment/config management, MCP connectivity, and synchronized console I/O. It centralizes the low-level adapters that higher-level application code depends on via infra traits, so changes here directly affect many crates and runtime behaviors (I/O, network, auth, and config persistence).
key_behaviors: Construct and expose a composable ForgeInfra that implements infra traits (file IO, directory reading, HTTP, gRPC, command execution, user prompts, environment access, auth, and console output).
Read/write and stream filesystem content with batched and concurrent readers while enforcing concurrency caps and binary/ignore filtering.
Manage runtime environment and config: produce domain Environment from runtime inputs, cache ForgeConfig in-memory, apply mutation operations atomically and invalidate cache after writes.

Perform HTTP operations (including eventsource streaming) with header sanitization and optional debug request persistence, and create lazily-initialized, cached gRPC channels with hydrate/reset support.
Connect to MCP servers over multiple transports (stdio/HTTP/SSE), resolve templated HTTP headers, and translate raw responses into domain ToolOutput with retry logic.

insights.title: Cached ForgeConfig must be invalidated and re-read after writes
insights.problem: Config was read once and cached with no clear re-read path, causing consumers to observe stale in-memory config after mutations.
insights.root_cause: No centralized apply-and-invalidate path for config mutations; the cache was not consistently invalidated after persisting changes.
insights.solution: Provide a single infra entry (apply_config_op) that persists mutations and invalidates cached_config so subsequent reads rehydrate from disk/overrides. Respect FORGE_CONFIG env var as base_path override when constructing Environment.

insights.title: Batched file reads must surface per-file errors
insights.problem: Previous batched reads returned a single Result for a batch, obscuring which file failed and losing per-file error context.
insights.root_cause: Batch API aggregated results rather than yielding per-file outcomes.
insights.solution: Change batch reader to stream per-file path/result pairs so callers can handle file-specific errors; ensure callers are updated to consume per-file results.

insights.title: Directory reads require concurrency caps to avoid EMFILE
insights.problem: Unbounded parallel reads used join_all and opened too many file descriptors in large workspaces.
insights.root_cause: Parallel file reads lacked a concurrency limiter; directory reader construction did not accept a parallelism parameter that higher-level infra can tune.
insights.solution: Introduce a concurrency cap for directory file reading and thread that parameter through ForgeInfra construction (parallel_file_reads) so the directory reader enforces limits and prevents EMFILE.

insights.title: Request debugging must sanitize headers and append streamed bodies
insights.problem: Ad-hoc header sanitization was inconsistent across providers and streaming request body logging overwrote earlier chunks, truncating debug logs.
insights.root_cause: Multiple implementations duplicated header sanitization lists and debug write logic; streaming bodies were written in a way that replaced earlier content.
