insights.solution: Centralize sanitize_headers with an expanded sensitive-header set and append debug body chunks to configured debug files. Ensure debug request persistence is optional and controlled by config.

insights.title: ForgeInfra and individual services must match updated trait signatures
insights.problem: Changes to infra trait signatures (e.g., read_batch_utf8 streaming return type, directory reader concurrency parameter) cause ForgeInfra construction and callers to mismatch function signatures.
insights.root_cause: Evolving interfaces in infra traits were not consistently propagated to the aggregator and its construction site.
insights.solution: Keep ForgeInfra in sync with trait signatures: adopt new read_batch_utf8 streaming semantics, accept and pass parallelism/concurrency params to directory reader, and update any dependent constructors and tests accordingly.

insights.title: gRPC channel creation should be lazy, cached, and re-hydratable
insights.problem: Multiple clients creating independent channels would duplicate resources and miss centralized TLS/concurrency configuration.
insights.root_cause: Lack of a shared, lazily-initialized channel wrapper that supports reset/hydrate semantics.
insights.solution: Provide a thread-safe ForgeGrpcClient that creates and caches a tonic Channel on first use, configures TLS for https endpoints, exposes concurrency settings, and allows forcing a reconnect via hydrate/reset.

key_files.why: Central aggregator that composes all infra implementations and is the primary entrypoint other crates rely on via infra traits.
Constructs domain Environment, manages cached ForgeConfig, and provides the apply_config_op path used for config persistence and cache invalidation.
Implements FileReaderInfra and the batched streaming reader whose signature changed to per-file results; callers must align with this behavior.
Directory reader enforces ignore rules and concurrency caps; it must receive and honor parallelism parameters to avoid descriptor exhaustion.
Centralized HTTP infra: header sanitization, TLS config, eventsource streaming, and debug request persistence — changes affect all external provider interactions.

construct_relationships: ForgeInfra composes implementations: FileReaderInfra (fs_read), FileWriterInfra (fs_write), FileDirectoryInfra (fs_create_dirs), FileRemoverInfra (fs_remove), FileInfoInfra (fs_meta), DirectoryReader (fs_read_dir), Command executor (executor), HttpInfra (http), Grpc client (grpc), UserInfra (inquire), KVStore (kv_storage), McpServerInfra (mcp_server).
ForgeEnvironmentInfra (env.rs) is consumed by ForgeInfra to satisfy EnvironmentInfra trait and provides cached_config and apply_config_op used by higher layers.
ForgeFileReadService yields a Stream of per-file results consumed by ForgeDirectoryReaderService which enforces concurrency caps and filters via walker/ignore rules.

Executor (executor.rs) writes command stdout/stderr to StdConsoleWriter (console.rs) and serializes execution via an internal mutex; Console writer must remain thread-safe to avoid interleaved output.
ForgeHttpInfra is used by ForgeMcpClient for HTTP/SSE MCP transports; ForgeMcpClient relies on header templating and debug request persistence provided by http.rs.
notable_relationships: This crate is broadly depended on by higher-level crates (forge_main, forge_repo, forge_services, forge_embed, etc.) — API and trait signature changes here ripple across the workspace.
Trait definitions live in domain crates (forge_domain / forge_app); infra implementations must remain compatible with those traits and consumers in other crates.
Configuration behavior (FORGE_CONFIG base path, cached ForgeConfig) impacts application startup, environment construction, provider auth flows, and file paths used by other crates.
HTTP and MCP behaviors affect external provider integrations and indexing servers (FORGE_WORKSPACE_SERVER_URL), with privacy implications when debug_requests are enabled.
CI expectations (zero warnings, tests) mean changes here must preserve compilation without warnings and pass crate/unit tests, as workspace builds treat warnings as errors in CI.
recommendations: When editing infra implementations, preserve trait method signatures or update all dependent call sites (ForgeInfra::new, callers in other crates) to prevent mismatches.
Respect cached_config semantics: any code that writes config must call apply_config_op or explicitly invalidate the cache so subsequent reads rehydrate persisted values.

If adjusting file-read batching or directory reader concurrency, update consumers to handle per-file Result semantics and ensure the parallel_file_reads parameter is threaded through ForgeInfra construction.

When modifying HTTP behavior, keep header sanitization centralized and maintain appending debug request body behavior (only when debug_paths configured); ensure sensitive headers remain excluded from logs.
Keep gRPC client behavior lazy and re-hydratable; maintain TLS configuration for https endpoints and ensure channel resets propagate safely to consumers.

Run this crate's unit tests and the workspace test suite after changes (cargo test --workspace) and ensure CI expectations (no warnings) are met. Also run any infra-specific integration tests that exercise MCP/HTTP/FS behaviors.

crates/forge_config/src.description: This crate defines the application's canonical, strongly-typed configuration surface (ForgeConfig) and the supporting primitives used to read, validate, serialize and migrate user configuration. It centralizes layered config resolution (embedded defaults, legacy files, user TOML, environment and .env), typed convenience newtypes for clean serialization/validation (Decimal, Percentage), and helpers for persisting configuration with editor schema metadata. These types are consumed broadly across the workspace and therefore shape runtime behavior, provider selection, and editor/user-visible config files.
key_behaviors: Layered configuration resolution: build a ForgeConfig by merging embedded defaults, legacy JSON, global user files, provided TOML blobs, and FORGE_* environment variables, while loading .env files once per process.
Typed config model + schema: expose ForgeConfig and many nested config types with serde and schemars annotations to produce stable JSON schema and editor integrations.
Clean numeric serialization and validation: use Decimal and Percentage newtypes to ensure two-decimal TOML output and range-validated percentage fields.
Migration and backward-compatibility: detect and convert legacy config formats into the current ForgeConfig shape, avoiding unintended overwrites of defaults.
Persistence with editor hints: serialize configs to TOML files and prepend a $schema pointer so editors can validate/autocomplete user configs.

insights.title: Legacy config can unintentionally overwrite defaults
insights.problem: Legacy JSON/TOML layers sometimes contained partial fields which would overwrite sensible embedded defaults when merged.
insights.root_cause: Merging configuration layers treated present legacy keys as authoritative even when they didn't represent a full configuration shape.
insights.solution: Make many ForgeConfig fields optional and have the reader centralize defaults so legacy migrations and merges do not clobber defaults inadvertently.

insights.title: Noisy float serialization degrades readability and tests
insights.problem: Direct f32/f64 serialization produced long noisy bit-patterns in TOML output (e.g., 0.10000000149), complicating human inspection and tests.
insights.root_cause: toml_edit and serde default float formatting exposes binary float artifacts.
insights.solution: Introduce a Decimal newtype that serializes to two decimal places and use it for user-facing numeric config fields.

insights.title: Percentage fields require bounded validation and stable formatting
insights.problem: Unconstrained float percentages could be out of [0,1] range and serialized noisily.
insights.root_cause: Previously percentage fields were raw floats without runtime validation or specialized formatting.
insights.solution: Wrap percentages in a Percentage newtype built on Decimal with construction-time validation and two-decimal serialization.

insights.title: Missing $schema prevents editor validation/autocomplete
insights.problem: Generated TOML config files lacked a $schema metadata pointer, so editors could not provide validation or completion for forge.yaml.
insights.root_cause: Writer logic did not inject schema hints into persisted files.
insights.solution: When writing configs, prepend a $schema pointer to the TOML output so editors can use the included JSON schema for validation and autocomplete.

insights.title: .env files should be loaded once and deterministically
insights.problem: Dotenv loading could run multiple times or at unpredictable places in the process lifecycle.
insights.root_cause: Environment-loading side effects were not centralized.
insights.solution: Centralize .env loading in the ConfigReader and ensure it runs once per process (LOAD_DOT_ENV flag) while discovering working-directory chain files.

key_files.why: Defines the top-level ForgeConfig structure, provider types, and read/write helpers that represent the canonical config surface used across the application.
Implements layered config resolution (defaults, legacy, files, env/.env) and is the entrypoint for how effective configuration is constructed at runtime.
Serializes and persists ForgeConfig to disk and injects $schema metadata used by editors — changes here affect on-disk format and tooling integrations.
Provides the Decimal newtype used to ensure consistent two-decimal serialization for user-visible numeric config values.
Implements Percentage validation and formatting — ensures percentage config fields are bounded and serialized predictably.

construct_relationships: ConfigReader (reader.rs) composes ForgeConfig (config.rs) by layering embedded defaults, LegacyConfig (legacy.rs), user TOML, and environment variables; it also triggers .env loading once per process.

LegacyConfig (legacy.rs) converts legacy JSON shapes into partial ForgeConfig representations which are merged by ConfigReader; conversion intentionally omits fields not represented to avoid overwriting defaults.
ConfigWriter (writer.rs) consumes ForgeConfig and serializes it to TOML, injecting a $schema pointer for editor tooling; decimal/percentage types influence the serialized numeric representation.

Decimal (decimal.rs) is used by Percentage (percentage.rs) and by numeric fields across Compact (compact.rs), Reasoning (reasoning.rs), HttpConfig (http.rs), and RetryConfig (retry.rs) to ensure consistent formatting.
Error (error.rs) is the unified error type used by reader, writer, and legacy modules to present configuration parsing/IO/serialization errors to callers.
ModelConfig and provider identifiers (model.rs and types in config.rs) are referenced by consumer crates to select providers/models at runtime; these are exported via lib.rs.
notable_relationships: Other workspace crates (e.g., forge_main, forge_services) depend on these types for runtime behavior — changing shapes, serde tags, or schema will affect cross-crate compatibility and persisted config files consumed by users.
On-disk format stability matters: writer.rs injects $schema and Decimal/Percentage control TOML appearance — editors and user workflows rely on predictable formatting.
Legacy migration (legacy.rs) interacts with user home files and must avoid overwriting embedded defaults; updates here affect users upgrading from older versions.
.env loading behavior from reader.rs has global process effects and interacts with other crates that read environment variables; it must remain idempotent and deterministic.
recommendations: Preserve serde and schemars annotations and update the JSON schema if you change any public config fields; consumers and editor tooling rely on that schema.
When modifying numeric fields keep Decimal/Percentage semantics intact to retain two-decimal TOML output and validation behavior; any change affects on-disk formatting and tests.
Maintain the ConfigReader layering order (defaults → legacy → global/user file → provided TOML → env) and its .env idempotency semantics; altering order changes effective config values.
If you change public types re-exported in lib.rs, update all downstream consumers and run the workspace tests; ensure Error conversions remain comprehensive.

Run the crate's unit tests and the workspace test suite (cargo test --workspace) and treat warnings as potential CI failures (RUSTFLAGS -Dwarnings in CI). Also run tests that assert TOML formatting and schema behavior (those present in decimal/percentage/compact/writer modules).

crates/forge_repo/src.description: This crate subtree implements the repository and provider integration layer for persistence, workspace indexing, and external LLM provider communication. It centralizes translation between domain types and storage/network protocols (SQLite/Diesel, gRPC, HTTP/SSE, snapshots) and provides adapters that higher-level services (CLI, agents, chat routing) depend on for durable storage, model discovery, chat streaming, and workspace search/index operations.
key_behaviors: Map domain repository operations to concrete infra: persist/fetch conversations, snapshots, workspace metadata, and KV entries via Diesel/SQLite or delegated snapshot service.

Route chat and model-list requests to provider-specific implementations (OpenAI-compatible, Anthropic, Google, Bedrock, Responses/Codex), including header composition, request transformation, and streaming SSE parsing into domain ChatCompletionMessages.
Translate between protocol boundaries: proto/tonic gRPC for workspace/fuzzy-search and HTTP/SSE for providers; convert proto/HTTP payloads into domain types and vice versa.
Maintain backward compatibility during deserialization and migrations: detect legacy persisted variants (old ToolValueRecord formats), and apply migration paths when reading stored conversations.
Manage infra resources and background tasks: create/configure database pools with PRAGMA tuning and retry, run embedded migrations, and manage model-caching background refresh lifecycle.

insights.title: Workspace indexing path normalization
insights.problem: Workspace indexing on Windows sent backslash paths which the remote workspace server expected with forward slashes, causing indexing/search mismatches.
insights.root_cause: File paths originating from Windows were not normalized before being sent over gRPC to the workspace index service.
insights.solution: Normalize Windows file paths to forward-slash form at the repository boundary that constructs workspace index/upload requests so server-side expectations are met.

insights.title: Relaxed SSE handling and provider-specific sanitization
insights.problem: Some providers/proxies emit SSE with non-standard content types or require sanitized tool IDs; strict parsing or unsanitized IDs caused stream/failure errors.
insights.root_cause: Earlier strict SSE validation and lack of tool ID sanitization assumed ideal provider behavior and failed against proxies (Vertex/AWS Bedrock) or providers requiring different content types/ID patterns.
insights.solution: Support raw SSE parsing fallback and integrate ID sanitization transformers (e.g., sanitize tool call/result IDs and inject Bedrock cache points) in the request pipeline prior to sending provider-specific payloads.

insights.title: Legacy conversation record variants require migrations
insights.problem: Deserializing persisted conversations that used now-removed ToolValueRecord variants (Markdown, FileDiff, Pair) caused failures when loading older data.
insights.root_cause: Storage schema continued to persist legacy variants while domain model removed them without robust migration logic.
insights.solution: Add legacy variant types and explicit migration paths in the conversation record conversions so old persisted records are safely transformed to current domain representations during deserialization.

insights.title: Repository constructor and infra contract changes
insights.problem: Callers relied on previous ForgeRepo construction and infra function signatures (e.g., read_batch_utf8 batch contract, override_model/provider passing), causing inconsistent behavior or missing runtime overrides.
insights.root_cause: Evolving infra trait contracts and repository constructor expectations were not kept in sync with consumers.
insights.solution: Keep repository constructors and infra trait method signatures stable or ensure all callers are updated; when infra contracts change, update repository layer to accept new streaming/batching contracts and explicit runtime overrides.

insights.title: Background refresh and pool initialization must be cancellable and resilient
insights.problem: Background model-list refresh tasks and pool/migration initialization can leak or fail without proper cancellation/retry semantics, impacting shutdown or startup robustness.
insights.root_cause: Model caching logic and DB pool creation required explicit lifecycle handling which wasn't consistently enforced across implementations.
insights.solution: Ensure background refresh tasks are cancelled on drop and database pool creation includes retry/backoff for transient failures; preserve PRAGMA tuning and migration application sequence used in production.

key_files.why: Aggregate repository façade that composes conversation, snapshot, provider routing, and infra adapters — the central entry point other crates hold for persistence and infra operations.
High-level chat repository and ProviderRouter that selects provider backends, performs model caching and background refresh, and mediates chat requests to provider implementations.

OpenAI-compatible provider client that defines header composition, SSE streaming pipeline, special-case model lists (Vertex), and provider-specific error enrichment used by many provider configurations.
Diesel-backed conversation persistence implementation mapping Conversation domain objects to on-disk records and handling legacy deserialization/migrations used throughout the application.
gRPC-backed WorkspaceIndexRepository implementation that translates workspace/index operations into proto RPCs — responsible for path normalization and gRPC mapping for workspace/server interactions.

construct_relationships: ForgeRepo composes multiple repository implementations: ConversationRepositoryImpl, SnapshotRepository adapter (fs_snap), ChatRepository router, FuzzySearchRepository and ContextEngineRepository, and exposes them through a single façade for callers.

ProviderRouter (in provider/chat.rs) dispatches chat and model-list requests to provider-specific implementations (OpenAIProvider, Anthropic, GoogleResponseRepository, Bedrock adapters, codex/reponses adapter) which implement ChatRepository and share SSE parsing utilities.

Provider implementations depend on common SSE/event-source parsing and HTTP infra traits: provider/event.rs transforms EventSource streams to ChatCompletionMessage streams consumed by OpenAI/Anthropic/Google provider clients.

ConversationRecord <-> Conversation domain conversions (conversation_record.rs) are used by ConversationRepositoryImpl (conversation_repo.rs) to serialize/deserialize DB rows; legacy variant handling in records is relied on when loading old conversations.

Database pool builder and MIGRATIONS (database/pool.rs and schema.rs) are required by ConversationRepositoryImpl at initialization to ensure the expected schema exists and to tune SQLite PRAGMAs for concurrency and retries.
notable_relationships: This subtree depends on infra traits and implementations defined outside this crate (FileReaderInfra, HttpInfra, CommandInfra, SnapshotService) — changes to method signatures or behavior there require coordinated updates here.

Workspace indexing and fuzzy search call out to a remote workspace server (gRPC) and expect normalized paths and proto schemas; the FORGE_WORKSPACE_SERVER_URL and proto changes are cross-cutting concerns.

Provider clients interact with external provider endpoints (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, Bedrock, Responses/Codex); behavior differences (SSE content-types, header expectations, ID constraints) are handled in repo provider adapters and must align with provider config in forge_config.

Conversation persistence schema/migrations are relied upon by other crates that read saved conversations (e.g., UI, agent replay); altering schema or embedded migrations impacts upgrades and tests across the workspace.
Provider unit tests use MockServer helpers that normalize dynamic ports for snapshot stability; test snapshot expectations and insta tests across the workspace rely on consistent mock normalization.
recommendations: Preserve public trait signatures (ConversationRepository, SnapshotRepository, WorkspaceIndexRepository, ChatRepository) or update all callers across the workspace when changing them.

When altering provider request/streaming pipelines (SSE parsing, sanitization, cache insertion), ensure provider routing (ProviderRouter) and all provider implementations are compatible and that model-list caching/refresh semantics are maintained.
Maintain legacy deserialization/migration code paths for conversation records; any change to storage record formats must include explicit migration logic and tests that cover old variants.
Respect Windows path normalization for workspace/index uploads; confirm gRPC proto expectations before changing path encoding.

Run the provider unit tests (which use MockServer), conversation DB tests, and the full workspace tests: cargo test --workspace, cargo insta tests for snapshots, and Diesel migration application flows. Verify that embedded migrations (MIGRATIONS) are applied and database PRAGMA tuning is preserved.
Be mindful of lifecycle and resource handling: database pool creation includes retry/backoff and PRAGMA tuning; background model cache refresh tasks must be cancellable on drop to avoid leaks.
Keep CI constraints in mind (RUSTFLAGS -Dwarnings): ensure changes compile without warnings and update snapshots/insta approvals when test fixtures change.

crates/forge_api/src.description: This crate exposes the high-level, async API surface that the rest of the application and external callers (CLI, TUI, RPC servers, tests) use to interact with Forge runtime services. It defines an async, Send+Sync trait for operations (discovery, chat streaming, provider/model/tool listings, conversation persistence, provider auth) and provides a concrete adapter that wires in-process services and infra implementations to that trait. The lib re-exports DTOs and domain types so consumers can import a unified public surface.
key_behaviors: Trait-based decoupling: an async Send+Sync API trait defines the contract used by UI/CLI/RPC layers so implementations can be swapped or proxied.
Concrete composition: ForgeAPI composes ForgeServices, ForgeRepo and ForgeInfra to implement the trait, bridging application services with environment/infra operations.
Streaming interactions: chat requests and certain operations return streaming responses that must be consumed asynchronously and are expected to be safe across threads.
Provider and model management: API surface enumerates and resolves tools, providers, and models (including per-agent/provider variants) and exposes provider auth flows (init/complete/remove).
Persistence and workspace operations: conversation CRUD (upsert/delete/rename/compact), workspace discovery/indexing queries, and execution of shell/infra operations are exposed through the API.

insights.title: Commit/suggest configuration and cross-provider model listing not previously exposed
insights.problem: Callers previously lacked API endpoints to read/update commit/suggest config and to enumerate models across providers, limiting client-side features and causing duplicated logic in callers.
insights.root_cause: Initial API surface assumed a narrow global provider/model view and omitted endpoints for per-provider model discovery and commit/suggest config.
insights.solution: Ensure the trait and implementation include explicit operations for commit/suggest configuration and cross-provider model listing; treat these as first-class parts of the API contract so callers rely on the API rather than reimplementing discovery.

insights.title: Stale configuration usage in long-lived API/Service instances
insights.problem: ForgeAPI kept a single copy of ForgeConfig and after updates the services could operate against outdated configuration leading to inconsistent behavior (e.g., agent resolution, retries, provider selection).
insights.root_cause: Configuration was read/held at initialization and not consistently re-read or centrally referenced after runtime updates.
insights.solution: Make config usage explicit in the API contract: expose an update_config operation and ensure the implementation re-reads or centrally references the authoritative config so subsequent operations use the latest values. Be aware of propagation and concurrency when applying config updates.

insights.title: Ambiguous method names and single-provider assumptions
insights.problem: The original API names and semantics assumed a single global provider/model and used ambiguous method names (e.g., tools, models, providers, list_conversations) which created ambiguity for per-agent/provider resolutions.
insights.root_cause: Early design over-specified a global view rather than supporting per-agent, per-provider contexts.
insights.solution: Treat the API trait as explicitly per-agent/provider aware. Any renaming/splitting must preserve the async Send+Sync contract and be reflected across all implementers and consumers to avoid breaking behavior.

key_files.why: Defines the async Send+Sync API trait — the canonical contract consumed by UI, CLI, RPC, and tests. Changing signatures here affects all consumers and implementations.
Concrete ForgeAPI implementation that composes ForgeServices, ForgeRepo, and ForgeInfra to satisfy the API trait; handles provider auth flows, streaming chat, persistence, and config hydration.
Facade re-exports for DTOs and domain types used by external consumers; keeps import surface stable for API servers and other crates.

construct_relationships: API trait (api.rs) defines high-level async methods that callers (CLI/TUI/RPC) depend on; implementations must satisfy Send + Sync and stream semantics.
ForgeAPI (forge_api.rs) implements the API trait by composing ForgeServices<ForgeRepo<ForgeInfra>> and a ForgeRepo<ForgeInfra> instance to provide application behavior and environment access.
ForgeAPI delegates provider auth flows, model/tool listings, chat execution (streaming), conversation persistence, and infra shell execution to the underlying services/repo/infra components.
lib.rs re-exports DTOs and domain models from forge_app, forge_config, and forge_domain so API consumers import types from a single namespace aligned with the API trait.
notable_relationships: Depends on other workspace crates: forge_services, forge_repo, forge_infra, forge_app, forge_config, and forge_domain — changes here often require coordinated updates in those crates and in callers (crates/forge_main CLI/TUI, RPC layers).
Consumed by external callers: CLI/TUI, RPC servers and tests rely on the trait signatures and streaming behavior; changes ripple to those consumers and any transport adapters.
Configuration propagation: forge_config and runtime update flows must be consistent with the API's update_config semantics to prevent stale config usage.
Streaming and concurrency expectations: streaming response types and async Send+Sync bounds imply cross-thread usage; downstream code (UI/TUI, network transports) expects that contract.
recommendations: Preserve the async + Send + Sync bounds and streaming contracts on the API trait; any signature changes require updating all implementers and consumers.
When modifying behavior that depends on configuration, ensure the implementation reads or references the authoritative config source and that update_config semantics are honored to avoid stale reads.

Coordinate changes with forge_services, forge_repo, forge_infra, forge_app, forge_config, and forge_domain — updates to DTOs or method semantics will need matching changes in those crates and in CLI/TUI/RPC consumers.

If you change method names, parameter types, or return streaming types, update all adapters (CLI/TUI, RPC server, tests, and any mocks) and run the workspace test suite (cargo test --workspace) and integration tests to catch breaking changes.

Respect provider/model enumeration and per-agent/provider semantics: ensure commit/suggest config and cross-provider model listing remain discoverable through the API to avoid callers reimplementing provider logic.

crates/forge_markdown_stream/src.description: This crate implements a streaming markdown-to-terminal renderer used to present LLM output and other markdown streams in the CLI/TUI. It provides a streaming API that repairs incoming text, parses it into events, and renders headings, lists, code blocks, tables and inline elements using pluggable styler backends and terminal-aware wrapping/highlighting.
key_behaviors: Accept streaming text fragments, repair malformed lines (embedded fences), buffer/parse them and emit parsed events for rendering.
Map parse events to terminal output with line wrapping, indentation and margin management across headings, lists, blockquotes, tables and code blocks.
Apply pluggable styling via styler traits (inline/heading/list/table) with concrete ANSI and test-tag implementations; theme selection based on terminal light/dark detection.
Preserve and account for ANSI escape sequences and Unicode column widths while wrapping and computing visible widths (particularly for tables and code).
Syntax-highlight code blocks (via syntect) and integrate highlighting results into wrapped/indented terminal output.

insights.title: Repair required for embedded fences and partial lines
insights.problem: Parser mis-parses code block boundaries when closing fences are embedded on the same line as code or when input arrives fragmented.
insights.root_cause: Streaming input produces partial lines and the upstream markdown parser cannot handle embedded closing fences without preprocessing.
insights.solution: The crate centralizes a repair step (repair_line / split_embedded_fence) before parsing; any edits must preserve this preprocessing and its exact splitting semantics to avoid regressions in streamed parsing.

insights.title: Wrapping must preserve ANSI escapes and respect Unicode widths
insights.problem: Naive wrapping can break ANSI escape sequences or miscalculate visible column widths for Unicode content, producing misaligned tables and broken styling.
insights.root_cause: Width calculations and wrap/split logic operate on visible text but input may contain embedded ANSI sequences and multi-column Unicode characters.
insights.solution: Table and wrapping utilities explicitly preserve ANSI sequences and compute visible widths; changes touching wrap/split/render must maintain ANSI-preservation and Unicode-aware width logic and be validated by existing wrapping and unicode tests.

insights.title: Pluggable styler trait contracts are relied upon by renderer
insights.problem: Renderer and block-specific renderers (heading, list, inline) assume a stable styler API and behavior (e.g., tag styler for tests).
insights.root_cause: Styling is abstracted via InlineStyler/HeadingStyler/ListStyler/TableStyler traits and concrete implementations (ANSI/Tag) are used interchangeably in tests and runtime.
insights.solution: Any changes to styler traits or their expected outputs must respect backward-compatible method signatures and maintain the TagStyler outputs used by snapshot tests.

insights.title: Code highlighting relies on syntect and theme detection
insights.problem: Syntax highlighting and theme choice can introduce heavy initialization, platform-specific behavior or runtime failures if resources are missing or detection fails.
insights.root_cause: CodeHighlighter uses syntect and Theme::detect queries terminal color utilities; both are environment-sensitive.
insights.solution: Edits must preserve safe initialization, clear error/fallback behavior (e.g., defaulting to dark theme), and not assume availability of external assets. Tests and CI expectations should be considered when changing highlighting or detection code.

insights.title: List and table rendering maintain nesting/numbering state
insights.problem: Nested lists, ordered-numbering across levels and table buffering depend on internal state; incorrect resets or mutations lead to formatting/numbering regressions.
insights.root_cause: Renderer holds ListState and table buffers to render items incrementally while streaming.
insights.solution: When modifying Renderer or list/table logic, ensure ListState lifecycle, numbering resets and table buffering/flush semantics are preserved; unit tests exist to validate nested behaviors.

key_files.why: Public streaming API (StreamdownRenderer) that composes repair, parsing and rendering; entrypoint used by consumers to push/finish markdown streams.
Core event-driven renderer that maps parse events to terminal output and manages rendering state (code, lists, tables, margins).
Defines styler trait contracts used across renderers; changing this affects all styling backends and tests.
Handles code block highlighting and wrapping; integrates external syntect dependency and influences performance and output of fenced code blocks.
Implements table width computation, wrapping and ANSI-preserving logic—critical for correct table rendering and Unicode support.

construct_relationships: StreamdownRenderer (lib.rs) buffers input, runs repair_line (repair.rs) and feeds parsed ParseEvent tokens into Renderer (renderer.rs).
Renderer delegates inline formatting to inline.rs via the InlineStyler trait (style.rs) and uses HeadingStyler/ListStyler/TableStyler implementations provided by theme.rs.
Renderer uses CodeHighlighter (code.rs) to highlight fenced code blocks and then applies wrapping and margin logic before emitting lines.
Table rendering (table.rs) is invoked by Renderer when table events are complete; it relies on wrap/split utilities to preserve ANSI sequences and compute visible widths.
List rendering utilities (list.rs) maintain ListState used by Renderer to manage nested bullets, ordered numbering and checkbox detection across streaming events.
notable_relationships: Relies on external crates: syntect for syntax highlighting and colorsaurus (or equivalent) for terminal theme detection; failures or behavior changes in those crates affect runtime rendering.
TagStyler output is used by unit snapshot tests (insta) across the crate; test harness expectations depend on specific tag-format outputs.
Public StreamdownRenderer API is consumed by other UI/display crates (e.g., forge_display, forge_stream) and by the CLI/TUI entrypoint; behavioral changes will ripple to upstream CLI/TUI rendering.
Parser and ParseEvent types are external to this folder (streamdown or similar); any change in parsing assumptions must respect the parse-event contract expected by Renderer.
recommendations: When editing API/signature surfaces (lib.rs, style.rs, renderer.rs), preserve backward compatibility because consumers and snapshot tests rely on the existing contracts.
After changes, run the crate's unit tests and snapshot tests (insta) to validate wrapping, ANSI preservation, unicode widths, list numbering and code highlighting behaviors.
If modifying wrapping, width or ANSI-handling code, validate with inputs containing ANSI escapes and wide Unicode characters to avoid regressions in alignment and color preservation.

When touching code highlighting or theme detection, ensure deterministic fallback behavior on missing resources or detection failures (tests assume dark-mode default) and validate CI environments where assets may differ.

Respect streaming semantics: push/finish semantics, repair_line preprocessing and Renderer state lifecycles (list numbering, table buffers, blockquote depth) must be preserved to avoid stream misrendering.

crates/forge_json_repair/tests.description: This folder contains the unit/integration tests that validate the json repair functionality of the forge_json_repair crate. The tests exercise a wide spectrum of malformed JSON scenarios (comments, truncation, punctuation, quotes, escapes, numbers, special values, unicode/multibyte characters) to ensure repaired output is valid JSON and safe to hand to downstream parsers/consumers.
key_behaviors: Verify that malformed JSON inputs (missing commas/colons/quotes/brackets, trailing commas, ellipses, truncated input) are repaired into syntactically valid JSON representations.
Ensure string-handling robustness: escape sequences, control characters, special/escaped quote characters, and conversion of unquoted tokens/symbols into JSON strings where appropriate.
Handle numeric edge cases by converting or normalizing invalid numeric representations (leading zeros, malformed numbers) so parsing remains stable.
Preserve correct Unicode handling and avoid panics when repairing inputs that contain multibyte UTF-8 sequences or truncated multibyte strings.
Treat Python-like constants and undefined tokens (true/false/null/None/undefined) consistently so they become valid JSON equivalents or safe stringified values.

insights.title: Regression risk from truncated multibyte UTF-8 sequences
insights.problem: Previous implementation panicked when attempting to repair truncated or malformed JSON strings that contained multibyte UTF-8 characters.
insights.root_cause: Repair logic attempted to operate on string bytes/indices without UTF-8 boundary checks, causing invalid slicing or character decoding failures.
insights.solution: Repair code must be UTF-8 aware: validate boundaries before slicing, operate on grapheme/char boundaries or use safe UTF-8 APIs; tests include multibyte inputs to guard against regressions.

insights.title: Repairing truncated structures must maintain syntactic balance
insights.problem: Truncated inputs (missing closing braces/brackets, ellipses) can lead to incorrectly balanced output or lossy repairs that still fail parsing downstream.
insights.root_cause: Repair heuristics that append closing tokens without regard for nesting or incomplete tokens can create invalid JSON or alter semantic structure unexpectedly.
insights.solution: Repairs should consider nesting depth and incomplete tokens; tests assert that added closers produce parseable JSON and preserve the intended structure when possible.

insights.title: Ambiguous or malformed escapes and quotes
insights.problem: Malformed escape sequences or missing end-quotes can produce unrecoverable strings or change tokenization, causing parsing to fail or misinterpret data.
insights.root_cause: Repair logic that naively injects quotes or backslashes without considering surrounding context or escape semantics.
insights.solution: Repair must handle escapes and quote insertion carefully, ensuring escape sequences are properly closed or escaped and that inserted quotes do not break surrounding syntax; tests cover control characters, special quote chars, and escaped contents.

insights.title: Numeric edge cases can break strict JSON parsers
insights.problem: Inputs with invalid numbers (leading zeros, malformed numerals) can fail strict JSON parsing.
insights.root_cause: Heuristics that leave invalid numeric tokens in place or attempt naive numeric repairs that still don't conform to JSON number syntax.
insights.solution: Either normalize invalid numeric tokens to valid JSON numbers or convert them to strings consistently; tests validate both conversion strategies and parser acceptance.

insights.title: Python-like constants and undefined tokens
insights.problem: Inputs containing language-specific constants (e.g., True/False/None/undefined) are not valid JSON and can cause downstream parse failures.
insights.root_cause: Assuming input tokens map directly to JSON primitives without normalization.
insights.solution: Map or stringify such tokens to valid JSON equivalents (true/false/null or quoted strings) according to crate policy; tests assert the chosen normalization behavior.

key_files.why: Exercises core repair behavior for incomplete JSON structures and ellipsis handling — central to ensuring repaired output is balanced and parseable.
Combines comment removal and Unicode handling; important for guarding against multibyte-related panics and ensuring comments are stripped safely from multibyte inputs.
Validates insertion and repair of missing quotes and end-quotes — critical for string token correctness across many malformed scenarios.
Targets escape sequences and control characters; ensures escape logic produces valid JSON string contents.
Sanity checks that valid input remains unchanged and that repair routines do not alter already-correct JSON — serves as regression protection.

construct_relationships: Repair routines exercised by truncation.rs and punctuation.rs must coordinate: punctuational fixes (commas/colons) affect whether added closing brackets/braces in truncation.rs yield valid nesting.

Quote insertion/repair (quotes.rs) interacts directly with escape handling (escaping.rs) and special-quote normalization (special_quotes.rs): inserted quotes must be escaped correctly when adjacent to escape sequences or special quote characters.

Unicode/multibyte handling (comments_unicode.rs and error_cases.rs) intersects with string repair and truncation logic: truncated multibyte characters must be detected and repaired/escaped before any byte-level operations used by other repair passes.

Numeric normalization (numbers.rs) and special value normalization (special_values.rs) both affect token classification: the tokenization step must consistently classify tokens so that repairs convert or quote them according to crate policy.
Valid JSON tests (valid_json.rs) assert that repair is idempotent for valid inputs, so all repair passes must be no-ops when input already conforms to JSON.
notable_relationships: Downstream crates and consumers (e.g., any code that calls this crate to sanitize LLM outputs before serde_json parsing) rely on the repaired output being valid UTF-8 and parseable by serde_json; regressions can cause panics or parse failures upstream.
Tests in this folder implicitly define the public behavioral contract of the repair library — changes to repair outputs will require updating these tests or altering downstream expectations.

CI constraints (workspace-wide test and lint rules) mean tests here must compile under project-wide settings (RUSTFLAGS -Dwarnings); modifying tests or their expectations can affect CI pass/fail status across the workspace.
recommendations: When modifying tests or repair behavior, run cargo test for the crate and consider running the workspace tests to catch cross-crate regressions.
Preserve and respect UTF-8/multibyte test coverage: do not remove or weaken multibyte/truncation tests without explicit, coordinated updates to repair logic and downstream consumers.
Ensure repaired outputs remain parseable by serde_json (or other documented downstream parsers) — tests assume parseability as the correctness criterion.
Keep tests deterministic: avoid introducing non-deterministic output in repaired JSON (ordering, whitespace, or randomized fixes) since tests assert concrete outcomes.

If changing the contract for handling of non-JSON tokens (numbers, Python-like constants, undefined), update all relevant tests in this folder to reflect the new normalization policy and ensure downstream consumers are aware.

crates/forge_fs/src.description: This crate implements an async, tokio-based filesystem abstraction (ForgeFS) used across Forge to read, write, classify and hash workspace files with consistent error semantics. It centralizes binary-vs-text detection, UTF-8-aware reads and line-range reads, file metadata helpers, and a stable whole-file hash used by change-detection and indexing subsystems.
key_behaviors: Classify files as binary or text using multiple heuristics (BOM/zero-byte heuristics and sampled-type inference) before reading/indexing.
Read file contents in several modes: raw bytes, UTF-8 lossy, owned UTF-8 strings, and inclusive line-range reads with validation and capping.
Write and modify filesystem state asynchronously (create dirs, write, append, remove) while surfacing contextual errors.
Compute and provide a stable whole-file SHA-256 content hash for external change detection and indexing workflows.
Expose small metadata helpers (exists, is_file, read_dir, file_size) to callers with unified Error conversions and contextual messages.

insights.title: Two binary-detection approaches exist
insights.problem: There are two separate detection implementations: a BOM/zero-byte heuristic (binary_detection.rs) and a sampled inference using the infer crate (is_binary.rs). If they diverge, callers may get inconsistent text/binary classification.
insights.root_cause: Different detection strategies were implemented for different merits (fast heuristics vs. richer mime inference) and both are exposed or delegated to from ForgeFS.
insights.solution: Treat classification behavior as a single contract: any edits must preserve observable classification semantics for callers (especially those that gate reading/indexing). When changing one detector, ensure the other approach and tests remain aligned with the intended classification policy.

insights.title: Read-range returns FileInfo but change-detector expects whole-file hash
insights.problem: Range-reading logic originally returned a hash of the truncated range, which did not match external-change detectors that compare whole-file hashes, leading to false-positives for file changes.
insights.root_cause: Range read computed a hash from returned content rather than from the entire file; external systems expect a stable whole-file hash.
insights.solution: FileInfo produced by range reads must include a whole-file content hash (computed from entire file) so external change-detection and indexing logic can compare consistent digests.

insights.title: Multiple UTF-8 read modes and invalid-UTF8 resilience
insights.problem: Callers rely on both lossy reads and strict UTF-8 reads and the line-range reader has to handle multi-byte UTF-8 boundaries and invalid bytes robustly.
insights.root_cause: Different consumers require different guarantees (best-effort display vs strict content validation) and range operations must respect codepoint boundaries and indexing.
insights.solution: Preserve the existing modes and semantics: read_utf8 (lossy), read_to_string (owned strict), and read_range_utf8 (validates/handles boundaries and caps). Tests exercise edge cases; any change must keep compatibility with those test expectations.

insights.title: Centralized contextual filesystem errors
insights.problem: Multiple helpers add contextual messages and convert to a shared Error enum; callers and tests depend on consistent error variants and messages for diagnosis and higher-level handling.
insights.root_cause: ForgeFS exposes domain-level errors to simplify upstream logic and debugging; ad-hoc changes may break error conversions or message expectations.
insights.solution: Maintain the Error enum shape and conversion behavior. When modifying functions, ensure context-rich errors are preserved and conversions from std/io errors continue to map to the same domain variants.

key_files.why: Defines the ForgeFS abstraction, re-exports helpers (is_binary, Error), implements compute_hash used by change-detection and ties the module together.
Implements inclusive line-range reads, performs UTF-8 boundary handling and returns FileInfo containing the whole-file hash — important for indexing/change detection.
Provides a sampled, infer-based classification implementation that returns both boolean and descriptive mime/type information used to decide reading/indexing behavior.
Contains the BOM and zero-byte heuristic detector used for fast binary detection and test utilities; a second detection strategy that must remain consistent with infer-based logic.
Centralizes ForgeFS domain error types and conversions; other modules rely on these variants and contextual messages.

construct_relationships: ForgeFS::is_binary (lib.rs) delegates to detection implementations: binary_detection.rs (BOM/zero-byte) and is_binary.rs (sample+infer) depending on call sites.
ForgeFS::read_range_utf8 (read_range.rs) calls metadata and read helpers to compute inclusive line slices, uses ForgeFS::compute_hash (lib.rs) to produce a whole-file content hash for FileInfo.
Meta helpers (meta.rs) call the binary detection API (ForgeFS::is_binary_file) to decide whether to treat a path as text or binary before read operations.
Read helpers (read.rs) provide the low-level async reads used by higher-level operations such as read_range_utf8 and compute_hash implementations.
File size helper (file_size.rs) uses filesystem metadata and is used by read_range validation logic to cap ranges and validate indices.
notable_relationships: Upstream indexing and embedding subsystems depend on ForgeFS binary/text classification to avoid sending binary files to semantic indexing (privacy/performance concern).
Change-detection and conversation persistence (other crates) rely on the stable whole-file SHA-256 computed by ForgeFS::compute_hash to detect external modifications reliably.
The infer crate and tokio filesystem are external dependencies used heavily by this crate; any API or behavioral change here can affect many consumers across the workspace.
Public APIs and error variants from this crate are consumed by other workspace crates that expect contextual error strings and specific Error enum shapes for handling and logging.
recommendations: Preserve public API signatures, Error enum shape and conversion behavior; if signatures must change, update all callers and tests across the workspace.

When editing detection logic, ensure binary classification semantics remain consistent between the BOM/zero-byte heuristics and sampled infer-based detector, and update tests that assert classification behavior.
Ensure read_range_utf8 continues to compute and return a whole-file content hash (not a range-only hash) to avoid breaking external change-detection consumers.
Maintain UTF-8 read modes (lossy vs strict) and the tested behaviors around multi-byte boundaries and invalid UTF-8; run crate tests that exercise these edge cases.

Run cargo test for this crate and relevant workspace tests after changes; validate behaviors of code that depends on compute_hash, is_binary_file and Error variants (integration points with indexing and persistence).
Retain async/tokio-based patterns and contextual error messages added by wrappers so higher-level code and diagnostics continue to function as expected.

crates/forge_tracker/src.description: This folder implements the telemetry/tracking subsystem for the workspace: deciding when tracking is allowed, constructing and normalizing telemetry events, collecting environment/identity metadata, rate-limiting and dispatching events to collectors (PostHog), and initializing structured tracing/log forwarding. It matters because it touches I/O (network and disk), runtime behavior (background writers and async dispatch), privacy/opt-out gating, and global logging/tracing lifecycles used by other crates.
key_behaviors: Determine whether telemetry should be active via build-time VERSION checks and explicit opt-out, gating all tracking flows
Collect environment, system, git/ssh-derived identity metadata and assemble normalized Event payloads
Buffer and rate-limit telemetry events and conversation payloads, then dispatch them asynchronously to configured collectors (PostHog)
Initialize and manage structured tracing/logging (JSON output), optionally forwarding traces to PostHog via a writer kept alive by a Guard
Provide platform-specific persistent client IDs (Android vs generic) and a Collect trait with a PostHog implementation

insights.title: Prevent runaway telemetry dispatch
insights.problem: Unbounded or poorly synchronized event dispatch can cause runaway loops (e.g., errors causing new events that trigger more errors) or race conditions that undercount/overcount events.
insights.root_cause: Initial rate-limiter implementations used complex atomic compare_exchange logic and had race/window boundary problems; dispatch logic could enqueue events without sufficient throttling.
insights.solution: Use a simple, well-synchronized rate limiter (current code uses a Mutex-protected fixed-window counter) and ensure dispatch paths consult the limiter before sending. Treat the limiter as the single source of truth for per-minute counts and reset on window boundaries to avoid duplicate sends.

insights.title: Respect development placeholders and explicit opt-out
insights.problem: Telemetry must be disabled in development or placeholder-version builds and when the user opts out, otherwise development builds or CI can leak events.
insights.root_cause: Tracking enabled state depends on build-time VERSION and runtime flags; dev/placeholder versions should not be treated as production.
insights.solution: Centralize version-based gating (can_track) and ensure all dispatch and log-forwarding entry points check this gate. Unit tests validate dev and production strings—maintain those assumptions.

insights.title: Telemetry must not destabilize the host process
insights.problem: Failures in telemetry (network, writer, file I/O) can cascade into further errors if they produce telemetry events themselves, or block startup/shutdown if writer lifecycle not handled.
insights.root_cause: Dispatch and tracing writer perform I/O and spawn background workers; errors during these operations may trigger additional telemetry events or block if not isolated.
insights.solution: Keep telemetry errors contained (use Result alias and conversions), avoid emitting telemetry directly from failure handlers that could loop, and maintain Guard semantics to keep writer workers alive without blocking normal shutdown.

insights.title: Platform-specific client IDs and identity collection have privacy implications
insights.problem: Client ID creation and metadata collection (emails from git/ssh) access local machine identifiers and persist them; these flows differ by platform and storage method.
insights.root_cause: Client-id implementation is platform-specific (Android vs generic) and collects hardware or user-configured identifiers; environment metadata comes from multiple sources.
insights.solution: Respect platform module selection in client_id/mod.rs, ensure persistent storage semantics remain consistent, and keep identity-gathering confined to the tracker subsystem so other crates cannot inadvertently leak identifiers.

insights.title: Tracing/Log forwarding lifecycle must be preserved
insights.problem: Structured tracing is initialized to either write local daily-rotated files or forward to PostHog; changes can affect filters, output format, and writer lifetimes.
insights.root_cause: init_tracing configures subscriber layers and returns a Guard to keep async writers alive; altering initialization semantics can break log delivery or lead to background tasks being dropped.
insights.solution: Maintain init_tracing contract (returns Guard), preserve JSON trace format and writer selection logic, and ensure filters and asynchronous writer lifetimes are honored by callers.

key_files.why: Core Tracker implementation: collects metadata, buffers conversation payloads, coordinates dispatch to collectors and enforces rate limiting and opt-out checks.
Determines tracking eligibility from build-time VERSION and provides the canonical gate used across the tracker (unit tests assert expected behaviors).
Implements the fixed-window RateLimiter used to throttle event dispatch and prevent runaway telemetry; contains important concurrency semantics.
Initializes structured tracing and configures optional PostHog forwarding; manages writer lifecycle via Guard which callers rely on.
PostHog-specific implementation of the Collect trait — actual network payload formatting and sending logic for analytics collectors.

construct_relationships: Tracker (dispatch.rs) constructs Event instances (event.rs) and queries can_track::VERSION/can_track to decide whether to produce/send events
Tracker uses RateLimiter (rate_limit.rs) to check and throttle outbound events before invoking Collect implementations
Collect trait (collect/mod.rs) is implemented by posthog::Tracker (collect/posthog.rs); dispatch.rs depends on the Collect abstraction to send events
init_tracing and PostHogWriter (log.rs) may forward structured traces to the same PostHog backend that collect/posthog.rs targets—both use configuration gated by can_track
Client ID providers (client_id/*) are invoked by Tracker to populate Event identity fields (event.rs -> Identity), with platform selection via client_id/mod.rs
Error enum (error.rs) is used across dispatch, collect implementations, and any I/O/reqwest/serde operations to provide unified error handling
notable_relationships: This crate is a global facility consumed by other workspace crates (exposed via lib.rs): changes affect any caller that initializes tracing or emits telemetry (e.g., forge_main, other library crates).
Network dependencies: PostHog integration uses HTTP (reqwest) and may require environment keys/URLs; changes can affect runtime network behavior and should consider CI/production secrets.
File system and platform behavior: client_id persistence and tracing file writer touch disk and need correct permissions and paths across platforms (Android vs desktop).
Async/runtime interactions: dispatch and writer components spawn background tasks and use tokio primitives; edits must respect async context and non-blocking expectations of host crates.
Build-time versioning: can_track reads build-time env vars or Cargo package version; build pipeline/CI expectations (dev vs prod gating) rely on this behavior.
recommendations: Always respect can_track gating: ensure any dispatch, log-forwarding, or PostHog usage checks tracking_enabled before performing network/file I/O.
Preserve RateLimiter semantics and synchronization: changes must not reintroduce race conditions or allow per-minute counts to be bypassed.
Keep telemetry errors contained: avoid emitting telemetry from telemetry error handlers and maintain unified Error->Result conversions to prevent cascading failures.
Maintain init_tracing contract: return and hold Guard as before so background writers remain alive for the expected lifetime; do not block synchronous startup during writer init.
When editing client_id code, preserve platform selection in client_id/mod.rs and ensure persistent storage behavior remains compatible across targets.

Run the crate's unit tests and integration tests (cargo test for this crate and workspace-wide tests), and run cargo check/Clippy with CI-like flags (RUSTFLAGS set to -Dwarnings) to avoid regressions.

Be mindful of environment and secrets: PostHog keys and FORGE_WORKSPACE_SERVER_URL or related env vars affect runtime behavior — local tests may need mocking or guarded behavior to avoid external calls.

benchmarks/evals/semantic_search_quality.description: This folder contains a self-contained benchmark and evaluation harness for measuring semantic-search quality. It coordinates lightweight shell orchestration, a TypeScript LLM-based judge (Vertex AI / Gemini), manual/local evaluators, test contexts, and documentation to run reproducible scenarios that validate whether sem_search usage and results meet a schema-backed quality rubric.
key_behaviors: Detect sem_search tool usage by scanning a JSON context, extracting intent and file-type expectations.
Conditionally run a TypeScript LLM judge (llm_judge.ts) only when Google Cloud/Vertex AI authentication is present, and parse its structured (Zod-validated) output for CLI consumption.
Orchestrate multiple evaluation scenarios (good/bad/missing) via run_tests.sh which invokes run_eval.sh and aggregates pass/fail/skip counts.
Provide an alternative/manual local evaluation path (test_queries.ts and shell validators) for offline or deterministic checks.
Surface results for CI or local runs through exit codes, textual summaries, and formatted JSON output compatible with downstream tooling.

insights.title: LLM judge depends on Vertex AI / GCP auth
insights.problem: The core automated evaluation uses a cloud LLM (Gemini) and will fail or be skipped if Google Cloud authentication is absent.
insights.root_cause: llm_judge.ts invokes Vertex AI and run_eval.sh checks for gcloud auth before executing the TypeScript judge.
insights.solution: Treat the LLM path as conditional: keep robust detection of credentials, ensure clear skip/exit semantics, and keep alternative local/manual evaluators for environments without cloud access.

insights.title: TypeScript runtime expectations (tsx / Node)
insights.problem: The judge is a TypeScript script executed directly (tsx), so missing runtime or incompatible Node/tsx versions will break evaluation.
insights.root_cause: llm_judge.ts is invoked by shell scripts expecting tsx to be available in PATH.
insights.solution: Document and preserve the required runtime invocation pattern and ensure shell orchestrators fail visibly when tsx/Node are not present.

insights.title: Structured output must adhere to a Zod schema
insights.problem: Downstream tools and CLI output expect the LLM to return a validated Evaluation object; divergence breaks automated parsing and test assertions.
insights.root_cause: llm_judge.ts validates the LLM response against a Zod schema and formats results for CLI; run scripts and harnesses expect that structure.
insights.solution: Preserve the schema, its field names, and the formatting/exit-code contract; if the schema changes, update all orchestrating scripts and test contexts together.

insights.title: Assumptions baked into test scenarios
insights.problem: run_tests.sh asserts specific pass/fail/skip outcomes for predefined contexts; changes to evaluator logic or expected semantics will invalidate the harness.
insights.root_cause: The harness encodes expected results for 'good', 'bad', and 'missing' sem_search contexts and counts outcomes.
insights.solution: Maintain the mapping between test contexts and expected outcomes, and ensure any behavioral changes are accompanied by updated scenario files and assertions.

insights.title: Potential leakage of workspace/context to external LLMs
insights.problem: Running the LLM judge sends context extracted from test_context.json to an external cloud model.
insights.root_cause: llm_judge.ts composes prompts from provided context and invokes Vertex AI over the network.
insights.solution: Preserve explicit checks and messaging around when networked LLMs are used; ensure environment and consent requirements are respected and documented for private repositories.

key_files.why: Primary evaluator: composes prompts from context JSON, calls the Vertex AI model (Gemini), validates responses with Zod, and formats structured evaluation output.

Orchestration wrapper that inspects context JSON for sem_search usage, checks GCP authentication, and conditionally runs the TypeScript LLM judge; defines exit-code behavior used by other scripts and CI.
Test harness that runs a suite of scenarios via run_eval.sh, aggregates pass/fail/skip counts, and enforces expected outcomes for the evaluation tooling.
Manual/local evaluator that implements evaluateQueries/formatEvaluation, providing an offline deterministic alternative or reference implementation to the LLM judge.
Canonical input used by the scripts to detect sem_search calls and derive intents/expectations; changes to its shape affect all evaluators and orchestrators.

construct_relationships: run_eval.sh reads a context JSON and verifies presence of sem_search calls, then invokes llm_judge.ts when Google Cloud authentication is available.

llm_judge.ts parses the same context JSON, extracts sem_search intent and file-type constraints, builds an LLM prompt, calls Vertex AI (Gemini), and validates the returned structure with a Zod schema before emitting formatted results.
run_tests.sh sequentially invokes run_eval.sh with multiple predefined context files and tallies exit codes to assert expected pass/fail/skip outcomes.
test_queries.ts provides a local evaluation implementation that mirrors the expected output structure of the LLM judge, enabling offline checks and comparisons against llm_judge.ts output.
README.md and task.yml define the evaluation task and expected behavior that the scripts and TypeScript evaluators implement; they are the human-readable contract for the tests.
notable_relationships: External LLM service: llm_judge.ts depends on Vertex AI (Gemini) and requires valid Google Cloud credentials; run_eval.sh explicitly checks gcloud auth state.
Runtime/tooling: scripts implicitly require Node + tsx in PATH for TypeScript execution and a POSIX shell for the shell scripts; CI or local environments must provide these.
Schema and format contract: Zod validation in llm_judge.ts creates a hard contract that other scripts and the test harness rely on; changes ripple across all files in this folder.
CI/automation: exit codes and textual/JSON outputs are used by run_tests.sh and could be consumed by external CI; altering them impacts automation.

Context provenance: test_context.json (and potential other context files) is the source of truth for intent detection; other repo components that generate or preprocess contexts must maintain compatibility.
recommendations: When editing, preserve the authentication detection and conditional execution semantics (run_eval.sh) so that environments without GCP credentials continue to skip the LLM path cleanly.
Do not change the Zod schema field names or output shape without updating llm_judge.ts, test_queries.ts, run_eval.sh parsing logic, and the test scenarios in run_tests.sh simultaneously.
Maintain clear, non-ambiguous exit codes and CLI output formatting because run_tests.sh and any CI consumers depend on them for pass/fail/skip aggregation.
If changing how context JSON is parsed or its expected shape, update test_context.json and all dependent evaluators in this folder to avoid breaking the harness.

Be mindful of network and privacy implications: any edits that alter what context is sent to the LLM should preserve explicit checks and messaging about external calls; ensure scripts fail visibly when required runtimes (tsx/Node) or credentials are missing.

crates/forge_display/src.description: This crate provides terminal-oriented formatting utilities used by the CLI/TUI: markdown rendering with syntax-highlighted code blocks, colored inline diffs with counts/line numbers, and ripgrep-style search output formatting. It is a focused display adapter layer that transforms raw text (markdown, diffs, grep lines) into terminal-friendly, colorized strings consumed by higher-level UI and streaming crates.
key_behaviors: Extract markdown fenced/indented code blocks, replace them with placeholders, render the markdown body, then restore highlighted code blocks into the final terminal output.
Compute and format inline diffs between two texts into a colored, line-numbered view while accumulating add/remove metrics and ensuring width calculations match terminal constraints.
Parse ripgrep-style output (path:line:content), group and align results by file, and optionally apply regex highlighting; fall back to raw paths when input doesn't match expected pattern.
Expose aggregator re-exports so other crates import DiffFormat, GrepFormat, and MarkdownFormat from a single entrypoint.

insights.title: Syntax highlighter initialization cost
insights.problem: Initializing the syntax highlighter eagerly caused unnecessary startup work when highlighting wasn't needed.
insights.root_cause: Eager construction of SyntaxHighlighter (syntect assets/load) during crate initialization or MarkdownFormat construction.
insights.solution: Ensure highlighter initialization is lazy (deferred until the first highlight request) and consider thread-safe lazy patterns; avoid incidental startup overhead for code paths that don't use highlighting.

insights.title: Placeholder-based code block processing
insights.problem: Code blocks are removed and replaced with placeholders during markdown render, then restored; any change to placeholder format or restore logic can break final output or indentation.
insights.root_cause: Tight coupling between CodeBlockParser extraction and restore phases and the placeholder token format.
insights.solution: Treat placeholder token format, indexing, and indentation preservation as stable contracts when editing either extraction or restoration code; maintain compatibility across both phases.

insights.title: Snapshot-backed formatting expectations
insights.problem: Diff and grep formatting behavior is validated with unit tests and snapshots; visual/spacing/color changes will break tests.
insights.root_cause: Formatting outputs (width, alignment, color sequences, placeholders) are asserted via snapshots and unit tests.
insights.solution: When editing formatting logic, run the crate's unit tests and snapshot tests (cargo test, cargo insta) to detect regressions; understand that small whitespace or color-code differences will fail snapshots.

insights.title: Grep parser fallback behavior
insights.problem: Grep formatting expects ripgrep-style lines but must not crash on unexpected input.
insights.root_cause: Parsing logic branches: structured parsing when input matches path:line:content pattern, fallback to raw path when not.
insights.solution: Preserve the fallback behavior and its output shape so callers (which may pipe arbitrary text) continue to receive sensible results rather than errors.

insights.title: Diff width and metric stability
insights.problem: Diff formatting includes width-based layout and add/remove line counts that consumers rely upon for UI presentation and metrics.
insights.root_cause: DiffFormat computes layout widths and counts during formatting; changing width calculations or counting logic alters downstream presentation and metrics.
insights.solution: Keep width computation and counting semantics stable; if changing algorithms, verify visual alignment and metrics via tests and consuming UI components.

key_files.why: Primary consumer-facing markdown formatter that composes code block parsing and syntax highlighting; impacts how markdown appears in CLI/TUI.
Implements CodeBlockParser and SyntaxHighlighter primitives (extraction, placeholdering, restore) used by markdown rendering.
Produces colored inline diffs with line numbers, counts, and width calculations; used wherever diff output must be human-readable.
Parses and formats ripgrep-style search results, grouping by file and aligning line numbers; relevant for search result presentation.
Re-exports the display types (MarkdownFormat, DiffFormat, GrepFormat) that other crates import; changes here affect public API surface.

construct_relationships: MarkdownFormat (markdown.rs) uses CodeBlockParser and SyntaxHighlighter (code.rs) to extract, highlight, and restore code blocks when rendering markdown.
CodeBlockParser::new / ::restore coordinate placeholders and highlighted code slices; the placeholder token format is the contract between extraction and restoration.
DiffFormat::format (diff.rs) uses an external diff computation (similar crate) and produces a DiffResult with formatted lines and add/remove metrics consumed by UIs.
GrepFormat (grep.rs) relies on ParsedLine::parse to recognize ripgrep-style input, then groups parsed entries by file and applies optional regex highlighting to content.
lib.rs re-exports MarkdownFormat, DiffFormat, and GrepFormat so other crates (CLI/TUI/streaming) import display functionality from a single module.
notable_relationships: Consumers in higher-level crates (e.g., CLI/TUI crates like forge_main, streaming/rendering crates like forge_markdown_stream) depend on stable output formats and re-exports from this crate.

Third-party crates: syntect (syntax highlighting), termimad (markdown rendering), similar (diff computation), and regex are external dependencies whose behavior or feature flags affect rendering and initialization costs.
Snapshot tests and unit tests in this crate are part of the repository's test suite; CI enforces warning-free builds and snapshot consistency, so changes here affect CI status.
recommendations: Do not change placeholder token formats, indexing scheme, or indentation-preservation semantics without coordinating both extraction and restoration code paths; these are contract boundaries.
When modifying SyntaxHighlighter initialization or usage, preserve lazy initialization and ensure thread-safe behavior; be mindful of syntect asset loading and startup cost.
Run this crate's unit tests and snapshot tests (cargo test, cargo insta) after edits to detect formatting regressions; expect visual/spacing/color changes to break snapshots.
Keep width calculation and add/remove counting logic stable in diff formatting; verify downstream UIs render alignment correctly after any change.
Preserve grep parser fallback behavior for unexpected input to avoid breaking callers that feed arbitrary lines; ensure grouping/alignment remains consistent.
Avoid introducing new public API surface changes in lib.rs without updating all consumers; re-exports are relied upon by other crates and should remain stable.
Ensure any changes compile warning-free (CI treats warnings as errors) and consider impacts on runtime dependencies (syntect, termimad, similar) including feature flags and asset requirements.

crates/forge_json_repair/src.description: This crate provides a robust JSON repair and schema-coercion utility used to tolerate and fix malformed JSON-like text (common in LLM outputs) and to coerce values to declared schemars JSON Schemas. It exposes a small public surface (repair APIs and a centralized error enum) intended for consumption by other workspace crates that need resilient parsing and schema-safe argument extraction.
key_behaviors: Incremental, tolerant parsing of JSON-like input: detect and repair common problems (missing commas/quotes, trailing commas, comments, markdown fences) and deserialize into serde types.
Schema-directed coercion: recursively coerce serde_json::Value to match schemars::Schema (nullable handling, anyOf/oneOf/allOf, arrays/objects, and string→number/boolean/array coercions).
Multi-strategy parsing fallback: attempt strict JSON → JSON5 → repair parser to extract objects/arrays from messy or double-encoded strings.
Centralized error reporting: all parsing/coercion failures propagate via a shared JsonRepairError enum exported by the crate.

insights.title: Out-of-bounds panics with multi-byte UTF-8
insights.problem: Indexing/slicing logic produced panics when inputs contained multi-byte UTF-8 characters, leading to runtime crashes during parsing or repair.
insights.root_cause: Code paths assumed byte-indexing or byte-length-based slicing on strings while also operating on character sequences, causing invalid byte-offsets for multi-byte characters.
insights.solution: Parser code must use char-aware indexing/iteration or otherwise map byte positions safely when slicing; add tests with multi-byte characters to exercise repair paths and prevent regressions.

insights.title: Nested / double-encoded JSON not recursively parsed
insights.problem: Strings that contain JSON (including double-encoded JSON) were not reliably unwrapped and parsed, causing coercion to miss intended object/array values.
insights.root_cause: Coercion relied on a single parsing attempt and did not recursively detect/parse nested JSON or fallback across JSON/JSON5/repair strategies in nested string contexts.
insights.solution: Coercion must implement recursive parsing/unwrapping for strings using multiple parsers (json, json5, repair) and detect double-encoded inputs; unit tests should cover nested/double-encoded scenarios.

insights.title: Centralized error enum is part of the public contract
insights.problem: JsonRepairError is exported and used across the workspace; altering variants, semantics, or public signature surfaces can break downstream callers.
insights.root_cause: lib.rs re-exports the error types and functions, making them part of other crates' compile-time contracts.
insights.solution: Treat JsonRepairError and public function signatures as stable; any changes require coordinated downstream updates and API/versioning considerations, plus tests that validate error mapping.

key_files.why: Public crate surface — re-exports json_repair, coerce_to_schema and the JsonRepairError used by other workspace crates; changing this affects consumers.
Core repair parser implementation — implements tolerant parsing, many helper routines, and is the primary source of runtime complexity (UTF-8 handling, slice safety).
Schema-directed coercion logic — transforms serde_json::Value to match schemars::Schema and uses permissive parsing fallbacks; this file coordinates recursive parsing behavior.
Defines JsonRepairError and Result alias — centralizes error variants relied on by parser/coercion and by downstream crates.

construct_relationships: lib.rs re-exports the public functions json_repair and coerce_to_schema and the JsonRepairError type to make the crate's functionality available to other workspace crates.
parser.rs (JsonRepairParser) produces serde_json::Value or directly deserializes into requested serde types and returns JsonRepairError on failure.

schema_coercion.rs consumes serde_json::Value and calls permissive parsing routines (JSON → JSON5 → repair) when string values look like encoded JSON; it relies on parsing logic to extract nested structures for coercion.
error.rs defines JsonRepairError variants which are raised by parser.rs and schema_coercion.rs, forming the common error surface across the module.
notable_relationships: This crate is a public utility used by other workspace components that need to extract structured data from LLM or provider outputs (e.g., agent/tool argument parsing); changes to its API or error model can require coordinated updates in callers.

The coercion logic depends on schemars::Schema types (schemars) and serde_json behavior; feature flags or dependency versions for serde/serde_json/schemars may affect behavior and must remain compatible with workspace consumers.

Because this crate is intended to be tolerant of diverse inputs, it is a boundary for security and correctness when accepting external content (LLM outputs); its heuristics will affect downstream behaviour and must remain predictable.
recommendations: Preserve public API signatures and JsonRepairError variants unless you coordinate version bumps and update downstream consumers that depend on them.
When editing parser logic, ensure char-aware string handling to avoid byte-indexing panics; add/maintain unit tests that include multi-byte UTF-8 characters and exercise all repair helpers.
When editing schema coercion, cover nested and double-encoded JSON cases and ensure the multi-strategy parsing fallback (JSON → JSON5 → repair) is applied recursively for string values.
