Run the crate's test suite and workspace tests (cargo test --workspace), and lint/compile with the same strictness as CI (warnings-as-errors) to catch regressions early.

If you change dependency versions or public behavior (serde/serde_json/schemars), verify downstream crates compile and run their tests; update feature flags and Cargo.toml carefully to avoid breaking consumers.

crates/forge_walker/src.description: This crate implements a workspace filesystem walker used to enumerate project files for indexing and sync pipelines. It provides a File model and a Walker with configurable depth/breadth/size limits, binary-file filtering, .ignore handling, and both async and blocking entrypoints; correctness here is critical because callers (indexers, syncers, and external services) rely on consistent, privacy-sensitive file discovery.
key_behaviors: Traverse a directory tree with configurable depth, breadth, per-file size and total-size cutoffs while honoring .ignore rules.
Produce File descriptors for discovered files while skipping binary files (heuristic by extension) and excluding symlinks.
Expose both async (Walker::get) and blocking (Walker::get_blocking) APIs that must behave identically.
Enforce limits (file count, total bytes) and stop traversal deterministically when limits are reached.
Provide a utility is_likely_binary used by traversal to decide whether to include file content or skip it.

insights.title: Symlinked paths were being included in discovery
insights.problem: Symlinked files and directories (including dangling symlinks) were discovered and returned, causing duplicates or invalid files to be processed by downstream indexers and sync pipelines.
insights.root_cause: Traversal did not consistently filter out symlink entries; filesystem walkers followed or yielded symlink entries rather than excluding them.
insights.solution: Ensure walker excludes symlinked files and directories from results and treats dangling symlinks as non-existent. Validate both async and blocking paths enforce this exclusion; downstream consumers assume no symlinked entries.

insights.title: Heuristic-based binary skipping
insights.problem: Files are skipped based on an extension-driven heuristic (is_likely_binary), which may misclassify some files leading to omission of relevant content or accidental inclusion of large binary blobs.
insights.root_cause: Relying on extension lists and a simple heuristic is brittle across repos and file types.
insights.solution: Treat the extension-based heuristic as a configurable policy boundary: callers and tests assume a deterministic set of extensions and behavior. Any edits must preserve determinism and provide clear configuration/visibility for the list used by is_likely_binary.

insights.title: Traversal limits affect discovery and indexing
insights.problem: Breadth/depth/file-count/total-size limits can cause traversal to stop mid-tree, producing partial results that downstream systems must handle.
insights.root_cause: Walker enforces multiple independent limits without a single deterministic ordering documented across traversal runs.
insights.solution: Maintain consistent and deterministic traversal ordering and limit enforcement across sync/async paths. Tests/fixtures rely on the current ordering; edits must preserve the contract that consumers rely on when limits are reached.

insights.title: Sync and async entrypoints must match
insights.problem: Differences between Walker::get (async) and Walker::get_blocking (blocking) could lead to divergent discovery behavior in different runtime contexts.
insights.root_cause: Separate implementations can drift if changes are applied to one without mirroring the other.
insights.solution: Any behavioral change must be reflected identically in both APIs; tests exercise both variants and should be kept green to ensure parity.

key_files.why: Primary implementation: traversal logic, limits enforcement, binary detection, symlink handling, and both async and blocking entrypoints are defined here.
Public crate surface and re-exports (File, Walker). Changes here affect other crates that import the walker types.

construct_relationships: Walker::get (async) and Walker::get_blocking (blocking) implement the same traversal behavior and both call into shared File construction and is_likely_binary checks.
is_likely_binary is invoked by the traversal logic to decide whether to include or skip a file's content during enumeration.
File is the data model produced by Walker and is the contract consumed by downstream indexers and syncers; its fields determine what information is available to consumers.
notable_relationships: Output from the walker is consumed by indexing and sync pipelines (e.g., workspace indexing, forge_embed, and remote workspace servers); privacy and correctness (no symlinked or large/binary blobs) are critical.
Behavior must align with .ignore semantics used elsewhere (forge_fs / workspace helpers) so that file discovery is consistent across the codebase.
Tests/fixtures within this crate define expected traversal semantics; other crates and CI rely on those semantics being stable.
Because traversal can send file lists or contents onward, changes here have security/privacy implications for any external workspace servers (FORGE_WORKSPACE_SERVER_URL) and provider integrations.
recommendations: Preserve deterministic traversal ordering and ensure limit enforcement is consistent between async and blocking entrypoints; run this crate's tests (and fixtures) after edits.
Do not regress symlink exclusion: ensure dangling and regular symlinks are excluded from discovery in both implementations.
Keep the is_likely_binary heuristic deterministic and visible; if you adjust the extension list or logic, update tests that assert binary-skipping behavior.
Validate behavior against .ignore rules and confirm consistency with other filesystem helpers in the workspace.

Run cargo test for this crate and cargo test --workspace where relevant; be mindful of CI rules (warnings-as-errors) and performance for large-workspace test cases because traversal is sensitive to limits and resource usage.

crates/forge_ci/src.description: This crate programmatically constructs the repository's GitHub Actions workflows and reusable CI job/step building blocks. It centralizes strongly-typed job generators (matrix release builds, release drafting, label/bounty syncs, lint/autofix) and small reusable steps (e.g. protoc install) so workflow YAML is generated deterministically from Rust code and can be reused across pipelines.
key_behaviors: Compose Workflow objects (events, concurrency, permissions) and serialize them to .github/workflows/*.yml files.
Construct gh_workflow::Job definitions with steps, environment, permissions, and conditional expressions for build, release, lint, labels, and bounty tasks.
Generate matrix definitions for cross/target builds and convert them to serde_json::Value for embedding into workflow YAML.
Provide and reuse standardized CI steps (protoc installation, checkout, npm/node steps) across multiple jobs to ensure consistency.
Wire outputs and job dependencies (e.g., draft release id/name) so downstream jobs can read upstream job results and conditionally run release/publish steps.

insights.title: Jobs require precise GitHub permissions and secret references
insights.problem: Jobs that publish (releases, npm, homebrew) or run actions like release-drafter and autolabeler fail or produce incorrect outputs if permissions or tokens are missing or mis-scoped.
insights.root_cause: Permissions and secret names are encoded into job definitions and sometimes tied to specific events (pull_request_target vs pull_request); changing job signatures or triggers without updating permissions will break the workflow runtime.
insights.solution: Treat job permission and secret references as stable public API for the workflows. Any edit to jobs/triggers must preserve or explicitly update the corresponding permission scopes and secret usage across dependent jobs.

insights.title: Matrix data is serialized and consumed by jobs and release steps
insights.problem: Altering target triples, binary paths, or serialization format can desynchronize build jobs from downstream upload/publish steps that expect specific artifact paths or matrix variables.
insights.root_cause: ReleaseMatrix/MatrixEntry produce a serde_json Value that is embedded into workflow YAML; downstream job logic assumes particular keys/paths produced by these entries.
insights.solution: When modifying MatrixEntry fields or JSON conversion, ensure backwards-compatible keys and artifact path conventions are kept or update all jobs that consume those matrix values in sync.

insights.title: Single-source steps are reused widely and are high impact
insights.problem: Changing the implementation or inputs of a shared step (e.g. setup_protoc) will affect many jobs, possibly breaking protobuf compilation or differing protoc versions across CI jobs.
insights.root_cause: The step is authored once and re-exported for inclusion in many job generators; callers assume a consistent behavior/versioning of the step.
insights.solution: Maintain stability of reusable step signatures and inputs. If the step's contract must change (version or inputs), coordinate updates to all job builders that include it and validate builds that depend on protoc.

insights.title: Downstream jobs rely on upstream job outputs (release ids, tag names)
insights.problem: Renaming, removing or changing job outputs (e.g. draft release id/tag) will break downstream consumers that read those outputs to publish or upload artifacts.
insights.root_cause: Workflows export job outputs as YAML outputs and reference them by name in dependent jobs; these names are part of the workflow contract.
insights.solution: Keep output names and semantics stable, or update all consumers in the same change. Validate generated YAML to ensure outputs are present and referenced correctly.

insights.title: Language-specific scripts (TypeScript) require explicit checkout/install steps
insights.problem: Jobs that run repo-local scripts (bounty sync) will fail if checkout, Node/npm setup, and correct working directory/context are not added or are misconfigured.
insights.root_cause: These jobs construct steps that invoke npm/tsx tooling; missing node setup or incorrect directory context leads to runtime errors during CI execution.
insights.solution: Ensure job builders include the full set of prerequisites: checkout, node/npm install, and environment setup for executing repository-local scripts; treat these prerequisites as required parts of the job definition.

key_files.why: Crate entry that exports jobs, workflows, steps and release matrix types used by higher-level tooling to generate workflow YAML.
Builds the primary repository CI workflow (ci.yml) and composes build, coverage and conditional release jobs; changes here alter core CI behaviour.
Constructs the matrixed release build job including toolchain setup and optional artifact upload to GitHub Releases; tightly coupled to release matrix and publish flows.
Defines MatrixEntry and ReleaseMatrix and their JSON conversion; authoritative source for the cross-build matrix data embedded into workflows.
Provides the standardized protoc installation Step used by many build jobs; changes propagate across all proto-using CI jobs.

construct_relationships: workflows/* modules assemble Workflow objects by importing job constructors from jobs/* and step definitions from steps/*.
workflows/ci.rs references release_build_job::ReleaseBuilderJob and embeds ReleaseMatrix (converted to serde_json::Value) to create matrix build jobs.
jobs/release_build_job.rs depends on steps/setup_protoc.rs to include the protoc installation step at the correct phase of the build.

jobs that create draft releases (release_draft.rs, release_draft_pr.rs) export job outputs (release id, tag) that downstream publish jobs (release_homebrew.rs, release_npm.rs) read to perform publication.

jobs/bounty_job.rs and workflows/bounty.rs pair TypeScript-based sync scripts with required checkout/node install steps and schedule/event triggers; workflows orchestrate these jobs and their permissions.
notable_relationships: Generated YAML files are intended to be written to .github/workflows and are consumed directly by GitHub Actions; changes affect repository CI behaviour immediately when committed.

CI jobs reference external marketplace actions (release-drafter, autolabeler, protoc installer) and external registries (npm, Homebrew) which require appropriate repository-level secrets and permissions to be configured in GitHub.
Cross-compilation relies on toolchains and rust targets; CI build job generation must align with platform matrix and the cross toolchain setup used in the repository's release pipeline.
Protobuf compiler version and usage are shared concerns for other crates that compile proto files (e.g., forge_repo), so changing protoc step semantics can impact those crates' build expectations.

The workflows express conditional triggers (push, pull_request_target, scheduled) that determine when sensitive actions run; altering triggers can change security posture and who/what can invoke publishing steps.
recommendations: Preserve job names, exported outputs, permissions, and secret references unless you update all downstream consumers in the same change; validate references across jobs.
If you modify MatrixEntry fields or JSON serialization, update all jobs that consume matrix variables and validate artifact path conventions used by upload/publish steps.
Any change to reusable steps (setup_protoc) requires running the CI generation and building the affected targets locally to ensure protobuf compilation still succeeds.
After edits regenerate the workflow YAML and inspect the resulting .github/workflows files for expected events, permissions, job names, outputs and matrix values before committing.

Run crate checks and tests (cargo check/test for the workspace), formatting/linting, and ensure CI expectations such as RUSTFLAGS -Dwarnings are satisfied; treat generated YAML differences as potentially breaking changes to CI.

crates/forge_repo/proto.description: This folder contains the protobuf service definition (forge.proto) that declares the Forge repository RPC surface used to upload, list, delete, chunk, and search files. It is the canonical contract used to generate server and client bindings across the workspace and therefore governs wire format, compatibility, and the public API surface for repository-related operations.
key_behaviors: Defines an RPC service (ForgeService) exposing file-repository operations: Search, UploadFiles, DeleteFiles, ListFiles, and ChunkFiles.
Specifies message schemas for requests and responses that are used to serialize/deserialize payloads between clients and the repository implementation.
Encodes support for large-file transfer semantics via chunking-related messages/operations.
Acts as the single source of truth for generated bindings consumed by multiple crates; changes require regeneration of language-specific artifacts.

insights.title: Proto schema is the public contract; changes impact multiple consumers
insights.problem: Modifying message shapes or field numbers in the proto will break generated clients/servers and can cause runtime interoperability failures.
insights.root_cause: Generated bindings are produced from this single .proto file and distributed across crates; consumers expect stable field numbers and message structures.
insights.solution: Treat the file as a stable API: when evolving messages prefer additive, backward-compatible changes (new optional fields), reserve removed fields, and update/commit regenerated bindings and dependent crates together.

insights.title: Chunking implies special handling for large file uploads
insights.problem: Large file uploads cannot rely on single-message RPCs and require correct chunking semantics and reassembly logic on both sides.
insights.root_cause: Presence of ChunkFilesRequest/UploadFilesRequest messages indicates a chunked protocol; improper use can lead to partial uploads, corrupted files, or resource exhaustion.
insights.solution: Ensure clients and server share the same expectations about chunk ordering, finalization, and retry behavior; maintain consistent chunk boundaries and streaming semantics in generated code and runtime.

insights.title: File payloads may contain sensitive data and are serialized over the wire
insights.problem: Uploading or indexing files via this API could leak repository contents if transport, authentication, or endpoint configuration are misused.
insights.root_cause: Protobuf definitions do not by themselves enforce transport-level security or access control; those are provided at the RPC layer or deployment configuration.
insights.solution: Treat any operation that sends repository content as sensitive: validate that callers authenticate/authorize, that transport is encrypted, and that any downstream services that consume these messages are trusted and configured appropriately.

insights.title: Proto changes require coordinated regeneration and CI tooling
insights.problem: Local builds or CI will fail if the .proto is modified without regenerating bindings or if protoc versions mismatch.
insights.root_cause: Builds depend on generated code; CI installs protoc and expects generated artifacts to align with the .proto used during development.
insights.solution: When changing the proto, regenerate language bindings (e.g., Rust) with the same protoc/tool versions used in CI and commit or provide build.rs hooks so downstream crates compile reliably.

key_files.why: Canonical protobuf definitions for the Forge repository service; source of generated client/server bindings and the public API contract for file operations.

construct_relationships: ForgeService defines RPC methods (Search, UploadFiles, DeleteFiles, ListFiles, ChunkFiles) that accept corresponding request messages and return corresponding response messages declared in the same proto.

UploadFilesRequest and ChunkFilesRequest are related: chunking semantics in the proto imply a protocol where large file content is split across ChunkFilesRequest messages and assembled into an UploadFilesResponse.
SearchRequest/Response carry file identifiers and metadata that clients will use to reference results returned by ListFiles or UploadFiles RPCs.

Each message type in the proto maps to generated types consumed by server and client code; behavioral contracts (e.g., required/optional fields) are enforced by those generated types at compile/runtime.
notable_relationships: Generated bindings from this .proto are consumed by crates/forge_repo and potentially other crates across the workspace; changes must be reflected in all consumers.
CI pipelines install protoc and rely on consistent protoc versions; mismatches between local and CI toolchains can cause build failures.
Runtime behavior for uploads/searches interacts with other systems (storage backends, indexers, external workspace servers) which assume the same message semantics defined here.
Authorization/authentication, transport encryption, and privacy policies are enforced outside the proto (RPC layer/deployment) but are critical when these messages carry repository content.
recommendations: Any edit to forge.proto is a public API change: coordinate updates with regeneration of language bindings and update all dependent crates in the same change to avoid mismatches.
Preserve existing field numbers and message semantics when possible; if removing fields, reserve their numbers to maintain backward compatibility.
After changes, run the same protoc toolchain used in CI to regenerate artifacts, then run cargo build && cargo test for the workspace (or affected crates) to validate consumers.
Be mindful of chunking semantics and ensure client/server implementations agree on ordering, finalization, and retries for large-file uploads.
Consider transport and authorization implications: verify that endpoint configurations and tests ensure encrypted transport and proper access control for file contents.

crates/forge_test_kit/src.description: This crate provides test utilities and fixtures used by Forge's Rust test suite. Its primary role is to expose helpers that load test data (raw fixtures and JSON fixtures), parse or return them for use by test code across the workspace, and ensure tests have deterministic sample inputs. It matters because many tests in other crates depend on stable fixture loading and parsing behavior during local runs and CI.
key_behaviors: Locate and load fixture files from the test fixtures directory (resolve on-disk paths reliably)
Return fixture content as raw strings or parsed JSON values for consumption by tests
Provide a small, stable API surface that other crates call from their unit/integration tests
Operate deterministically and be compatible with concurrent test execution in the workspace

insights.title: Fixture path resolution must be platform-agnostic
insights.problem: Tests running on different OSes or from different CWDs can fail to find fixture files if paths are hardcoded or rely on relative working directories.
insights.root_cause: Using runtime-relative paths or assuming a specific working directory instead of resolving fixture paths relative to the crate manifest or embedding fixtures.
insights.solution: Ensure fixtures are resolved using crate-root-aware mechanisms (e.g., CARGO_MANIFEST_DIR/manifest path at build time or deterministic resolution relative to the crate), and handle path separators and Unicode consistently.

insights.title: Fixtures must be safe for parallel tests
insights.problem: If fixture utilities return shared mutable state or perform mutation on fixture files, parallel tests can have races or flakiness.
insights.root_cause: Shared mutable caches or in-place modifications of fixture data without synchronization.
insights.solution: Fixture loaders should return owned immutable data or provide isolation (copies) and avoid global mutable state; ensure any caching is thread-safe and documented.

insights.title: Stable public helpers relied on by many tests
insights.problem: Changing function signatures, return types, error behavior, or fixture file names will break many tests across the workspace.
insights.root_cause: Broad test-level coupling to small utility functions and specific fixture content/filenames.
insights.solution: When editing the API or fixture files, maintain backwards-compatible signatures or coordinate simultaneous updates across dependent tests; preserve error semantics where tests assert on them.

insights.title: Fixtures must be available in CI and when running workspace tests
insights.problem: Tests can pass locally but fail in CI if fixture files are not included in the crate's package files or are generated only in developer environments.
insights.root_cause: Assuming fixture generation or presence without ensuring they are tracked and discoverable in CI, or using include/exclude rules that omit them.
insights.solution: Ensure fixtures are checked into the repo and discoverable at test runtime on CI agents; if using include_str! or build-time embedding, verify build scripts behave identically in CI.

insights.title: Large fixtures affect test speed and determinism
insights.problem: Very large or randomly generated fixtures slow tests and can introduce nondeterminism.
insights.root_cause: Tests relying on big data blobs or on fixtures that change between runs.
insights.solution: Prefer small, deterministic fixture artifacts for unit tests and ensure JSON parsing produces consistent Value shapes; consider lazy loading or caching with thread-safety to limit overhead.

key_files.why: Primary public API for test utilities: functions to load raw fixtures and parse JSON fixtures used across workspace tests.

construct_relationships: fixture loader functions in lib.rs read fixture files from the crate's fixtures directory and return owned String content to callers in tests.
json_fixture helpers in lib.rs read fixture files and parse them into JSON values (serde Value or equivalent) which test code consumes for assertions or to construct inputs.
Fixture helpers are intended to be lightweight building blocks called by test modules in other crates, meaning changes here propagate to many test call sites.
notable_relationships: Used by unit/integration tests across the Rust workspace; changes affect cargo test --workspace runs.
CI expectations require these utilities to behave identically in CI environments (Ubuntu/macOS/Windows matrix) and not rely on developer-only state.
Interacts indirectly with project conventions (AGENTS.md) and test tooling such as cargo insta — tests that compare outputs against recorded snapshots may depend on these fixtures being stable.
recommendations: When editing, preserve public function signatures, return types, and error behavior unless you coordinate updates in dependent tests across the workspace.
Ensure fixture path resolution remains robust across OSes and working directories (prefer crate-root-aware resolution or build-time embedding).
Avoid introducing global mutable state or non-thread-safe caches; assume tests may run in parallel and design for immutable returns or synchronized caches.
Verify changes by running cargo test --workspace and any snapshot tests (cargo insta test --accept where appropriate) in environments matching CI; confirm behavior on supported OSes.
Do not remove or rename existing fixture files without updating all dependent tests and CI references; ensure fixtures remain present in the repository and discoverable during CI builds.

crates/forge_config.description: This crate defines the canonical, strongly-typed Forge configuration surface and the layered resolution machinery used by the application. It centralizes typed config models (ForgeConfig and nested types), migration from legacy JSON, validation/newtypes for clean TOML output, and read/write helpers that produce editor-friendly schema-annotated files — all of which are consumed by other crates to determine runtime behavior, provider/model selection, networking, and tooling.
key_behaviors: Layered configuration resolution: merge embedded defaults, legacy JSON, global user TOML, provided TOML blobs, and FORGE_* environment variables (plus .env files loaded once per process) to produce a final ForgeConfig.
Typed configuration surface: expose ForgeConfig and many nested typed structs with serde and schemars annotations to produce a stable JSON schema used for editor tooling and runtime validation.
Clean numeric handling and validation: use Decimal and Percentage newtypes to ensure two-decimal serialization and range validation for percentage-like fields.
Migration and compatibility: convert legacy ~/forge/.config.json into the current ForgeConfig shape while avoiding unintentional overwrites of defaults.
Persisting with schema metadata: write TOML files with a $schema pointer so editors can validate/autocomplete user configuration.

insights.title: Legacy files can unintentionally overwrite defaults
insights.problem: Legacy JSON or partially-populated user TOML could overwrite current defaults when merged, leading to missing default values or surprising runtime behavior.
insights.root_cause: Merging layers naively (present fields override defaults) without making many ForgeConfig fields optional and without selective preservation of defaults.
insights.solution: Treat many config fields as optional at merge time and explicitly preserve defaults when legacy layers lack full configuration; keep merge semantics documented and tests that assert defaults survive migration.

insights.title: Noisy floating-point TOML output harms readability and tests
insights.problem: Serializing raw f32/f64 values produced long noisy bit-patterns in toml_edit output, which reduced readability and broke expectations in tests.
insights.root_cause: Direct serialization of native floats into TOML without rounding or formatting.
insights.solution: Use a Decimal newtype that formats numeric values to two decimal places for stable, readable TOML and update serialization/JsonSchema implementations accordingly.

insights.title: Percentage-like fields must be constrained
insights.problem: Raw floats allowed out-of-range values and floating noise for percentage fields used in retention/eviction windows.
insights.root_cause: Unvalidated f64 usage for percentages without a domain constraint or consistent serialization.
insights.solution: Wrap percentages in a validated Percentage newtype built on Decimal that enforces [0.0, 1.0] and produces two-decimal TOML output; keep tests that assert range validation and formatting.

insights.title: Generated configs lacked editor schema metadata
insights.problem: Config files written by the tool did not include a $schema pointer, preventing IDE/editor validation and autocomplete.
insights.root_cause: Config writer omitted schema header when serializing ForgeConfig to disk.
insights.solution: Prepend a $schema pointer into written TOML files so editors can validate the file against forge.schema.json; ensure this header is kept in the persisted output and tests cover it.

insights.title: .env loading must be deterministic and limited
insights.problem: Repeated or inconsistent loading of .env files could cause nondeterministic environment-based config overrides across process lifetime.
insights.root_cause: Potential for directory-walking .env loads to be invoked multiple times or at unexpected phases of config resolution.
insights.solution: Load .env files once per process and centralize .env discovery in ConfigReader so merging order is stable and side effects are minimized; keep this invariant when changing reader logic.

key_files.why: Defines ForgeConfig, provider definitions and read/write helpers — the canonical typed model consumed across the workspace.
Implements layered configuration resolution (defaults, legacy, files, env, .env) and the single-entry build path used by callers to obtain ForgeConfig.
Persists ForgeConfig to disk and injects the $schema pointer for editor/IDE validation — affects user-visible files.
Provides the Decimal newtype used to produce stable two-decimal TOML serialization for numeric config fields.
Handles migration from the legacy JSON format into the current ForgeConfig shape and controls which legacy fields are preserved.

construct_relationships: ConfigReader builds a ForgeConfig by layering embedded defaults, LegacyConfig::into_forge_config, global/user TOML, provided TOML strings, and environment variables; it also ensures .env files are loaded once per process.

ForgeConfig contains provider-related structs (ProviderEntry, ProviderAuthMethod, ProviderTypeEntry, ProviderUrlParam, ProviderResponseType) which downstream crates (services, provider clients) rely on for provider selection and auth behavior.
Decimal is used by Percentage (and possibly other numeric config fields) so Percentage delegates serialization/validation to Decimal for two-decimal TOML output and enforces range constraints.
ConfigWriter consumes ForgeConfig and writes TOML to disk while injecting a $schema pointer, connecting schemars-generated schema (tests/schema.rs) and editor tooling.
Error is the unified error type for the crate and is used by reader/writer/legacy conversion functions to present consistent error messages to callers.
notable_relationships: ForgeConfig types are public API used by other workspace crates (crates/forge_main, crates/forge_services, crates/forge_repo, etc.), so changes to shapes/serde/schemars annotations affect many consumers.

Environment variable overrides (FORGE_*) and .env loading interact with runtime behavior elsewhere (provider selection, workspace indexing, server URLs) and can expose secrets or change network endpoints.
Editor integration relies on the $schema pointer emitted by ConfigWriter and the forge.schema.json artifact, so schema stability is important for user experience and editor autocomplete.
CI enforces -Dwarnings and schema/tests; any change must keep the crate warning-free and schema-generation tests passing to succeed in the workspace CI matrix.
recommendations: Preserve serde and schemars annotations and the public ForgeConfig shapes unless you coordinate updates across all dependent crates; treat these types as a public API.

When modifying numeric fields or serialization, maintain two-decimal Decimal behavior and Percentage validation to avoid noisy TOML and out-of-range values; update tests that assert formatting/validation.
Respect the layering semantics in reader.rs: ensure legacy migration does not inadvertently overwrite defaults, and keep the ‘load .env once per process’ invariant.
Keep ConfigWriter's $schema header behavior intact; if changing the schema pointer or format, update the schema artifact and tests (tests/schema.rs) accordingly.

Run workspace-level checks after edits: cargo test --workspace, schema generation tests, and CI-like static checks (clippy/warnings treated as errors) to ensure compatibility with other crates and editor integrations.

crates/forge_repo.description: This crate provides the repository and provider-adapter layer that bridges domain operations to concrete persistence and external services. It implements durable conversation storage (SQLite/Diesel), workspace/index gRPC clients (protobuf/tonic), provider-specific chat clients (HTTP/SSE), filesystem snapshots and migration/compatibility logic — all of which higher-level services (CLI, agents, chat routers) rely on for correctness and interoperability.
key_behaviors: Translate domain repository calls into concrete infra actions: persist/fetch conversations, create snapshots, read files, execute commands, and run HTTP requests.
Map workspace/index operations to a gRPC ForgeService (compile-time proto contract) and convert between proto types and domain models.

Route chat/model requests from a unified chat entrypoint to provider-specific implementations (OpenAI/Responses, Anthropic, Google, Bedrock), stream SSE responses into domain ChatCompletionMessage items, and cache model lists with background refresh.
Maintain compatibility and migration paths for persisted conversation records and provider-specific payload variants (legacy tool value formats, ID constraints).
Provide test scaffolding and infra (mock HTTP server, in-memory DB pools, proto build) used by provider and repo unit tests.

insights.title: Workspace path normalization on Windows
insights.problem: Workspace indexing sent Windows backslash paths to the workspace server which expected forward slashes, causing mismatches or failed indexing.
insights.root_cause: Differing path separators on Windows were forwarded unchanged across the gRPC boundary.
insights.solution: Normalize workspace file paths to forward slashes before sending to the workspace/indexing RPC to ensure server compatibility; preserve this behavior when modifying workspace upload/search flows.

insights.title: Protobuf/tonic boundary centralization
insights.problem: Network serialization/deserialization and proto-to-domain conversions are concentrated in the gRPC repository implementations; breaking changes to proto types or conversion logic affect multiple flows.
insights.root_cause: The ForgeContextEngineRepository and fuzzy-search repo map domain operations directly to generated proto types and tonic requests.
insights.solution: Any change that touches proto messages, generated prost types, or the request/response mapping must consider build.rs prost compilation, the generated module public API, and consumers that depend on those domain conversions.

insights.title: Legacy conversation/tool value variants require migration
insights.problem: Older stored conversations used tool value variants (Markdown, FileDiff, Pair) that no longer exist in the domain model, causing deserialization failures on load.
insights.root_cause: Domain model evolution removed variants without corresponding migration handling in repository DTO conversions.
insights.solution: Repository record conversions include legacy variant branches and migration logic to map old formats into current domain types; preserve and extend these migrations when altering storage schema or domain model shapes.

insights.title: Relaxed SSE parsing and provider-specific sanitization
insights.problem: Some providers and proxies send SSE streams with non-standard content types or require tool ID constraints (Bedrock/Anthropic), which previously caused failures or rejections.
insights.root_cause: Strict SSE content-type validation and un-sanitized tool IDs did not accommodate proxy or provider-specific requirements.
insights.solution: Allow raw SSE parsing where needed and sanitize tool call/result IDs (replace invalid characters) before sending; maintain these paths when changing provider request construction or SSE parsing code.

insights.title: Chat router mixes routing, model caching, and background refresh
insights.problem: ForgeChatRepository both selects provider backends and manages on-disk model caches with background refresh tasks, leading to tight coupling and lifecycle considerations (abort on drop).
insights.root_cause: Provider routing and model list caching were implemented together in a single high-level repository.
insights.solution: Consumers must be aware that changes to chat routing or model caching affect background tasks and shutdown behavior; ensure model list caching, refresh scheduling, and cancellation semantics remain consistent when modifying routing logic.

key_files.why: Aggregates and composes lower-level repositories and infra traits; primary façade used by higher-level code to access conversation, snapshot, provider, HTTP, and file infra.
Implements gRPC-backed WorkspaceIndexRepository mapping domain workspace operations to the ForgeService proto API — central to indexing/upload/search flows.
Diesel-backed ConversationRepository implementation; handles persistence, retrieval, and legacy deserialization for conversations — changes here impact on-disk schema and migrations.
High-level chat routing entrypoint that selects provider implementations and manages model caching and background refresh tasks used by the rest of the system.
Creates/configures SQLite connection pools, runs embedded migrations, and includes retry/backoff logic — touching DB setup affects tests and production migrations.

construct_relationships: ForgeRepo composes multiple concrete repositories (ConversationRepositoryImpl, SnapshotRepository adapter, ChatRepository implementations) and delegates infra calls (FileReaderInfra, HttpInfra, CommandInfra) supplied via an Arc<F> infra object.

ForgeContextEngineRepository uses tonic-generated proto types (compiled by build.rs) to call ForgeService RPCs for workspace operations and maps proto responses into domain workspace and search node types.

ConversationRepositoryImpl persists domain Conversation objects via ConversationRecord DTOs defined in conversation_record.rs, which include migration logic for legacy variants during TryFrom/From conversions.

ProviderRouter/ForgeChatRepository routes chat and model-list calls to provider-specific implementations (Anthropic, Google, Bedrock, OpenAI/Responses codex repo); provider implementations implement ChatRepository and stream SSE responses which are parsed by event.rs helpers into ChatCompletionMessage streams.

DatabasePool (database/pool.rs) is used by ConversationRepositoryImpl for SQLite connection management and runs embedded Diesel migrations; tests rely on in-memory pool creation and pool config tuning.
notable_relationships: Proto contract dependency: build.rs compiles proto/forge.proto producing prost/tonic code consumed by context_engine.rs and fuzzy_search.rs; changing proto messages or RPCs requires regen and careful mapping updates.

External services: workspace/index gRPC server (FORGE_WORKSPACE_SERVER_URL) receives normalized paths and uploads; provider endpoints (Anthropic, Google, Bedrock, OpenAI/Responses) are invoked via HttpInfra and depend on SSE parsing behavior and header composition.

Cross-crate infra: snapshot adapter delegates to forge_snaps::SnapshotService; infra trait implementations (HttpInfra, FileReaderInfra, CommandInfra) are provided by the runtime environment crate and expected by ForgeRepo.

Persistence surface: conversation storage schema/migrations are persisted via embedded Diesel migrations; changes to record shapes affect on-disk compatibility and require migration paths in conversation_record.rs.
Tests and mocks: provider unit tests use MockServer utilities and normalize_ports for stable snapshots; altering SSE formats or port usage will affect test fixtures and snapshots.
recommendations: When modifying proto-related code, run cargo clean && cargo build to ensure build.rs regenerates prost types and verify no API/ABI mismatches; update all mappings that consume generated types.
Preserve Windows path normalization for workspace uploads/search; validate end-to-end with the workspace server or mocked gRPC tests to avoid cross-platform regressions.

If changing conversation record shapes or storage schema, add migration logic for legacy variants and update embedded Diesel migrations; run conversation persistence unit tests and migration scenarios.

When adjusting provider request/streaming behavior, run provider unit tests (they use MockServer) and verify SSE parsing modes (raw vs strict) and header composition for each provider; ensure sanitize-tool-IDs logic remains applied where required.

Changes to chat routing/model caching must consider background refresh lifecycle: run tests that exercise model caching, background refresh, and repository drop/cancellation to avoid dangling tasks or unflushed caches.

Database pool/migration changes require running the embedded migrations and pool-related tests; ensure retry/backoff semantics and PRAGMA tuning remain suitable for both in-memory test pools and production SQLite files.

Run the crate's test suite (cargo test -p forge_repo) and provider integration tests after changes; consider running workspace-level tests that exercise the generated proto interactions and cross-crate infra bindings.

crates/forge_api.description: This crate defines the high-level, async API surface used by the CLI/TUI/RPC/test callers and provides a concrete adapter that wires application services and environment infra to that trait. It exists to decouple callers from concrete service implementations, support streaming interactions (chat, shell), and expose DTOs/domain types for external consumers.
key_behaviors: Trait-based decoupling: an async, Send+Sync API trait describes discovery, chat streaming, listings, conversation persistence, and provider auth so callers can depend on an interface rather than concrete implementations.
Concrete composition: ForgeAPI composes ForgeServices, ForgeRepo and ForgeInfra and implements the API trait to bridge in-process services with environment operations.
Streaming interactions: chat and certain operations return streamed, async responses that must be consumed safely across threads and async tasks.
Provider and model discovery/management: lists tools/models/providers/agents and coordinates provider authentication flows (init/complete/remove) and per-agent provider/model resolution.
Configuration lifecycle: the crate surfaces configuration operations (including update_config/hydration) and must avoid stale config being used by services.

insights.title: Missing commit/suggest config and cross-provider model listing
insights.problem: The API surface originally lacked endpoints for commit/suggest configuration and for listing models across providers.
insights.root_cause: The trait initially assumed a single global provider/model and did not include per-provider or commit-level configuration hooks.
insights.solution: Ensure the public API includes commit/suggest configuration and cross-provider model listing semantics so callers can discover provider-scoped models and configure commit/suggest behavior uniformly.

insights.title: Per-agent provider/model support required
insights.problem: Method names and semantics previously implied a single global provider/model, causing ambiguity when agents or skills need per-agent providers/models.
insights.root_cause: Trait design and method naming assumed global defaults rather than scoping operations per agent or skill.
insights.solution: Treat providers and models as potentially agent-scoped in the API contract; callers and implementations should accept explicit scope or return provider-scoped listings to avoid ambiguity.

insights.title: Stale configuration risk in long-lived API implementations
insights.problem: ForgeAPI stored a single ForgeConfig copy and operations could run against stale configuration after updates, leading to inconsistent behavior (resolution, retries, auth).
insights.root_cause: Configuration was captured/held inside the API implementation without an explicit update path.
insights.solution: Expose/update configuration in the API surface (e.g., update_config) and ensure implementations refresh/hydrate config before operations that depend on it to maintain coherence between callers and services.

insights.title: Streaming responses must be Send + Sync across async boundaries
insights.problem: Streaming chat and shell execution return async streams that need to be safe to move across threads and interoperate with different runtimes.
insights.root_cause: The API is used by CLI/TUI and potential RPC servers; streams that are not Send/Sync or that carry non-thread-safe state will break cross-thread usage.
insights.solution: Ensure stream types, closures, and any captured state in the API are Send + Sync and that ConsoleWriter implementations and stream consumers are designed for cross-thread async usage.

key_files.why: Defines the async Send+Sync API trait that callers (CLI/TUI/RPC/tests) depend on; changing this file alters the public contract.
Concrete ForgeAPI adapter that composes ForgeServices/ForgeRepo/ForgeInfra and implements the API trait; central point where config, provider auth, streaming, and infra interactions are wired.
Facade/re-export module that presents DTOs and domain types to external consumers; changes affect import surface for API servers and callers.
Crate manifest: dependency and feature changes here affect compilation and cross-crate interactions.

construct_relationships: API trait (api.rs) is implemented by ForgeAPI (forge_api.rs), so any trait method signature changes require corresponding implementation updates.
ForgeAPI composes ForgeServices, ForgeRepo and ForgeInfra types (generic parameters) to perform discovery, chat, persistence, provider auth, and shell execution.
ForgeAPI implements or delegates ConsoleWriter functionality to provide structured streaming output; streaming paths cross the API trait and the concrete adapter.
lib.rs re-exports API types and DTOs from forge_app/forge_config/forge_domain so consumer-facing types are surfaced consistently from this crate.
notable_relationships: This crate sits between caller layers (crates/forge_main CLI/TUI, RPC servers, tests) and lower-level crates (forge_services, forge_repo, forge_infra, forge_config). Changes here ripple to both caller code and service/infra implementations.
Configuration changes must be coordinated with forge_config and any consumers that read config; update_config semantics here affect agent resolution and provider behavior elsewhere.
Provider authentication and credential lifecycles interact with provider/service crates and persistent storage (forge_repo); auth API changes can impact login flows used by the CLI and zsh plugin.
Streaming/chat behavior affects UI rendering crates (forge_stream, forge_markdown_stream, forge_display) and any RPC transport expecting a particular stream shape or threading guarantees.
Because CI enforces -Dwarnings and workspace-wide checks, modifying public API or dependencies may require updates in other workspace crates to remain warning-free and build cleanly.
recommendations: Preserve API trait stability: any signature or semantic changes to api.rs require coordinated updates to forge_api.rs and all callers (CLI/TUI/RPC/tests) that implement or use the trait.
Maintain Send + Sync and async safety for all public types and streaming responses; ensure stream implementations and captured state are thread-safe.
When touching configuration handling, ensure update_config/hydration semantics are respected so services do not operate on stale config; consider where config is read and refreshed inside ForgeAPI.
Validate provider/model scoping: updates that affect provider/model listing or auth must account for per-agent/provider resolution and impacts on downstream callers.

Run workspace-wide verification after edits: cargo check/test --workspace, cargo clippy, and any integration tests that exercise conversation persistence, provider auth flows, and streaming chat to catch regressions.

crates/forge_ci.description: This crate programmatically generates the repository's GitHub Actions workflows and reusable job/step building blocks. It centralizes typed job constructors (release build matrices, release drafting, label/bounty syncs, lint/autofix) and serializes them into workflow YAML so CI pipelines are deterministic and re-usable across the repository.
key_behaviors: Compose GitHub Actions workflows (events, concurrency, permissions) and serialize them to .github/workflows/*.yml
Generate matrixed release build jobs that install toolchains (protoc, cross, rust targets) and optionally upload artifacts to GitHub Releases
Produce specialized jobs for release drafting, release publishing (npm/homebrew), and label/bounty synchronization that run Node/TS scripts or GitHub Actions
Provide small reusable CI steps (e.g., setup_protoc) that are referenced by multiple jobs to ensure consistent environment setup
Wire job-level inputs/outputs and conditional expressions so multi-job release pipelines (draft -> build -> publish) propagate metadata (release_id, tag)

insights.title: Matrix and job payload shapes must serialize to valid GitHub Actions YAML/JSON
insights.problem: Manual conversion of ReleaseMatrix/MatrixEntry to serde_json::Value can produce an incorrect key/value shape or missing fields leading to invalid workflow YAML or unexpected matrix behavior.
insights.root_cause: The CI generation code produces nested JSON/Value objects for embedding matrices and job inputs; changes to the MatrixEntry shape or serialization logic are not automatically validated against the GitHub Actions schema.
insights.solution: Treat the serialization boundary as fragile — ensure changes to matrix types preserve the expected JSON structure and validate generated YAML/JSON (e.g., run the generator and a YAML linter/validator) before committing.

insights.title: Build jobs rely on external tool installation steps (protoc, cross, rust targets)
insights.problem: If a job omits the protoc/cross/target setup step or the setup step changes semantics, protobuf compilation or cross builds will fail in CI.
insights.root_cause: Release build constructors assume the presence of setup_protoc and toolchain setup steps; these are implemented as reusable Steps referenced by multiple job builders.
insights.solution: Keep the setup_protoc and toolchain installation steps stable and ensure any change preserves the expected binaries/paths available to later steps in the job.

insights.title: Jobs performing label/release operations require explicit permissions and correct event choices
insights.problem: Incorrect permissions or using the wrong trigger context (e.g., pull_request vs pull_request_target) can cause failures or security regressions when jobs try to write labels, create drafts, or upload releases.
insights.root_cause: Job constructors set Permissions and rely on event contexts; these are embedded in the generated Job definitions and need to match the action being executed.
insights.solution: Preserve and review job-level Permissions and event conditions when editing job constructors; ensure the intended GitHub context is used for actions that require secrets or elevated permissions.

insights.title: Release artifact upload depends on draft creation outputs and consistent output names
insights.problem: If the draft-release job output names or the job dependency chain change, downstream upload steps will not find release_id or tag and will skip publishing.
insights.root_cause: The release pipeline is implemented as multiple jobs where create_draft_release_job emits outputs (release id, tag) that are consumed by release_build_job/upload steps.
insights.solution: Keep output names and the job dependency graph stable; when changing any job that produces or consumes outputs, update the consuming job definitions to use the new names/expressions and validate end-to-end generation.

insights.title: Jobs that run TypeScript/NPM scripts require Node environment setup and scoped permissions
insights.problem: Bounty/label sync jobs execute npm/tsx scripts; if Node is not installed in the job environment or package artifacts are missing, those steps will fail.
insights.root_cause: Job builders assume Node and npm are available (or that a setup-node action is included) before running TypeScript scripts.
insights.solution: Ensure job step sequences include Node setup steps and that the repository produces the expected scripts/assets; verify permissions needed to comment/update issues/PRs are present.

key_files.why: Primary generator for the main CI workflow; composes build, coverage, performance, and conditional release jobs that define the repository's CI surface.
Constructs the matrixed release build job, wires toolchain installation steps, and conditionally uploads release artifacts — central to release pipeline correctness.
Defines the release build matrix entries and serializes them to JSON; changes here alter the cross-build target set and artifact names embedded in workflows.
Generates jobs that run repository TypeScript scripts to reconcile bounty labels and propagate them to PRs — it relies on Node environment and correct Permissions.
Reusable step to install protoc; referenced by multiple build jobs and required for protobuf codegen used by other crates.

construct_relationships: ReleaseMatrix (release_matrix.rs) is converted to serde_json::Value and embedded into ReleaseBuilderJob (release_build_job.rs) to produce the GitHub Actions matrix for matrixed builds.
setup_protoc() (steps/setup_protoc.rs) is referenced by release_build_job.rs and other build job constructors to ensure protoc is available before compilation steps.

create_draft_release_job (jobs/release_draft.rs / release_draft_pr.rs) exposes outputs (release id/tag) that downstream release_build_job or upload steps consume to attach artifacts to a GitHub Release.

bounty_job (jobs/bounty_job.rs) and label_sync_job (jobs/label_sync_job.rs) include job step sequences that expect a Node/npm runtime and specific Permissions; workflow generators (workflows/bounty.rs) assemble these jobs into scheduled and event-triggered workflows.
workflows modules (workflows/*.rs) import and re-export job constructors from jobs/mod.rs to assemble complete workflow YAML files written to .github/workflows by the generator.
notable_relationships: Generated workflow YAML is an upstream artifact consumed by GitHub Actions; changes here affect CI behavior across the repository and must align with GitHub Actions semantics and available actions in the marketplace.

Protoc and protobuf steps in CI are tied to crates that use proto-generated code (e.g., forge_repo); the protoc version and install method must be compatible with those crates' build.rs/proto expectations.

Release upload steps interact with GitHub Releases and external package repos (npm, Homebrew); the job outputs, artifact paths, and repository-level secrets/permissions are an external coupling point.

Node/TypeScript scripts run by CI require that the repo's package.json and Node artifacts exist and are compatible with the Node version used in CI jobs (implied cross-toolchain dependency outside the Rust workspace).

CI generation code depends on the gh_workflow (or equivalent) types/library for Job/Step/Workflow construction; changes to that library or its types will impact serialization/structure of generated YAML.
recommendations: When editing job or matrix types, run the workflow generator and validate the produced YAML (YAML linter / GitHub Actions local schema) to catch serialization or schema regressions.
Do not change job output names, permission scopes, or event trigger choices without updating all consumers; verify the draft-release -> build -> publish pipeline end-to-end after edits.

If you modify or remove setup_protoc or other reusable steps, verify that all jobs that require protoc/toolchains still include equivalent setup steps to avoid CI breakage for proto compilation and cross builds.

Any edits to jobs that run TypeScript/NPM scripts must preserve Node setup steps (or add them) and maintain the required permissions for issue/PR mutation; run the script steps locally or in a disposable runner to validate runtime assumptions.

Run the crate's tests and a full workspace cargo check/build; treat CI's strict settings (RUSTFLAGS -Dwarnings) as binding — ensure changes do not introduce warnings or build errors that CI would treat as failures.

crates/forge_display.description: This crate is the terminal-oriented display adapter for Forge: it converts raw textual outputs (markdown, code blocks, ripgrep-style search hits, and inline diffs) into colorized, width-aware terminal strings consumed by the CLI/TUI and streaming components. It matters because its formatting and placeholder/restore flows determine user-facing presentation, snapshot stability in tests, and runtime costs (e.g. syntax-highlighting initialization).
key_behaviors: Parse markdown input, extract fenced/indented code blocks to placeholders, render the markdown body, then restore syntax-highlighted code blocks into the output.
Perform terminal-friendly inline diff computation and formatting with line numbers, colored inline changes, and add/remove metrics while respecting terminal width constraints.
Parse ripgrep-style lines (path:line:content), group results by file, align and optionally highlight matched fragments; fall back to raw path output when input is not parseable.
Expose re-exported formatter types (MarkdownFormat, DiffFormat, GrepFormat) for other crates to call, keeping display concerns centralized.

insights.title: Syntax highlighter initialization can be expensive
insights.problem: Eager initialization of the SyntaxHighlighter caused unnecessary startup cost when highlighting was not required.
insights.root_cause: SyntaxHighlighter (syntect-backed) was initialized eagerly even for flows that don't use highlighting.
insights.solution: Ensure syntax highlighter is lazily initialized so highlighting cost is only incurred when needed; preserve thread-safety and deterministic initialization semantics.

insights.title: Code block extraction/restore must preserve indentation and language info
insights.problem: Incorrect extraction or placeholder substitution could break indentation, language attribution, or relative positioning when restoring highlighted blocks.
insights.root_cause: Extraction replaces code blocks with placeholders and later restores highlighted code; mismatches in placeholder tokens, indexing, or newline normalization can corrupt output.
insights.solution: Maintain a stable, collision-resistant placeholder scheme, preserve original indentation and language metadata in CodeBlock structures, and use the exact same normalization rules when restoring.

insights.title: Snapshot-sensitive formatting
insights.problem: Diff, grep and markdown formatters have unit tests and snapshot assertions; small string/whitespace changes break snapshots and CI.
insights.root_cause: Formatter outputs are deterministic and captured in snapshot tests; any change to spacing, width handling, coloring markers, or newline normalization will cause test failures.
insights.solution: When editing formatting logic, run and update snapshot tests deliberately; avoid unintentional alterations to newline/width/escape sequences and preserve deterministic output.

insights.title: Grep parsing must tolerate unexpected input
insights.problem: Input that is not strictly ripgrep-style should not cause crashes or misformatted output.
insights.root_cause: GrepFormat expects path:line:content lines and groups by file; non-matching lines need a safe fallback.
insights.solution: Keep and test the fallback path that prints raw file paths when parsing fails; ensure the parse function and formatters handle malformed or edge-case lines gracefully.

insights.title: Width calculations affect layout and readability
insights.problem: Incorrect width calculations or not honoring terminal width can break alignment for diffs and grouped grep output.
insights.root_cause: Formatting uses terminal width to compute line truncation and alignment for numbers and gutter columns.
insights.solution: Preserve current width-aware logic and validation; ensure any changes continue to compute and apply width constraints consistently across diff and grep formatters.

key_files.why: Core parser and syntax-highlighting primitives: extracts code blocks, defines placeholders, and performs syntax highlighting via SyntaxHighlighter used by MarkdownFormat.
High-level markdown rendering entrypoint: composes code extraction, markdown rendering (termimad) and final placeholder restore; central to output shown by the CLI/TUI.
Implements inline colored diffs with line numbers and add/remove metrics; consumers rely on its width and formatting semantics.
Parses and formats ripgrep-style search output; includes grouping, alignment, and optional regex highlighting with a safe fallback behavior.
Public crate surface that re-exports MarkdownFormat, DiffFormat and GrepFormat for other crates; changing this affects all consumers.

construct_relationships: CodeBlockParser in code.rs extracts fenced or indented code blocks into CodeBlock records and replaces them with placeholders; MarkdownFormat in markdown.rs calls CodeBlockParser::new and later CodeBlockParser::restore to re-insert highlighted blocks.

SyntaxHighlighter (code.rs) is used by MarkdownFormat to convert extracted CodeBlock content into terminal-colored strings; lazy initialization is important to avoid startup costs for flows that don't highlight.
MarkdownFormat::strip_excessive_newlines is applied before placeholder restore so that normalization of newline runs and placeholder indexing remain consistent during rendering.

DiffFormat (diff.rs) and GrepFormat (grep.rs) are independent formatting flows but share constraints: both compute and honor terminal width, require deterministic alignment/number formatting, and their outputs are re-exported by lib.rs for consumption by higher-level UI/streaming crates.
notable_relationships: The display crate is consumed by higher-level components (CLI/TUI and streaming crates such as forge_main, forge_stream and forge_markdown_stream) which expect stable, deterministic formatted strings for terminal output and snapshot testing.
The SyntaxHighlighter depends on syntect (or similar) which can add binary size and initialization cost; this has runtime and build implications for the final CLI binary.
Markdown formatting uses termimad for rendering; any changes to token boundaries or placeholder markers must remain compatible with termimad expectations.

Snapshot tests in this crate are part of the project's CI expectations; CI enforces -D warnings which means changes must compile warning-free and maintain snapshot compatibility unless intentionally updated.
recommendations: When editing formatters, run this crate's unit tests and snapshot tests (cargo test for the crate; run insta tests where applicable) to detect output regressions before committing.
Preserve lazy initialization semantics for the syntax highlighter; if modifying initialization, ensure thread-safety and deterministic behavior across tests and multi-threaded contexts.

Do not change placeholder token formats, indexing, or restore ordering without evaluating effects on markdown rendering and existing snapshots; ensure placeholder tokens cannot collide with user content.
Respect terminal width and alignment logic in diff and grep formatters — validate with a variety of terminal widths to avoid truncation/alignment regressions.
Maintain robust parsing fallbacks (especially in GrepFormat) so malformed or non-standard input does not crash or produce confusing output.
Keep the public re-exports in lib.rs stable; changes there affect all consumers across the workspace. Ensure any API changes are coordinated with downstream crates.
Avoid introducing new compiler warnings; CI treats warnings as errors. Run cargo check/clippy as part of verification.

crates/forge_fs.description: This crate provides an async, tokio-based filesystem abstraction (ForgeFS) used everywhere in the workspace to read, write, classify and hash files with consistent error semantics. It centralizes binary-vs-text detection, UTF-8-aware reads and inclusive line-range reads, metadata helpers, and a stable whole-file hash used by change-detection and indexing subsystems.
key_behaviors: Classify files as text or binary before reading/indexing using multiple detectors (BOM/zero-byte heuristics and sampled-type inference).
Read file contents in several modes (raw bytes, UTF-8 lossy, owned UTF-8 string) and return contextual filesystem errors.
Read inclusive line ranges with validation, UTF-8 handling, and whole-file hash computation for external change detection.
Write operations (create_dir_all, write, append, remove_file) that wrap tokio fs calls and attach contextual errors.
Provide metadata helpers (exists, is_file, read_dir, file_size) that use metadata rather than full reads when possible for efficiency.

insights.title: Multiple binary-detection strategies coexist
insights.problem: There are two different detection approaches: a BOM/zero-byte heuristic (binary_detection.rs) and a sampled-type inference using the infer crate (is_binary.rs). Inconsistencies between them can lead to divergent classification results depending on call sites and sample sizes.
insights.root_cause: Different modules implemented detection with different heuristics and trade-offs (fast heuristics vs. sampled MIME inference) without a single canonical decision point.
insights.solution: Treat these as complementary detectors and ensure callers use the intended one for their use case; when editing, preserve the documented behavior and contract of each detector and keep their public wiring stable so callers that rely on a particular behavior continue to get it.

insights.title: Whole-file hash used for external change detection
insights.problem: Range reads compute or surface a content hash; earlier implementations used range-only hashes which mismatched the external-change detector expecting a whole-file hash.
insights.root_cause: Mismatch between what consumers expect (stable, whole-file identifier) and what read-range code originally returned (range-limited hash).
insights.solution: Range-reading APIs must compute and return the whole-file hash (not the truncated range) so external detectors and indexing can reliably detect changes. Preserve this contract when modifying read_range logic or hash helpers.

insights.title: UTF-8 multi-byte and invalid-UTF8 resilience
insights.problem: Reading line ranges needs to correctly handle multi-byte UTF-8 boundaries, invalid UTF-8 bytes, and enforce caps on requested ranges.
insights.root_cause: Line/byte indexing and naive slicing can break multi-byte characters and return invalid or misaligned slices.
insights.solution: Read-range logic validates indices, caps at file length, uses UTF-8-aware slicing or lossy conversion where documented, and includes tests for multi-byte and invalid UTF-8 cases. Maintain these invariants when editing.

insights.title: Centralized, contextual filesystem errors
insights.problem: Filesystem operations require clear, contextual error messages and unified error types so higher layers can map, log, or present errors reliably.
insights.root_cause: Multiple async fs helpers and detectors share underlying I/O and validation failure modes; ad-hoc errors would complicate callers.
insights.solution: All operations surface the crate-level Error type with contextual messages and conversions. Preserve Error variants, textual context, and conversion behavior when changing signatures or control flow.

insights.title: Sample sizes and metadata-only operations for large files
insights.problem: Some detectors read up to a fixed sample (8192 bytes) and metadata-based helpers are used to avoid full reads; naive changes could cause full-file reads and heavy I/O for large workspaces.
insights.root_cause: Detection and classification trade off accuracy and performance; full reads are expensive across many files.
insights.solution: Respect existing sample-size bounds and metadata-based file_size/read_dir behaviors. Any change that increases read size or performs full-file reads should be evaluated for performance impact and tests should verify behavior on large files.

key_files.why: Defines the ForgeFS abstraction, central helpers (compute_hash) and re-exports errors and detection utilities — the public entrypoint other crates use.
Implements inclusive line-range reads and enforces hashing and UTF-8 semantics relied upon by change-detection and indexing logic.
Performs sampled-type inference (using the infer crate) and exposes ForgeFS is_binary APIs that callers use to decide whether to treat files as text or documents.
Provides fast BOM and zero-byte heuristics for binary detection used by other parts of the crate and in tests to avoid misclassifying encoded text as binary.
Defines the unified Error enum and conversions used throughout the crate to provide consistent, contextual error reporting.

construct_relationships: ForgeFS (lib.rs) exposes methods that delegate to binary_detection.rs and is_binary.rs for file classification (ForgeFS::is_binary_file → underlying detectors).
ForgeFS::read_range_utf8 (read_range.rs) reads a truncated view for lines but computes a whole-file SHA-256 via compute_hash (lib.rs) to produce FileInfo expected by external change-detectors.
Read helpers (read.rs) and write helpers (write.rs) wrap tokio fs functions and convert io::Error into the crate Error (error.rs) before returning to callers.
Meta helpers (meta.rs) use metadata-only operations (file_size, exists) to avoid full reads and call ForgeFS::is_binary_file when classification is required.

Binary detection heuristics (binary_detection.rs) and sampled inference (is_binary.rs) both feed classification results into reading/indexing flows but use different heuristics (fast BOM/zero-byte vs. infer-based sample matching).
notable_relationships: Indexing/embedding subsystems (e.g., crates/forge_embed or remote workspace indexers) depend on ForgeFS compute_hash and binary classification to decide what to send and how to detect changes.
Change-detection and any external workspace server clients expect the whole-file hash semantics returned by read-range APIs; mismatches can break synchronization or diff logic.
Higher-level services across the workspace (workspace walkers, conversation persistence, tools that load file contents) call ForgeFS methods; API and error shape changes will propagate widely.
The crate uses third-party crates (tokio, infer) whose behavior and performance characteristics affect classification and I/O; keep dependency contracts in mind when updating code or Cargo.toml.

CI and workspace-wide expectations (warnings as errors) mean edits must compile without warnings and tests must be preserved — this crate includes targeted tests for UTF-8, binary detection, and read-range edge cases.
recommendations: Preserve public API signatures and the documented semantics: ForgeFS methods, FileInfo/hashing semantics (whole-file hash), and Error variants must remain stable for downstream callers.

When editing detection logic, ensure both detectors' contracts remain intact (fast heuristic vs. sample-based) and verify classification behavior with existing tests that exercise different encodings and binary patterns.

Run the crate's unit tests (including read_range UTF-8 / invalid-UTF8 cases) and run cargo test --workspace if cross-crate effects are possible; CI treats warnings as errors so compilation must be warning-free.

Avoid unexpectedly increasing I/O for common ops: respect sample-size caps (8192 bytes), use metadata (file_size) instead of full reads where intended, and avoid full-file reads unless the API contract requires a whole-file hash.

Maintain contextual error messages and conversions; changing error shapes or text will affect logs and higher-level error handling. Ensure new errors map into the existing Error enum or provide clear conversions.

Preserve async/tokio usage and signatures (async functions, tokio fs calls). If changing implementation details, keep async behavior and performance expectations (non-blocking, efficient sampling) intact.

crates/forge_infra.description: crates/forge_infra is the workspace's concrete infrastructure layer: a collection of adapters that implement file I/O, directory walking, command execution, HTTP/gRPC clients, MCP connectivity, environment/config persistence, console I/O and key-value storage. It centralizes low-level side-effecting behavior behind infra traits so higher-level crates depend on stable implementations and composition (ForgeInfra). Changes here affect runtime I/O, network behavior, config persistence and concurrency characteristics across the application.
key_behaviors: Provision a single composed ForgeInfra that implements multiple infra traits (file reader/writer, directory reader, HTTP, gRPC, command executor, user prompts, env/config access, auth, MCP server/client).
Manage on-disk configuration discovery, caching, and atomic application of domain-level config mutations; derive a runtime Environment from config + runtime inputs.

Perform file system operations with batched and rate-limited readers, directory listing that respects ignore rules and filters, and safe writes/removals while ensuring parent directories and snapshot semantics are handled by callers.
Execute shell commands serially or in raw mode while streaming stdout/stderr to a thread-safe console writer.
Provide network clients: a reqwest-based HTTP infra with header sanitization, debug request logging and TLS config, and a lazily-initialized, cached tonic gRPC channel with hydrate/reset support.
Offer MCP connectivity over multiple transports (stdio/HTTP/SSE), header templating, retries and translation of raw remote responses into domain ToolOutput.
Store small JSON-serializable values in cacache with TTL and deterministic key hashing for ephemeral infra use.

insights.title: Cached ForgeConfig with explicit invalidation needed
insights.problem: ForgeConfig was read once and cached with no clear re-read path; after config mutations the in-memory cache could remain stale.
insights.root_cause: No centralized atomic apply-and-invalidate path for config mutations and environment resolution.
insights.solution: Provide a single ForgeEnvironmentInfra that caches config for reads, exposes cached_config, and invalidates/re-reads after apply_config_op to ensure callers observe persisted changes.

insights.title: Base path must be env-overridable
insights.problem: Base path resolution previously hard-coded to home_dir()/"forge", preventing runtime overrides.
insights.root_cause: Environment discovery ignored FORGE_CONFIG env var.
insights.solution: Make base path driven by FORGE_CONFIG and derive Environment from ForgeConfig/Option-backed values with defaults resolved so layering does not drop defaults.

insights.title: Directory reads can exhaust file descriptors without concurrency cap
insights.problem: Asynchronous directory reads previously used join_all over many file reads, causing EMFILE when walking large trees.
insights.root_cause: Unbounded parallel reads; no concurrency control in directory reader.
insights.solution: Introduce a concurrency cap (parallel_file_reads) to ForgeDirectoryReaderService and propagate it to directory reader construction so reads are rate-limited.

insights.title: Batched file reads need per-file error results
insights.problem: Batch reader previously returned a single Result for the whole batch, hiding per-file errors and making partial success handling difficult.
insights.root_cause: Batch API aggregated success/failure instead of emitting per-path results.
insights.solution: Change batched reader signature to yield per-file path/result pairs (stream of (PathBuf, Result<String, Error>)) so callers can process partial successes and failures.

insights.title: Request debugging and header sanitization must be consistent
insights.problem: Different providers duplicated header sanitization logic and debug request logging sometimes overwrote earlier chunks for streaming bodies.
insights.root_cause: Ad-hoc implementations and incomplete sensitive-header lists; debug logging overwrote instead of appending for streamed bodies.
insights.solution: Centralize sanitize_headers and expand sensitive header list; when debug_requests configured, append request body chunks to debug files rather than overwriting so full streaming payloads are preserved.

insights.title: Shared gRPC channel with lazy init and reset
insights.problem: Multiple clients create redundant gRPC channels or lacked a way to force reconnects.
insights.root_cause: No centralized, thread-safe channel wrapper that can be cheaply cloned and rehydrated.
insights.solution: Use a lazily-initialized ForgeGrpcClient that caches a tonic Channel, configures TLS for https endpoints, and exposes hydrate to reset the connection when needed.

insights.title: Concurrent command output must not interleave
insights.problem: Concurrent command execution could interleave stdout/stderr writes and corrupt terminal output.
insights.root_cause: Multiple threads writing to stdout/stderr without synchronization.
insights.solution: Provide a thread-safe StdConsoleWriter that wraps stdout/stderr in mutexes and is used by the command executor to stream output safely; tests assert behavior and allow injection of mock writers.

insights.title: ForgeInfra constructor must match updated per-service signatures
insights.problem: Changes to individual infra service signatures (e.g., FileReaderInfra read_batch_utf8, directory reader parallel parameter) must be reflected where ForgeInfra constructs them.
insights.root_cause: Central composition tracked older service constructors and did not propagate new parameters.
insights.solution: Ensure ForgeInfra::new and composition logic are updated to pass new parameters (parallel_file_reads, updated batched reader signatures) so trait implementations remain consistent.

key_files.why: Central composition of all infra services (ForgeInfra) used by higher-level crates; changes here alter how per-service implementations are wired and their constructor parameters.
Responsible for discovering runtime Environment, caching ForgeConfig, and atomically applying config mutations — edits affect config persistence and environment resolution across the app.
