Implements file reading and batched streaming API; signature changes here (per-file results) affect callers and ForgeInfra wiring.
Directory reader that enforces ignore rules and concurrency caps; behavior here dictates file descriptor usage and performance when scanning workspaces.
Central HTTP client with header sanitization, TLS handling, debug request logging and eventsource support; edits affect all outbound provider and MCP HTTP interactions.

construct_relationships: ForgeInfra composes implementations: ForgeFileReadService, ForgeFileWriteService, ForgeCreateDirsService, ForgeFileRemoveService, ForgeDirectoryReaderService, ForgeWalkerService, ForgeHttpInfra, ForgeGrpcClient, ForgeCommandExecutorService, ForgeMcpClient/McpServer, CacacheStorage, StdConsoleWriter and ForgeEnvironmentInfra, exposing them via infra traits.
ForgeCommandExecutorService uses StdConsoleWriter to stream stdout/stderr and serializes execution with an internal mutex; it also returns captured output buffers and exit codes to callers.

ForgeDirectoryReaderService uses ForgeFS and forge_walker for traversal, filters via glob patterns and ignore rules, and leverages the concurrency cap passed into its constructor to limit parallel file reads.
ForgeFileReadService supplies read_batch_utf8 as a Stream of per-file results; directory reader consumes it to read files in parallel with limits.

ForgeHttpInfra reads TLS/root-certs and debug_paths from ForgeEnvironmentInfra/ForgeConfig and sanitizes headers before logging; ForgeMcpClient resolves HTTP header templates and uses HttpInfra for HTTP/SSE MCP transports.

ForgeGrpcClient provides a lazily-initialized Channel used by any component requiring gRPC (e.g., workspace server clients); it supports hydrate to reset the cached connection when ForgeInfra or callers request reconnection.
notable_relationships: Other crates (crates/forge_main, forge_repo, forge_services, forge_embed, forge_app) depend on the infra traits and concrete types exported by this crate; signature or behavioral changes will ripple to callers.

Configuration persistence and runtime Environment depend on the FORGE_CONFIG env var and persisted ForgeConfig files; changing storage layout or path resolution affects all consumers of config and TLS/root certificate loading.

HTTP outgoing behavior (sanitization, debug logging, TLS roots) impacts provider integrations and workspace indexing; debug request file writes and any appended logs live on disk and may contain sensitive data unless sanitized.

Directory walking and file-reading changes directly influence workspace indexing and semantic search (forge_embed), and can affect performance tests such as zsh rprompt benchmarks if concurrency or IO semantics change.
MCP connectivity touches external MCP servers and tooling: header templating, retry behavior and transport selection (stdio/HTTP/SSE) are relied on by tooling integrations in other crates.
recommendations: When editing ForgeInfra composition (forge_infra.rs), keep constructor signatures in sync with per-service implementations (parallel_file_reads, updated read_batch_utf8 stream types) to avoid compile-time trait mismatches.

Respect and preserve config caching/invalidation semantics in env.rs: apply_config_op must persist and invalidate cached ForgeConfig so subsequent cached_config reads reflect updates; FORGE_CONFIG env var remains authoritative for base path resolution.
Maintain the per-file result streaming API for batched reads (read_batch_utf8 yields path -> Result) so callers can handle partial failures; update all call sites if the stream item shape changes.
Honor the concurrency cap for directory reads to avoid EMFILE; any change to parallel_file_reads should consider default values and tests that cover large-workspace behavior.

Ensure header sanitization and debug request logging remain centralized and append streaming chunks rather than overwrite; any change must preserve sensitive-header filtering and debug_paths behavior.

Preserve StdConsoleWriter's mutexed stdout/stderr behavior or ensure an equivalent synchronization when changing command executor logic to avoid interleaved terminal output; unit tests exist to validate this.
If changing gRPC channel caching/hydrate behavior, ensure clients able to force reconnect (hydrate) remain supported and concurrency limits for channel usage are not lowered unexpectedly.

Run the crate's unit tests and workspace tests after edits (cargo test --workspace), and run lints/compile checks (cargo check / cargo clippy). When making changes touching IO or network behavior, run tests that exercise directory traversal, batched reads, HTTP header templating, and MCP client tests to catch integration regressions.

crates/forge_main.description: This crate implements the forge CLI/TUI binary and is the top-level orchestration layer for interactive and one-shot runs. It defines the command surface, bootstraps runtime/platform quirks, wires configuration and provider/model overrides into API construction, and hosts the interactive loop, editor/completion plumbing, streaming output, and small utilities (sandbox, OAuth loopback, info/porcelain rendering). Changes here directly affect user-facing behavior, CLI semantics, and integration points with other crates (config, repo, services, fs).
key_behaviors: Parse CLI arguments, detect interactive vs piped input, and map top-level flags (provider/model/agent) into ForgeAPI and UI initialization.

Initialize and run the interactive event loop: render prompt, read input via reedline editor, perform completion, parse slash-style commands, and dispatch to command handlers or one-shot prompt processing.
Manage UI state and background tasks: hydrate caches, persist active agent/conversation, and coordinate spinner/streamed markdown output without interleaving.
Present structured, machine- and human-friendly output: convert domain objects into Info sections, flatten to Porcelain for tabular/machine output, and render aligned terminal views.

Support external flows and tooling: create/manage git sandboxes (worktrees), host a localhost OAuth callback for browser-based provider logins, and expose completion infrastructure for shell integrations (zsh plugin).

insights.title: Atomic provider+model overrides via CLI
insights.problem: Setting provider via CLI without an atomic model selection caused extra interactive prompts and mismatch between in-memory and persisted configuration when scripting.
insights.root_cause: Provider activation flows could complete independently of model selection and the CLI previously allowed provider/model to be set separately (and environment overrides were deprecated), causing inconsistent state when prompts were suppressed.
insights.solution: Treat provider and model overrides as an atomic input path during CLI parsing and ensure these flags are threaded into ForgeAPI/UI initialization so persisted config and in-memory state remain consistent.

insights.title: Background hydration race at short-lived UI commands
insights.problem: Background cache hydration tasks started by UI actions could race with process exit, producing join/await panics or unclean shutdowns for short-lived commands (e.g., info).
insights.root_cause: on_new() and similar initialization triggered background tasks unconditionally; short-lived commands returned before tasks completed and shutdown hooks did not await or cancel them safely.
insights.solution: Initialize background tasks selectively and coordinate their lifecycle with command duration, ensuring tasks are cancellable or joined/stopped on process exit to avoid panics.

insights.title: Info/Porcelain alignment and Unicode truncation
insights.problem: Columns/keys were misaligned across sections and truncation used byte lengths, which broke multibyte characters and caused display corruption or panics.
insights.root_cause: Ad-hoc formatting across UI modules led to inconsistent key widths; truncation logic relied on byte counts instead of character/grapheme counts.
insights.solution: Normalize key column widths per section, centralize porcelain/tabular formatting, and use character-aware (not byte) length calculations for truncation to preserve multi-byte Unicode output.

insights.title: Localhost OAuth loopback constraints
insights.problem: OAuth redirect handling requires careful host validation and timeout semantics to avoid accepting non-loopback hosts or hanging waiting for callbacks.
insights.root_cause: Loopback redirect flows need to bind to IPv4/IPv6/localhost variants and must enforce state and method validation plus timeouts; otherwise, credentials flows can stall or accept invalid responses.
insights.solution: Run a dedicated localhost listener that validates origin/state/method, supports IPv4/IPv6/localhost, enforces timeouts and shutdown semantics, and surfaces success/error HTML responses back to the browser.

key_files.why: Entry point: parses CLI, detects piped input, loads config, sets platform terminal behavior (Windows VT), and constructs the UI/ForgeAPI with threaded CLI overrides.
Canonical, strongly-typed CLI surface (clap) for all commands/flags; changes here alter user-facing command names, flags, and runtime override semantics consumed by main/UI.

Orchestration of interactive loop, subcommand handling, provider/model resolution, background hydration tasks, and spinner/stream coordination; core place where runtime lifecycles and concurrency interact.
Command registration and parsing layer: registers agent/workflow commands used for completion and parsing into SlashCommand values consumed by the UI and completers.
Centralized machine-friendly tabular conversion for Info output and truncation/sorting logic; impacts scripting compatibility and Unicode-safe display.

construct_relationships: main::main parses Cli and passes parsed flags and ForgeConfig into UI::init / ForgeAPI construction, so CLI flag semantics must be propagated into API initialization.
UI uses ForgeCommandManager (model.rs) to register commands and drive completions; CommandManager output is consumed by CommandCompleter and InputCompleter for tab completion.
Editor (editor.rs) and Console (input.rs) form the input path: ForgeEditor supplies user input and reedline signals which Console maps into SlashCommand values via ForgeCommandManager::parse.

Info (info.rs) converts Environment/Config/Metrics/Conversation domain objects into an Info representation which Porcelain (porcelain.rs) then flattens and formats for human-readable or machine-friendly output.

StreamingWriter/StreamDirectWriter (stream_renderer.rs) coordinate with UI's SharedSpinner to pause/resume spinner and stream markdown output without interleaving, so spinner state and writer flush semantics are coupled.

OAuth localhost callback server (oauth_callback.rs) is invoked by provider activation flows orchestrated via UI/provider code paths to complete browser-based OAuth logins and return authorization codes.
notable_relationships: This crate depends on forge_config for loading forge.yaml and defaults; changes to CLI flags or model/provider handling must remain compatible with configuration parsing in that crate.
Conversation selection/persistence implicates forge_repo: UI and ConversationSelector present and persist active conversation IDs that are stored by the repo crate.

Provider credential flows and model resolution interact with forge_services: provider login, activation, and model negotiation travel through services APIs and may trigger OAuth loopback flows implemented here.

Sandbox/worktree creation touches forge_fs and git worktree expectations; modifications to sandbox paths or creation semantics affect filesystem and git integration in other crates and user workflows.

Zsh plugin and external shell integrations depend on the CLI surface and output formatting (porcelain); changing command names, flags, or machine-readable output will impact the plugin and scripting consumers.
recommendations: When editing CLI shapes (cli.rs), ensure new/changed flags are threaded into main::run and UI/ForgeAPI construction so persisted config and runtime state remain consistent; preserve deprecated env-var behavior only if intentionally retained.

Coordinate background task lifecycle in UI (ui.rs) — make background hydrations cancellable or guarded to avoid panics on short-lived commands; verify clean shutdowns in one-shot modes and during tests.
Maintain Unicode-safe handling in Porcelain/Info (porcelain.rs, info.rs): use character/grapheme counts for truncation and preserve alignment logic that normalizes key widths per section.

Any change to completion or command parsing (model.rs, completer/*) must keep command name sanitization and reserved-word handling intact because shell plugin and interactive tab-completion rely on stable command strings.
Respect spinner/stream synchronization (stream_renderer.rs): ensure writers pause/resume the SharedSpinner and flush deterministically to avoid output races; test interactive streaming scenarios.

OAuth callback behavior (oauth_callback.rs) must continue to validate loopback hosts, state and enforce timeouts; changing these semantics affects provider login flows and tests that simulate OAuth redirects.

Run full workspace tests, clippy, and the CI presets: cargo test --workspace, cargo clippy --workspace, and be aware CI treats warnings as errors (RUSTFLAGS -Dwarnings). Also run any integration tests that exercise the interactive loop, OAuth flows, and porcelain output formatting.
When changing public re-exports in lib.rs, verify downstream crates that import Cli, TopLevelCommand, or the global TRACKER still compile and behave as before.

.github/scripts.description: This folder contains a small TypeScript toolset and its tests that compute and apply bounty-related label changes on GitHub issues and pull requests. It includes a lightweight GitHub REST wrapper, a rules engine that computes label/comment patches, CLI entry points to plan or apply patches for a single issue or all issues, and domain types and tests that verify rule and sync behavior. These scripts are intended to be run with a GitHub token and have direct side effects on repository issues/PRs and labels, so correctness and idempotence matter.
key_behaviors: Compute desired label and comment mutations for issues and PRs based on repository-specific bounty rules (value labels, claimed, rewarded, and links between PRs and issues).
Plan and optionally apply patches (label add/remove, comments) to a single issue or across all issues using a REST API abstraction over GitHub.
Resolve authentication tokens and perform authenticated GitHub REST operations (fetch issue/PR, list issues, add/remove labels, post comments).
Propagate bounty state from issues to linked pull requests (and vice versa) according to defined rules, and generate a patch describing changes instead of mutating in place.
Unit-test rule computations and sync flows using a mock API to validate idempotence and expected label sets.

insights.title: Patch-computation must be deterministic and idempotent
insights.problem: Label and comment patches are computed and then applied; if compute logic or patch application is non-deterministic, repeated runs can produce confusing churn on issues/PRs.
insights.root_cause: Rules encode pattern matching (regex for value labels, label prefixing) and produce label add/remove operations; mismatch between detection and application semantics or order can cause unnecessary add/remove cycles.
insights.solution: Ensure computeIssuePatch/computePrPatch produce a minimal set of label ops reflecting desired final state and that sync functions apply patches idempotently. Tests already validate this behavior and should be kept in sync with any rule changes.

insights.title: Scripts run with GitHub credentials and modify repository state
insights.problem: The CLI will add/remove labels and post comments using an authenticated REST client; incorrect token handling or execution context can leak secrets or produce unintended mutations.
insights.root_cause: Token resolution is part of sync-issue.ts (resolveToken) and the GitHub REST wrapper performs mutating operations; these scripts can be run in CI or by maintainers locally.
insights.solution: Respect existing token-resolution semantics and permission expectations. Any edits must preserve how tokens are sourced/resolved and the authenticated client usage, and tests should continue to mock the API to avoid making live changes.

insights.title: Tight coupling between rules, types, and REST client
insights.problem: Rules operate over typed issue/PR structures and rely on particular fields (labels, state, body, linked numbers). Changing types or API shapes without updating rules/sync will break coordination.
insights.root_cause: The rules, sync entrypoints, and the GitHub REST wrapper share domain types defined in types.ts and expect specific label naming conventions.
insights.solution: When modifying domain types or REST shape, update all dependent rule and sync logic and their tests. Maintain consistency of label naming regexes (VALUE_LABEL_RE) and the label prefixes used across code and tests.

insights.title: Mock-driven tests validate business logic but must mirror real API behavior
insights.problem: Unit tests use a makeMockApi to simulate GitHub responses; if mocks diverge from the real GitHub REST responses or behavior (pagination, case sensitivity), false confidence can occur.
insights.root_cause: Mocks and real API responses can differ in shape or edge cases (e.g., label casing, missing fields, linked issue extraction from PR bodies).
insights.solution: Keep the mock API and test fixtures aligned with the actual REST wrapper expectations and GitHub response shapes; when making changes that affect API calls, update mocks and tests accordingly.

insights.title: External effects and rate/permission constraints
insights.problem: Sync-all and sync-issue can perform many API calls and mutating operations which may be subject to GitHub rate limits and required repository permissions.
insights.root_cause: Scripts iterate over issues, list by labels/prefixes, and apply label patches/comments; running them at scale or without appropriate scopes can fail or produce partial updates.
insights.solution: Edits must preserve or explicitly handle pagination/iteration semantics and be conscious of the potential volume of API calls; maintain any existing retry/ordering or batching behavior and the expectation that the caller provides appropriately scoped credentials.

key_files.why: Central GitHub REST wrapper used by the sync CLIs to fetch and mutate issues/PRs; changes affect all network interactions and how tests mock the API.
Encodes the core business logic for computing label/comment patches (value detection, claimed/rewarded rules); most behavioral changes to bounty semantics live here.
Per-issue CLI entrypoint that resolves tokens, plans and optionally applies a patch; coordinates token resolution, patch computation, and patch application.
Orchestrates planning/applying patches across multiple issues and relies on pagination/iteration and the REST wrapper; changes impact bulk operations.
Shared domain types for issues, PRs, labels, and patches; must remain consistent across api, rules, sync code, and tests.

construct_relationships: GitHubRestApi (api.ts) provides fetch and mutate primitives that sync-issue.ts and sync-all-issues.ts call to retrieve Issue/PR objects and apply Label/Comment operations.
rules.ts exports computeIssuePatch and computePrPatch which accept typed Issue/PR objects (types.ts) and produce a Patch (types.ts) consumed by sync entrypoints to apply changes.

sync-all-issues.ts iterates over issues (via GitHubRestApi.listIssuesWithLabelPrefix) and delegates per-item planning to the same patch-computation logic used by sync-issue.ts to ensure consistent behavior across single and bulk operations.
Tests construct mock API instances that implement the same call signatures as GitHubRestApi to validate rule outputs and sync flows without hitting the real GitHub API.
notable_relationships: These scripts interact with the live GitHub repository state (labels, issues, PRs) and are likely invoked from CI workflows or maintainer-run automation; changes can have repository-wide effects.
Authentication depends on environment-provided tokens (resolveToken) or CI-provided secrets such as GITHUB_TOKEN; any change to token handling affects how the scripts are executed in CI and locally.

Label naming conventions (VALUE_LABEL_RE, BOUNTY_* prefixes) are a cross-repo convention: external tooling or workflows (outside this folder) and repository label sets may assume these exact names/regexes.
Tests and other repository scripts outside this folder may rely on the exported CLIs or helper functions; modifying exports or call signatures will require coordinated updates.
recommendations: Preserve the public function signatures and domain types (types.ts) or update all consumers (api, rules, sync, and tests) together to avoid type/behavior mismatches.
When editing logic that computes labels or label-name regexes, update corresponding unit tests (rules.test.ts and sync tests) because tests encode expected label sets and idempotence.

Do not change token-resolution or REST client mutation semantics without validating how CI and local runs obtain and scope credentials; ensure tests continue to mock the API rather than performing live mutations.

Be mindful of side effects: sync scripts mutate repository state (labels/comments). Any code changes should be validated against the mock-based tests and, if needed, a dry-run mode to confirm planned patches before applying.

Verify bulk-operation behavior (sync-all-issues) against expected pagination and rate-limit scenarios; maintain existing iteration and batching assumptions so large repositories are handled predictably.

crates/forge_app.description: crates/forge_app is the application core that composes domain objects, infra services and provider clients to execute end-to-end agent/chat flows, tool dispatch/execution, compaction and model queries. It defines the runtime contracts (infra/services), orchestrates the request/response lifecycle (including tool calls and streaming events), and transforms tool-level results into UI-facing outputs — changes here affect end-to-end behavior, provider selection, and persisted conversation state.
key_behaviors: Initialize and run chat flows: load conversations and config, resolve agent-specific provider/model/tool definitions, build context and invoke the orchestrator to stream ChatResponse events.
Orchestrate agent turns: build context, call provider chat APIs, apply transformers, run tool calls (parallel Task vs sequential system tools), emit lifecycle events, handle retries and streaming.
Resolve, validate and dispatch tools: determine target executor (built-in, delegated agent, MCP), enforce restricted-mode and modality/policy checks, apply timeouts and permission checks.

Execute tools and materialize outputs: normalize paths, enforce read-before-edit semantics, call service-layer implementations, produce ToolOperation values, update metrics and produce ToolOutput/Element structures.
Compact/persist conversation contexts: summarize long message sequences while preserving usage metrics and the last non-empty reasoning details, then persist compacted state.

insights.title: Config must be available at runtime and per-agent
insights.problem: Earlier flows threaded global config through many constructors or assumed a single active config; callers sometimes used outdated cached config or lacked access to ForgeConfig.
insights.root_cause: Configuration was not consistently exposed via infra/services traits; some modules relied on implicit global state or constructor-injected config that became stale after refactors.
insights.solution: Respect the EnvironmentInfra/get_config contract or inject ForgeConfig at boundaries where needed; when modifying call sites ensure per-agent overrides are consulted (agent-specific provider/model and tunables).

insights.title: Agent-scoped provider/model selection
insights.problem: Flows previously always used a global active provider or model for chat and suggestions, causing expensive or incorrect provider usage and prevented per-agent overrides.
insights.root_cause: Agent execution and suggestion code depended on a single active provider/model rather than resolving AgentId-specific settings.
insights.solution: Treat provider and model selection as agent-scoped operations via AgentProviderResolver; when changing call signatures or call sites, ensure AgentId (not raw String) is passed and per-agent defaults are applied.

insights.title: Compaction must preserve usage metrics and reasoning continuity
insights.problem: Compaction replaced message sequences with summaries but lost accumulated usage metrics and sometimes broke reasoning continuity or duplicated reasoning_details across compactions.
insights.root_cause: Compaction pipeline discarded per-message usage aggregation and did not correctly extract and attach the last non-empty structured reasoning into the remaining assistant frame.
insights.solution: Accumulate and transfer usage metrics to the summary entry and inject the last non-empty reasoning_details into the post-compaction context; ensure transformers operate on ContextSummary and preserve metrics bookkeeping.

insights.title: Normalize paths and report correct file metadata
insights.problem: Relative paths were handled inconsistently; read outputs sometimes reported sliced content line counts as total_lines, and internal IDs leaked into serialized outputs.
insights.root_cause: Tool calls accepted relative paths and services returned sliced/truncated content without consolidating full metadata; serialization exposed internal identifiers and separate range fields.
insights.solution: Normalize relative paths to absolute using environment cwd at executor boundaries, report actual file total_lines (not trimmed slice counts), use FileInfo metadata for read outputs, and avoid exporting internal IDs.

insights.title: Evolving infra signatures require coordinated updates
insights.problem: Signatures changed (e.g., EnvironmentInfra.get_config, read_batch_utf8) and call sites/tests failed because the updated behavior (streaming individual file results, returning Config type) was not propagated.
insights.root_cause: Trait refactors altered return shapes and semantics; many callers and tests assumed the old result shapes.
insights.solution: When changing infra/service traits propagate signature changes to all implementers and consumers (including test harnesses), pass parallel_file_reads and config-derived params explicitly where needed, and update tests to use new types (FileInfo, Config).

key_files.why: Top-level application orchestrator that loads conversations/config, resolves per-agent providers/models, and starts chat/compaction flows — central entrypoint for chat-related behaviors.
Core orchestrator implementing the agent request/response loop, lifecycle events, tool execution semantics (parallel vs sequential), retry logic and streaming — controls runtime policy for turns.
Resolves and validates tool calls (built-in, agent-delegated, MCP), enforces permissions/modality/timeouts, and dispatches to the appropriate executor — central for any tool dispatch changes.
Bridges tool inputs to service calls, enforces read-before-edit normalization, handles truncation/temp files and updates metrics — responsible for file/path normalization and metrics correctness.
Defines infra/service trait contracts consumed throughout the crate (file IO, env, HTTP, MCP, user IO) — changes here propagate broadly and require coordinated implementation updates.

construct_relationships: ForgeApp builds Conversation/Context, uses AgentProviderResolver to resolve provider/model for a given AgentId, applies ApplyTunableParameters, and delegates execution to Orchestrator.
Orchestrator composes provider chat calls (via Services traits), applies transformer pipeline, and routes tool call requests to ToolRegistry (which validates and dispatches).

ToolRegistry decides dispatch target and forwards to ToolExecutor for Forge built-in tools, or to AgentExecutor/McpExecutor for delegated tools; it also enforces restricted-mode and modality checks and applies timeouts.

ToolExecutor calls lower-level Services/Infra traits (FileReaderInfra, FileWriterInfra, CommandInfra, etc.), creates ToolOperation values (operation.rs), and relies on operation.rs to convert those into ToolOutput elements and update Conversation Metrics.

Compactor consumes Conversation message sequences and TemplateEngine output to create ContextSummary, then writes compacted state back to conversation persistence (Services Conversation APIs) while preserving usage metrics and last reasoning details.
notable_relationships: This crate depends on domain types in forge_domain for ToolCatalog, ToolOperation, Conversation, Context and FileInfo — changes to domain types require matching updates here (formatting and serialization).

Infra/service traits here must be implemented by runtime crates (main binary or test harness) and by provider clients in crates/forge_services; trait changes require coordinated updates across those crates and tests.

Serialization DTOs (dto/anthropic/request.rs) map internal Context into provider-specific request shapes; any changes to Context or reasoning config must be reflected in these DTOs to preserve provider compatibility.

Formatter and ToolOperation outputs are consumed by UI/streaming crates (forge_stream, forge_markdown_stream, forge_display); modifications to output shapes or metadata (e.g., FileInfo fields, IDs) affect UI rendering and snapshots/tests.

Test harnesses under orch_spec and other tests depend on particular fixture fields (parallel_file_reads, FileInfo shapes); infra and service signature changes will require test fixture updates across the workspace.
recommendations: When editing public traits (infra/services) or DTOs, update all implementers and test fixtures in tandem; expect compile failures across multiple crates if signatures change.
Preserve per-agent provider/model resolution: pass AgentId (not raw String) through call chains and use AgentProviderResolver to apply agent-level overrides.
Maintain compaction invariants: ensure accumulated usage metrics are preserved and last non-empty reasoning_details are injected into the reduced context.

Enforce path normalization and correct file metadata: normalize relative paths at ToolExecutor boundaries and report true total_lines and FileInfo-based metadata (avoid counting truncated slice lines).

Respect concurrency/config options (parallel_file_reads, tool timeouts, Task parallelization) and ensure they are passed explicitly from config or EnvironmentInfra/get_config to detectors, executors and tests.

Run the workspace test suite and update snapshot/fixture files after changes (formatter tests, orch_spec tests, changed_files and compacting tests) — CI treats warnings as errors so ensure code is warning-free.

crates/forge_domain.description: The forge_domain crate is the canonical domain model for the Forge workspace: it defines typed, serializable representations and runtime helpers for agents, conversations, messages, providers, credentials, tools, workspace/indexing nodes, compaction algorithms, and repository contracts. This crate centralizes the wire-format and in-memory semantics that the CLI, services, storage, indexer, and UI depend on, so maintaining backwards-compatible serialization, stable contracts, and consistent runtime helpers is critical for cross-crate interoperability.
key_behaviors: Model and serialize/deserialize domain entities (agents, messages, conversations, providers, credentials, index nodes, tool calls) for on-disk persistence and network exchange.
Provide runtime environment and session helpers (paths, workspace hashing, session config) used by other crates to locate artifacts (logs, snapshots, credentials, skills).
Describe and enforce tooling schema for agent-invoked tools (ToolCatalog) and normalize tool identity for robust tool dispatch.
Manage authentication flows and credential lifecycle (API keys, OAuth/device flows, token expiry and refresh metadata).
Represent streaming agent events and tool-call lifecycle signals used by orchestrators and UIs to coordinate execution and display.
Compute and apply compaction policies/strategies on conversation contexts while preserving tool-call atomicity and reasoning semantics.

insights.title: Centralize artifact path helpers
insights.problem: Credentials and other runtime artifact paths were constructed at multiple call sites leading to duplicated logic and fragile tests.
insights.root_cause: No centralized, testable helper exposed canonical artifact locations relative to base_path or cwd.
insights.solution: Provide Environment helpers (e.g., credentials_path) and session-level path utilities so callers use a single source of truth for locating runtime artifacts.

insights.title: Template providers may have fixed URLs
insights.problem: Template provider URL accessor returned None for providers that had a fixed url string but empty url_params, hiding usable API endpoints.
insights.root_cause: The url() accessor treated template providers uniformly and only returned URLs when parameterization metadata existed.
insights.solution: Return a usable URL when template providers include a fixed url string even if url_params is empty so callers can reliably detect configured endpoints.

insights.title: Tool name and Todo semantics must be normalized for LLM variability
insights.problem: LLM outputs used inconsistent casing/whitespace for tool names and exposed full server-side Todo records to models, causing lookup failures and semantic mismatch with incremental update semantics.
insights.root_cause: Tool names were matched verbatim and todo objects exposed server IDs and fields not meaningful to the model.
insights.solution: Normalize tool names (trim + case-insensitive) and expose content-keyed TodoItem shapes to the model so tool dispatch and incremental updates remain robust across varied LLM outputs.

insights.title: Attachment content must carry content hashes and consolidated metadata
insights.problem: File/content attachments lacked a content hash and carried duplicated/redundant fields for line ranges and counts across the codebase.
insights.root_cause: Attachment types evolved without centralizing file-range metadata and content integrity details.
insights.solution: Consolidate file-range metadata into a FileInfo and add content_hash to FileContent attachments so callers can de-duplicate, verify, and diff content reliably.

insights.title: Avoid duplicated or invalid reasoning when compaction or model changes occur
insights.problem: Raw reasoning text and structured reasoning_details could both be appended, producing duplicate thinking blocks (or null signatures) and resulting in invalid requests for some models; reasoning was also reused across different models.
insights.root_cause: append_message logic and compaction/compaction-signature handling did not consider presence of structured reasoning or model-specific signatures.
insights.solution: Detect and avoid duplicating reasoning blocks when structured reasoning_details exist and treat reasoning signatures as model-specific to prevent invalid reuse across model changes.

insights.title: Tool call events must carry notifier/coordination metadata
insights.problem: ToolCallStart previously embedded full ToolCall payloads but lacked separate notifier coordination, complicating UI/tool execution coordination.
insights.root_cause: Tool call event structure did not separate execution coordination metadata from payloads.
insights.solution: Change ToolCallStart to carry a notifier and normalize enum style so orchestrators and UIs can coordinate start/end and retries reliably.

key_files.why: Crate root and API re-export: the single entry point other crates import to access all domain types and helpers; changes here affect public surface exported to the workspace.

Centralized runtime Environment and SessionConfig helpers used to compute canonical paths (credentials, logs, snapshots, agents, skills) and workspace hashing; many callers rely on these paths being stable and testable.

Core message and Context types used when building provider requests, estimating tokens, serializing conversation state, and implementing compaction; subtle changes impact provider requests and compaction logic.
Defines the canonical tool wire-format (ToolCatalog) and tool input/output types consumed by agents and executors; LLM-facing schemas and tool name normalization live here.

Domain-level repository trait contracts (snapshots, conversations, providers, index) that all persistence and integration layers implement; trait changes require coordinated updates in implementing crates.

construct_relationships: Environment (env.rs) exposes credential_path, logs, snapshots and agents folder locations that repository implementations (traits in repo.rs) and runtime components rely on for persistence and discovery.
Context and Message types (context.rs) are the canonical inputs to provider chat requests and are referenced by compaction logic (compact/*) to decide eviction ranges and token accounting.

ToolCatalog and tool input structs (tools/catalog.rs) are consumed by Agent definitions (agent.rs) and by ChatResponse tool-call lifecycle events (chat_response.rs) to coordinate tool invocation and result handling.

Provider types and ModelSource (provider.rs) pair with credential types (auth/credentials.rs and new_types.rs) to represent configured providers and to drive authentication flows modeled by AuthContext types (auth/auth_context.rs).
Indexing Node/File types (node.rs) and WorkspaceAuth map to the WorkspaceIndexRepository trait (repo.rs) used by external indexer implementations and the forge_embed/workspace server clients.
CompactionStrategy (compact/strategy.rs) relies on Context and Message boundaries (context.rs) to preserve tool-call atomicity when computing eviction ranges for conversation contexts.
notable_relationships: forge_main (CLI/TUI) depends on forge_domain for Agent, Context, ChatResponse, ToolCatalog and Environment helpers to build requests, display events, and locate runtime artifacts.

Persistence crates (forge_repo and any Conversation/Snapshot implementations) implement the repository traits declared in repo.rs; changing trait signatures requires coordinated updates across implementations.

Service integrations (forge_services, provider clients) depend on provider.rs and auth/* types for configuring endpoints, credentials, and authentication flows; wire-format changes affect network interoperability.

Workspace/indexing components (forge_embed, external workspace server) exchange Node/File types defined in node.rs; these types are serialized to APIs and must remain compatible with indexer expectations.

UI and orchestrator crates (forge_display, forge_stream, forge_markdown_stream) consume ChatResponse and tool-call events; modifications to event shapes alter streaming and rendering behavior across the UI stack.
recommendations: Preserve backwards-compatible serde/wire formats: when adding fields to serializable types, provide defaults and #[serde(default)] where appropriate to avoid breaking on-disk and network consumers.
Any change to repository traits (repo.rs) or public type signatures must be coordinated with implementing crates and accompanied by workspace-wide compilation and tests.
When editing auth, provider, or credential types, ensure token expiry/refresh semantics and display/truncation behavior remain intact; these types are used across login flows and provider clients.
Be careful when modifying Context/Message/compaction logic: compaction must preserve tool-call atomicity and avoid creating duplicate reasoning blocks; changes can affect provider request validity.

Run the full Rust workspace verification after edits: cargo check --workspace, cargo test --workspace, and cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings (CI treats warnings-as-errors). Re-run any snapshot tests (cargo insta) and regenerate protobuf artifacts if proto-adjacent types are touched.
Respect Environment path semantics and versioning: tests rely on predictable credentials_path, snapshots, and workspace_hash behavior; changing these may require updates to callers and tests.
Any change to ToolCatalog or tool name handling must account for LLM variability (whitespace/casing) and for model-facing schemas used by agents and executors.

crates/forge_services.description: This crate is the application service layer that adapts lower-level infra (file IO, command runner, walker, provider repo, credential store) into domain services consumed by the Forge application. It composes a central ForgeServices container and many focused adapters (workspace indexing/search bridge, provider/auth, attachments, discovery, policy, conversation, command loader, agent registry) that enforce cross-cutting invariants the rest of the system depends on (hashing semantics, discovery/fallback, credential lifecycle, persistence).
key_behaviors: Compose runtime services on top of an EnvironmentInfra implementation and expose them via the forge_app::Services trait.
Discover workspace files (git ls-files preferred) and fall back to a filesystem walker, filter paths (extensions, symlinks), and stream sync progress to a remote indexer via a sync engine.
Resolve provider templates and orchestrate provider authentication/credential refresh flows, persisting refreshed credentials back to storage.
Prepare attachments by resolving file tags/URLs to file or directory attachments, reading either raw bytes for images or ranged UTF-8 text while preserving full-file hashes.
Load and cache runtime artifacts (commands, agents, AGENTS.md instructions, conversations, policy) and expose read/update operations with appropriate caching and persistence semantics.
Evaluate policy decisions, prompt the user when required, and optionally persist policy rules to disk.

insights.title: Commit/Suggest model configs must be persisted in AppConfigService
insights.problem: App-level commit/suggest model settings were not persisted or accessible consistently across services.
insights.root_cause: forge_app.AppConfig previously lacked storage/accessors for commit/suggest configs; infra writes were not routed through an adapting service.
insights.solution: AppConfigService adapts infra (ForgeConfig) and ProviderRepository, reading defaults from on-disk infra and applying updates via infra.update_environment so commit/suggest configs are persisted and consistently readable.

insights.title: Attachment content hash must reflect raw full-file contents
insights.problem: Attachment service hashed line-range content (or transformed content), producing mismatches with the external-change detector and causing false 'modified externally' warnings.
insights.root_cause: Range-reading transformed the content before hashing and stored that hash; the detector hashed raw on-disk bytes.
insights.solution: AttachmentService now uses the full-file hash returned by range_read_utf8 (or otherwise computes/stores raw full-file hash) and stores that as content_hash so detectors and attachment consumers agree about file identity.

insights.title: Git discovery must surface errors and have walker fallback
insights.problem: git ls-files errors were swallowed or returned empty results, making failures hard to diagnose and occasionally producing no-file discovery on valid repositories.
insights.root_cause: A single discovery path collapsed discovery, reading and error handling into one streaming function and suppressed git errors.
insights.solution: File discovery pipeline separates discovery and reading, explicitly returns git errors, and uses walker fallback when git fails or is not available. Maintain clear error propagation so calling code can surface diagnostics to users.

insights.title: Exclude symlinks and filter by allowed extensions
insights.problem: Symlink targets and disallowed extension files were being included in indexing/sync, which can lead to indexing unexpected files or permissioned content.
insights.root_cause: filter_and_resolve previously included symlink targets and did not consistently exclude them across discovery implementations.
insights.solution: Discovery utilities enforce ALLOWED_EXTENSIONS and explicitly exclude symlinks/dangling links; git fallback/walker implementations cooperate with the same filter logic.

insights.title: Google ADC marker handling must recognize Vertex-like providers
insights.problem: Google ADC (Application Default Credentials) marker handling was only implemented for a single VERTEX_AI provider constant, causing other Vertex variants to use incorrect flows.
insights.root_cause: Provider-auth code matched only one provider enum/identifier for Google ADC and did not generalize to other Vertex-like provider identifiers.
insights.solution: ProviderAuthService records and treats the Google ADC marker across Vertex-like providers so refresh/init flows choose the correct auth strategy; edits must preserve or extend this recognition.

insights.title: Active agent choice should be session-scoped
insights.problem: Setting the active agent previously wrote to persistent app config, making the choice global across sessions and causing unnecessary disk writes.
insights.root_cause: Active-agent persistence was implemented at the same level as config persistence rather than as an in-memory session state.
insights.solution: AgentRegistry now keeps active agent in-memory for the session and loads agent definitions from repository only when needed; callers should rely on session-scoped active-agent semantics.

insights.title: Command loading requires clear precedence and embedded builtin loading
insights.problem: Built-in commands embedded in binary were not consistently loaded and precedence between built-in and custom commands needed clarity.
insights.root_cause: Command loader did not always initialize built-in commands from embedded markdown and the override resolution order across global/local/CWD was ambiguous.
insights.solution: CommandLoaderService explicitly initializes built-in commands via an init_default path and resolves conflicts by defined precedence (CWD highest).

key_files.why: Central composition container that wires concrete service implementations to an EnvironmentInfra and exposes the forge_app::Services API — changes here alter how all services are instantiated and forwarded.
WorkspaceService bridge that orchestrates discovery, sync, and semantic search and delegates to WorkspaceSyncEngine; changes affect indexing, sync progress streaming, and error propagation.
Attachment preparation, path resolution, range reads, and content-hash semantics live here; edits impact external-change detection and downstream consumers of attachments.
Provider authentication flows and credential refresh logic are implemented here; updates affect provider login, completion, refresh behavior and persisted credentials.
Discovery filtering and symlink/extension rules are enforced here and used by both git and walker discovery; modifications change which files get indexed/synced.

construct_relationships: ForgeServices composes concrete service implementations (ForgeWorkspaceService, ForgeProviderService, ForgeAuthService, ForgePolicyService, ForgeConversationService, ForgeAgentRegistryService, etc.) on top of a supplied EnvironmentInfra and re-exports them via forge_app::Services.

ForgeWorkspaceService (context_engine) uses FileDiscovery implementations (FdDefault which delegates to FsGit or FdWalker) to discover files, then delegates sync/upload/streaming work to WorkspaceSyncEngine while querying infra for credentials and workspace metadata.

AttachmentService resolves FileTag paths using infra.get_environment().cwd and infra.range_read_utf8, producing Attachments whose content_hash must match the full-file hash expected by the external-change detector elsewhere in the system.

ProviderAuthService and ForgeProviderService both interact with ProviderRepository and credential storage: ProviderService renders provider URL templates and delegates model/chat calls, while ProviderAuthService handles auth flows and writes credentials back to the repository/storage.
AgentRegistry loads agent definitions from an AgentRepository, caches them in-memory, and exposes the active agent id as session state rather than persisting it via AppConfigService.
notable_relationships: Relies on infra traits and on-disk config managed by crates/forge_config and the underlying EnvironmentInfra implementation (for reading/writing forge.yaml, credentials, and policy files).
Workspace sync and semantic search interact with external indexing/search backends (workspace server URL) and require proper credential handling from the provider/credential store.
File discovery depends on git being present (invoked via CommandInfra) but must gracefully degrade to walker-based discovery; changes here affect workspace indexing across the entire application.

Provider authentication flows and credential refresh affect external providers (OpenAI, Vertex, etc.) and may change network/API usage patterns; provider templates are resolved before delegating to ProviderRepository and may involve URL templating and environment/config interpolation.
AGENTS.md discovery and CommandLoaderService prefer project-local files (git root / CWD) which means edits to discovery logic change what instructions/commands are surfaced to agents.
recommendations: When editing service composition (forge_services.rs), preserve construction order and forwarding semantics — ForgeServices is the single point that wires infra to all domain services.

Any change touching AttachmentService must preserve full-file hashing semantics (use the full-file hash returned by range_read_utf8) to avoid breaking external-change detection; validate with tests that compare detector and attachment hashes.

When modifying discovery or fd.* files ensure symlink exclusion, allowed-extension filtering, and git->walker fallback remain consistent and that git errors are surfaced instead of being silently ignored.

Provider auth and provider_service edits must respect existing Google ADC/Vertex-like provider handling and credential refresh semantics; ensure refreshed credentials are written back to the provider repository/storage the same way current code expects.
Changing AgentRegistry behavior must retain session-scoped active-agent semantics (no implicit persistent writes) unless callers and config layers are also updated in tandem.
Run the crate test suite (cargo test -p forge_services) and the workspace tests after edits; be mindful CI treats warnings as errors (RUSTFLAGS -Dwarnings) and ensure no new warnings are introduced.
Keep changes compatible with infra trait contracts (range_read_utf8, CommandInfra git invocations, ProviderRepository APIs) because many services adapt or forward to these infra implementations.

templates.description: This folder contains markdown prompt templates that the agent runtime composes into system/user/tool messages for LLM-driven workflows: generating shell commands, commit messages, agent behavior scaffolds, partial summary frames, tool error reflection prompts, retry guidance, and short title generation. These templates are a critical glue layer between code (tooling, skills, and execution safeguards) and LLM output: changes to them directly alter agent behavior, tooling invocation formats, and user-facing responses.
key_behaviors: Render templates into system/user/tool messages that are sent to LLM providers as part of a conversation turn.
Compose partial templates into larger prompts (e.g., system info + summary frame + tool examples) to shape multi-message agent interactions.
Provide structured examples (JSON tags) that define expected tool-call shapes and are parsed/validated against tool schemas at runtime.
Produce executable artifacts (shell commands, commit messages, titles) that downstream components may run, display, or persist.

insights.title: Placeholders and structural invariants must remain valid
insights.problem: Templates contain placeholders, tags, and structured examples; inadvertent edits can break parsing, injection boundaries, or template composition.
insights.root_cause: Templates are text artifacts relied upon by prompt composition code and parsers expecting specific markers or JSON snippets.
insights.solution: When editing keep placeholder tokens, markup conventions, and example JSON intact and syntactically valid; validate composed prompts and parsing code paths that consume them.

insights.title: Tool usage examples must match tool/interface schemas
insights.problem: Examples in templates (notably forge-partial-tool-use-example.md) provide JSON examples used as canonical tool call shapes; mismatches cause runtime errors or malformed tool calls.
insights.root_cause: Templates are authoritative examples but are separate from tool descriptors (.mcp.json, SKILL.md) and code-level type definitions, so they can diverge.
insights.solution: Ensure any edits preserve JSON example conformance to the tool interfaces and keep the examples synchronized with MCP/skill descriptors and runtime validation logic.

insights.title: Prompt wording affects downstream safety and execution
insights.problem: Small wording changes can change LLM output style, riskier command suggestions, or more verbose outputs which downstream execution/safety guards must handle.
insights.root_cause: Templates directly define prompts sent to LLMs; the models’ behavior depends sensitively on phrasing and examples.
insights.solution: Treat template edits as behavioral changes: run end-to-end scenarios and check safety/execution guard paths (shell execution, dry-run modes) and update any dependent tests or snapshot expectations.

insights.title: Templates are surfaced in tests and snapshots
insights.problem: Changes to templates can break snapshot tests or expected outputs used by integration tests and developer workflows.
insights.root_cause: Automated tests and snapshot assertions assume stable prompt wording and examples.
insights.solution: When editing, update or rebaseline snapshot/integration tests intentionally, and run the project's test suite (including any insta snapshot tests) to validate behavioral changes.

insights.title: Templates may cause additional data to be sent externally
insights.problem: Template content that entices more file/context inclusion or instructs broader indexing could increase the amount of repository content sent to external services.
insights.root_cause: Prompt instructions and system-info partials influence what the agent includes in semantic indexing or provider calls.
insights.solution: Ensure edits do not unintentionally broaden data included in prompts; verify alignment with workspace/server URL defaults and any privacy constraints documented in AGENTS.md and configuration.

key_files.why: Source template for converting natural-language intents into shell commands — directly influences what commands agents propose and what execution guards must handle.
Generates conventional commit messages from diffs; changes affect repository metadata and any tooling that relies on commit formatting.
Scaffold for custom agent behavior and integrations; often composed with partials to create full system prompts used by agent instances.
Contains JSON-tagged tool-call examples that serve as canonical shapes for tool invocation and must match MCP/skill schemas.
Used when tools fail to guide LLM reflection and retry logic; affects error handling flows and retry behavior.

construct_relationships: Partial templates (forge-partial-*.md) are composed by compound templates (forge-custom-agent-template.md) to build full system prompts.
forge-tool-use-example.md provides JSON examples that the prompt parser and tool invocation layer (MCP/skills runtime) rely on to validate and execute tool calls.
forge-command-generator-prompt.md output is consumed by safe execution codepaths in the FS/shell helpers, so template output must conform to those execution guards.
forge-commit-message-prompt.md is fed by diffs supplied by VCS helpers in the repo persistence layer; commit text formatting expectations may intersect with git hooks and downstream CI.
notable_relationships: Changes to templates impact code in crates/forge_main (agent orchestration and prompt composition) and crates/forge_repo (conversations persisted with prompt-derived content).
Tool example JSON must remain consistent with .mcp.json and SKILL.md in the repository; divergence can break provider/tool client integrations in crates/forge_services.

Template-driven behavior influences semantic-indexing and provider calls (crates/forge_embed, forge_services) which may send data to external workspace servers; privacy/configuration settings in forge_config apply.
Snapshot and integration tests (including insta tests) reference stable prompt outputs; CI expectations may fail if templates change unexpectedly.
recommendations: Preserve placeholder tokens, tag markup, and JSON example syntax exactly when editing; validate JSON snippets with a JSON linter.
When modifying examples that represent tool calls, cross-check against MCP/skill descriptors (.mcp.json, SKILL.md) and runtime validators to avoid schema mismatches.
Run the project's test suite and snapshot/integration tests after edits (including any insta snapshot acceptance workflow) to detect behavioral regressions.
Verify end-to-end flows that use modified templates: command generation → dry-run/execution guards, commit message generation → git commit flow, and tool-failure flows → retry/reflection logic.
Avoid introducing wording that broadens data collection or encourages inclusion of sensitive files; confirm alignment with privacy defaults and workspace server configuration.

benchmarks.description: This folder contains a small TypeScript-based benchmark/evaluation runner and a collection of concrete evaluation scenarios (semantic-search quality) used to exercise and validate parts of the project. It provides a CLI that loads a task.yml, expands tabular inputs into per-run contexts, executes shell commands with controlled parallelism/timeouts and validations, streams and logs outputs, and emits summary results for automated verification and integration with CI or local checks.
key_behaviors: Load and normalize a task specification (task.yml) and associated contexts (CSV, inline, or command-generated) into a series of concrete run contexts.
Render Handlebars templates for command generation and produce a Cartesian product of input rows to form many permutations to execute.
Execute each run in a per-task temporary workspace: run optional before_run setup, run multiple sequential commands per context, enforce per-command timeouts, and allow early-exit validations.
Coordinate parallelism across multiple runs with a concurrency limiter while maintaining sequential execution of commands within a run.
Stream stdout/stderr to both in-memory buffers and persistent per-run log files (stripping ANSI for logs while preserving original output for validation).
Provide evaluation-specific helpers and scripts that optionally invoke external LLM providers (Vertex/Gemini) and shell wrappers to validate higher-level behavioral metrics.

insights.title: Per-run isolation with parallel orchestration
insights.problem: Multiple contexts run in parallel but share the repository environment unless isolated; temporary workspace and log artifacts can race or leak.
insights.root_cause: The runner creates per-task temporary directories and uses a concurrency limiter (p-limit) but otherwise runs shell processes that may read/write shared paths if templates or commands reference repo-relative locations.
insights.solution: When running or modifying orchestration, preserve per-run workspace creation and unique log paths, and be aware that commands referencing repo paths may break isolation or introduce flakiness; ensure cleanup and unique naming are respected.

insights.title: Dual-output stream: stripped logs vs original output
insights.problem: Log files are recorded with ANSI sequences stripped while returned in-memory output retains ANSI; validators or downstream consumers may require one representation or the other.
insights.root_cause: Task executor intentionally writes a sanitized log (no ANSI) but preserves the original captured output in the result object for validation.
insights.solution: Any change that reads logs or validates output must use the correct representation (stripped log file vs raw in-memory capture) and must account for possible differences when matching text, timestamps, or regexes.

insights.title: Early-exit validations can terminate processes mid-stream
insights.problem: Processes can be killed when validations pass early; this may leave partial output, buffered data, or unflushed log state, which affects reproducibility and reporting.
insights.root_cause: The executor runs incremental validations during process output and sends SIGKILL when all validations pass before natural process exit.
insights.solution: Respect the semantics that early-exit is possible: callers and any reporting logic must handle abrupt terminations, ensure stream flush/completion is awaited, and treat exit reason (killed vs natural exit) as part of the TaskResult.

insights.title: Strict template rendering and type/schema expectations
insights.problem: Handlebars is used in strict mode and task definitions are typed; malformed CSV rows, missing placeholders, or mismatched task.yml shape can cause runtime failures.
insights.root_cause: Command generation relies on deterministic CSV parsing and strict template rendering, while task file input is external YAML that may not be type-checked at runtime.
insights.solution: Any code touching command generation or task parsing must preserve strict rendering semantics and perform validation of parsed contexts against expectations (task shape, required keys) before executing commands.

insights.title: Evaluations depend on external services and auth
insights.problem: Some evaluation scripts (LLM judge) only run when cloud credentials are present; behavior and exit codes vary depending on environment authentication.
insights.root_cause: Evaluation scripts call external LLM providers (Vertex/Gemini) or other cloud services and include shell wrappers that detect auth to optionally run or skip steps.
insights.solution: When modifying evaluation scripts or runner wiring, preserve explicit checks for cloud auth and ensure skip/exit semantics remain clear; tests should cover both authenticated and unauthenticated runs.

key_files.why: Top-level CLI driver that loads task.yml, expands contexts, orchestrates parallel execution, creates per-task temporary workdirs, and emits summary logs — the primary entrypoint for running evaluations.
Responsible for running each shell command with streaming logs, enforcing timeouts, performing incremental validations for early exit, and returning structured TaskExecutionResult used by reporting.
Produces contexts from CSV or inline sources and renders strict Handlebars templates to generate the concrete shell commands that the executor runs — core to input expansion logic.
Defines the canonical Task, Source, Validation, and TaskStatus types referenced across the runner and evaluation scripts; authoritative shape for task.yml and result objects.
Centralizes CLI argument parsing and path resolution logic (eval directory vs direct task file) used by the CLI to locate and normalize the task specification to run.

construct_relationships: cli.ts uses parseCliArgs (parse.ts) to resolve the task file and eval directory before loading the Task model defined in model.ts.
cli.ts calls getContextsFromSources / generateCommands (command-generator.ts) to expand sources into a Cartesian set of contexts and Handlebars-rendered commands for each run.

For each generated command, cli.ts schedules executeTask (task-executor.ts) under a concurrency limiter; task-executor streams stdout/stderr to per-run log files and returns TaskExecutionResult objects the CLI aggregates into TaskResult summaries.
Utilities in utils.ts are used by the CLI and task-executor for timestamp formatting, temp dir creation, and CSV parsing that feed into command generation and log naming.

Evaluation scripts under benchmarks/evals (including llm_judge.ts and the shell harnesses) are invoked as part of configured tasks (task.run or before_run) and rely on the same CLI/workflow primitives when called from the task runner.
notable_relationships: Node toolchain and TS runtime: the folder assumes a working Node/tsx environment and uses TypeScript (tsconfig.json); changes should remain compatible with the project's npm scripts (npm run eval) that invoke these scripts.

External service dependencies: LLM-based judges depend on cloud provider credentials (Vertex/GCP) and will change behavior (skip vs run) depending on environment auth; CI or local runs must account for this variability.

Filesystem and repo context: generated commands may reference repository files or paths; the runner provides per-run temporary directories but commands can still access repo-relative locations, affecting isolation and reproducibility.

CI/integration usage: the benchmarks are used by repository tooling (docs/validation or manual verification) and may be invoked as part of test/benchmark flows; maintain CLI argument semantics and exit codes expected by shell harnesses (run_eval.sh, run_tests.sh).
Type/schema coupling: model.ts types must align with task.yml files under benchmarks/evals; mismatches can produce runtime failures when the CLI parses YAML and generates contexts.
recommendations: Preserve CLI semantics: maintain parseCliArgs behavior (evalName vs direct task file) and the exit codes expected by existing shell harnesses (run_eval.sh / run_tests.sh).
Respect per-run isolation and cleanup: ensure unique temp/workdir creation and that log file paths remain stable; verify cleanup callbacks are invoked where expected to avoid artifact leaks.

When touching execution logic, account for early-exit semantics and process lifecycle: wait for streams to flush, capture and record kill vs natural-exit reasons, and ensure logs reflect final process state.

Keep template rendering strictness and validate contexts before execution: any change to command generation must preserve strict Handlebars behavior and verify CSV/inline inputs satisfy required placeholders.

Test both authenticated and unauthenticated evaluation flows: run the evaluation harnesses (run_tests.sh / run_eval.sh) with and without cloud credentials to confirm conditional LLM-invocation behavior and exit code contracts.

Be mindful of log representation differences: consumers may use stripped log files or raw captured output; maintain both outputs and ensure validators reference the intended representation when matching text/regexes.

shell-plugin.description: The shell-plugin folder implements the Forge Zsh integration: it provides the plugin entrypoint and managed setup snippet, runtime configuration, ZLE widgets & keybindings, fzf-driven interactive actions (providers, models, conversations, commits), RPROMPT theme integration, diagnostics, and helper utilities that consistently invoke the Forge CLI from the shell. This subtree matters because it mediates every interactive terminal experience with Forge (prompt display, :commands, provider/login flows, and background sync/update), and it must preserve shell safety, TTY semantics, and cross-platform UX constraints.
key_behaviors: Plugin composition at shell startup: forge.plugin.zsh sources modular lib/* files and the managed forge.setup.zsh block ensures the plugin/theme are loaded and required plugins are present.

ZLE-driven interactions: bindings.zsh registers widgets (accept-line, completion, bracketed-paste) and dispatcher.zsh parses colon-prefixed commands and delegates to _forge_action_* handlers in lib/actions/*.zsh.

Interactive pickers & CLI invocation: lib/actions use fzf-based pickers and call the Forge CLI; helpers.zsh centralizes CLI invocation variants (interactive with TTY, non-interactive, background) to avoid blocking the shell and to preserve API-key prompts.

Right prompt rendering and session state: forge.theme.zsh calls the Forge binary to populate RPROMPT with per-session agent/model/token info; the plugin exports and uses session-scoped overrides (FORGE_CONVERSATION_ID, FORGE_ACTIVE_AGENT) to reflect active state.

Diagnostics and platform guidance: doctor.zsh performs environment checks (dependencies, terminal, fonts) and keyboard.zsh documents ZLE shortcuts; actions/doctor.zsh bridges the plugin to the binary's doctor command.

Background tasks and file/system integration: plugin starts background sync/update checks and integrates with system clipboard, fd/bat/fzf tooling and external editor workflows for composing commands.

insights.title: Interactive commands must be attached to a TTY
insights.problem: Commands invoked from ZLE widgets or non-login shells can run without an attached TTY, causing interactive flows (API-key prompts, fzf, rustyline) to fail with EOF or behave incorrectly.
insights.root_cause: Child processes launched from ZLE widgets inherit non-interactive stdin/stderr unless explicitly attached to /dev/tty or invoked with an interactive wrapper.
insights.solution: Use a dedicated interactive exec helper (e.g., _forge_exec_interactive) that attaches child commands to /dev/tty; maintain this helper as the single place to run interactive CLI operations.

insights.title: Avoid polluting the global shell environment
insights.problem: Global exports for session state (FORGE_CONVERSATION_ID, FORGE_ACTIVE_AGENT) and other variables can conflict with user shells or other plugins and produce brittle cross-session behavior.
insights.root_cause: Earlier code relied on normal variable exports and globals without typeset -g/local scoping, making state persist across unintended scopes and sessions.
insights.solution: Scope plugin configuration and session-overrides with typeset to limit global leakage; keep a stable, documented set of exported env vars and preserve backward-compatible names/aliases.

insights.title: Command alias and routing inconsistencies
insights.problem: Dispatch and action naming were inconsistent (e.g., 'login' vs 'provider-login'), and dispatcher missed routing for recently added actions (copy, background update), causing confusing UX and unhandled commands.
insights.root_cause: Incremental additions of actions and aliases without a single authoritative command registry led to divergence between dispatcher routing and action handler names.
insights.solution: Keep dispatcher mappings and alias lists aligned with action handler names; preserve legacy aliases where users/scripts may rely on them and ensure dispatcher forwards unknown aliases appropriately.

insights.title: RPROMPT and prompt substitution can be local to lazy-loaded functions
insights.problem: When the theme is lazy-loaded (sourced inside a function), setting RPROMPT without global declaration can create prompt values that are local to the function scope or not honored by prompt expansion.
insights.root_cause: Zsh scoping rules cause assignments inside functions to be local unless typeset -g or global assignment is used; prompt subst options may be unset in some lazy-load contexts.
insights.solution: Ensure theme code sets RPROMPT in a global scope (typeset -g where appropriate) and verifies PROMPT_SUBST and prompt option state when rendering or appending prompt strings.

insights.title: Doctor script severity and guidance drift
insights.problem: Some doctor checks mark informational issues as failures (e.g., missing Oh My Zsh, fonts) and the remediation hints include outdated installation commands (npm i -g forgecode@latest).
insights.root_cause: Diagnostic heuristics and installation guidance were not updated after product/tooling changes and overzealously treat optional items as blocking failures.
insights.solution: Classify checks into fail/warn/info appropriately and keep installation hints aligned with current packaging/distribution workflow; ensure doctor output is safe for non-privileged environments.

insights.title: FZF preview/layout impacts selection usability
insights.problem: Default fzf preview placement and full-height layouts occluded the prompt or made selection awkward in many terminals.
insights.root_cause: A single fzf layout configuration was used across diverse terminal sizes/platforms without optimizing for reverse order and preview at the bottom.
insights.solution: Adopt a consistent layout (reverse with preview bottom) in the unified fzf wrapper and centralize fzf options so all pickers benefit from the same UX settings.

key_files.why: Top-level plugin entrypoint that composes and sources all modular plugin components; edits here change what is loaded at shell startup.
Central command parser and ZLE accept-line widget; it routes colon-prefixed commands to action handlers and resets prompt state after actions.
Provides the core CLI invocation helpers (interactive/non-interactive), fzf wrapper, logging, and background sync starter used widely by action handlers.
Responsible for RPROMPT rendering and exporting per-session flags; changes affect prompt display, lazy-load behavior, and session visibility in the right prompt.
Diagnostic tool used by users to debug installation and environment issues; its messages and severity classification materially affect support workflows.

construct_relationships: forge.plugin.zsh sources lib/config.zsh, lib/dispatcher.zsh, lib/bindings.zsh and thereby composes the plugin at startup.
dispatcher.zsh parses colon-prefixed buffer content and invokes specific _forge_action_* handlers implemented in shell-plugin/lib/actions/*.zsh.
helpers.zsh exposes _forge_exec and _forge_exec_interactive which lib/actions/*.zsh call to run the Forge CLI (interactive flows must use _forge_exec_interactive).
bindings.zsh registers ZLE widgets (forge-accept-line, forge-completion, bracketed-paste) that trigger dispatcher logic and completion handlers in lib/completion.zsh.
forge.theme.zsh calls the Forge CLI to build RPROMPT content and relies on session-scoped environment variables (FORGE_ACTIVE_AGENT, FORGE_CONVERSATION_ID) maintained by dispatcher/actions.
doctor.zsh is invoked via lib/actions/doctor.zsh to surface installation diagnostics from the plugin to the user.
notable_relationships: All interactive actions ultimately invoke the external Forge binary; changes here can affect CLI invocation patterns, TTY requirements, and provider login UX (API-key prompts).

The plugin depends on external tooling (fzf, fd, bat, clipboard utilities, editor) and platform-specific behavior (macOS vs Linux clipboard, meta/Alt key handling); missing tools must be handled gracefully.
forge.setup.zsh modifies users' .zshrc (machine-managed block) and the plugin must preserve marker format and idempotence to avoid corrupting startup files.
Prompt and ZLE behavior interacts with user frameworks (Oh My Zsh, Prezto, custom themes); load order and plugin ordering matter for highlighting, keybindings, and prompt substitution.
Background sync/update checks and workspace sync actions interact with networked services (workspace server, update endpoints) and can surface network errors in the interactive shell.
recommendations: Preserve and respect typeset scoping for plugin configuration and session overrides to avoid exporting unexpected globals; do not remove existing public env var names without preserving aliases.
When invoking interactive CLI flows from actions, use the centralized interactive exec helper that attaches to /dev/tty; verify flows that prompt for API keys or use fzf still receive real TTY input.
Keep dispatcher routing and aliases backward-compatible: validate that existing aliases (e.g., login) and newly-introduced aliases are all correctly mapped to action handlers.

Test changes interactively on macOS and Linux shells, covering: ZLE accept-line behavior, bracketed paste, fzf pickers (provider/model/conversation), provider login/logout, copy-to-clipboard, and RPROMPT rendering in both eager and lazy-loaded contexts.

When modifying the doctor script or messaging, ensure severity levels are appropriate for optional dependencies and that installation hints match current distribution/installation methods (avoid stale npm hints).
Do not alter the managed forge.setup.zsh block marker format or its machine-managed semantics; edits that change injection format must preserve idempotence and not break existing .zshrc management.

.forge.description: The .forge directory bundles repository skills: small, opinionated CLI scripts and human-facing skill descriptions (SKILL.md) that automate developer workflows such as plan creation/validation, GitHub integration, conflict resolution, release-note generation, and smoke tests. These artifacts matter because they codify repository conventions, gate quality via validators used by humans and CI, and invoke external tools/credentials that create upstream/downstream side effects.
key_behaviors: Authoring and validating project plans: templates, per-plan linting, and bulk validation summarization.
Interacting with GitHub: creating issues via gh CLI, fetching release metadata/PR details, and extracting active PR comments.
Pre-merge and maintenance helpers: detecting unresolved merge conflicts and locating FIXME/TODO markers.
CLI smoke tests and provider reasoning tests: running the forge CLI (or another BINARY) through scripted scenarios and asserting structured outputs.
Release notes enforcement: assembling metadata and enforcing length/format constraints before publishing.

insights.title: Scripts assume specific external tooling and environment variables
insights.problem: Many scripts call external CLIs (gh, jq, git) and rely on environment variables (BINARY, VERSION, REPO, MAX_CHARS) without in-repo fallbacks.
insights.root_cause: These are small POSIX/Bash scripts intended to run interactively or in CI where these tools/vars are preinstalled/configured.
insights.solution: When running or editing scripts, ensure the target environment provides the required executables, API tokens, and environment variables; treat absence of these as a usage/CI configuration issue rather than a script-only problem.

insights.title: Exit codes and textual output are used as gates
insights.problem: Scripts emit colored output, warnings, and exit codes to indicate pass/fail; CI or callers likely rely on these signals.
insights.root_cause: Validators and test scripts are intended to be consumed by humans and automated runners that check exit status and parse summaries.
insights.solution: Preserve existing exit code semantics and summary output formats when modifying scripts; callers (CI or other scripts) depend on them to accept/reject changes.

insights.title: Scripts expect specific repo structure and file naming
insights.problem: Plan validators assume plans live in a plans directory with filename/date patterns and specific markdown section headings; other scripts expect .forge/skills paths or git working-tree layout.
